Given a research prompt and a dataset, EDM-ARS produces a complete LaTeX manuscript with real Semantic Scholar citations, validated machine learning analyses, and automated methodological peer review. We also provide a detailed description of the system architecture, the three-tier data registry design that encodes educational domain expertise, the specification of each agent, the inter-agent communication protocol, and mechanisms for error-handling and self-correction. Finally, we discuss current limitations, including single-dataset scope and formulaic paper output, and outline a phased roadmap toward causal inference, transfer learning, psychometric, and multi-dataset generalization. EDM-ARS is released as an open-source project to support the educational research community.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EDM-ARS, a multi-agent LLM-based system that automates the full pipeline of educational data mining research, currently focused on predictive modeling and capable of generating full, cited LaTeX manuscripts from a prompt and dataset.
AI.motivation: Educational data mining studies often require substantial expert effort across many stages: problem formulation, data engineering, modeling, analysis, writing, and peer review. Existing automated ML or agent frameworks are not tailored to educational research needs (e.g., domain-specific constructs, outcome variables, and methodological norms). The authors aim to reduce the labor and expertise barrier, standardize workflows, and explore how domain-aware LLM agents can automate end-to-end EDM research, making such research more scalable and accessible to the education community.
AI.method: The authors design EDM-ARS, a domain-specific multi-agent pipeline coordinated by a state machine. Five specialized LLM-powered agents (ProblemFormulator, DataEngineer, Analyst, Critic, Writer) collaborate via a defined communication protocol. The system uses a three-tier data registry that encodes educational domain knowledge, supports sandboxed code execution for data and modeling steps, employs checkpoints and revision loops for robustness, and integrates real Semantic Scholar citations. Given a textual research prompt and a dataset, the pipeline automatically performs problem formulation, feature engineering, predictive modeling, evaluation, critique, and manuscript drafting in LaTeX format, including an automated methodological peer-review layer.
AI.result: The system can, from a prompt and a single dataset, autonomously generate complete LaTeX research manuscripts that include validated machine learning analyses and realistic scholarly citations. The authors also deliver an open-source implementation, a full description of the architecture (agents, coordinator, data registry, communication, error-handling, self-correction), and qualitative illustrations of EDM-ARS’s capabilities and limitations in practice. The current system is constrained to single-dataset predictive modeling tasks and tends to produce somewhat formulaic paper structures.
AI.conclusion: EDM-ARS demonstrates that domain-aware, multi-agent LLM pipelines can automate much of the EDM research lifecycle, from problem formulation to manuscript generation and critique. While the present instantiation is limited to predictive modeling on single datasets and produces relatively templated outputs, it provides a concrete, extensible framework and open-source tooling. The authors propose a roadmap toward richer educational analyses, including causal inference, transfer and multi-dataset learning, and psychometric applications, suggesting a path to more sophisticated, generalizable automated research systems for education.

title: Modeling the human lexicon under temperature variations: linguistic factors, diversity and typicality in LLM word associations
comment: 11 pages, 12 figures, to appear in LREC 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve impressive results in terms of fluency in text generation, yet the nature of their linguistic knowledge - in particular the human-likeness of their internal lexicon - remains uncertain. This study compares human and LLM-generated word associations to evaluate how accurately models capture human lexical patterns. Using English cue-response pairs from the SWOW dataset and newly generated associations from three LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen-2.5-32B) across multiple temperature settings, we examine (i) the influence of lexical factors such as word frequency and concreteness on cue-response pairs, and (ii) the variability and typicality of LLM responses compared to human responses. Results show that all models mirror human trends for frequency and concreteness but differ in response variability and typicality. Larger models such as Qwen tend to emulate a single "prototypical" human participant, generating highly typical but minimally variable responses, while smaller models such as Mistral and Llama produce more variable yet less typical responses. Temperature settings further influence this trade-off, with higher values increasing variability but decreasing typicality. These findings highlight both the similarities and differences between human and LLM lexicons, emphasizing the need to account for model size and temperature when probing LLM lexical representations.

AI.tldr: The paper compares word associations produced by humans and several LLMs to assess how human-like the models’ internal lexicons are.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are fluent text generators, it is unclear whether their underlying lexical representations resemble human mental lexicons. Word association norms are a classic psycholinguistic tool to probe human lexical organization, so comparing them with LLM-produced associations can reveal how well models capture human lexical patterns and where they differ.
AI.method: The authors use English cue–response pairs from the SWOW word association dataset as a human baseline. For the same cue words, they elicit associations from three LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-32B) under multiple temperature settings. They then analyze: (i) how lexical variables like word frequency and concreteness affect cue–response patterns in humans vs. models; and (ii) how variable and how typical (i.e., close to human norms) the LLM responses are compared with human responses.
AI.result: All LLMs reproduce human-like trends with respect to frequency and concreteness effects on associations. However, they diverge in variability and typicality: the larger Qwen model tends to give very typical human-like responses but with little diversity, resembling a “prototypical” human respondent, while smaller models (Mistral, Llama) generate more diverse but less typical associations. Higher sampling temperatures increase variability but reduce typicality across models.
AI.conclusion: LLMs share some structural properties of the human mental lexicon but differ systematically in how they distribute responses across alternatives. Model size and generation temperature strongly shape the balance between typicality and variability, so these factors must be carefully controlled and interpreted when using LLMs to study or emulate human lexical representations.

title: CORE: Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection via Confidence and Orthogonal Residual Scoring
comment: 26 pages, 5 figures, includes supplementary material as appendix
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential for deploying deep learning models reliably, yet no single method performs consistently across architectures and datasets -- a scorer that leads on one benchmark often falters on another. We attribute this inconsistency to a shared structural limitation: logit-based methods see only the classifier's confidence signal, while feature-based methods attempt to measure membership in the training distribution but do so in the full feature space where confidence and membership are entangled, inheriting architecture-sensitive failure modes. We observe that penultimate features naturally decompose into two orthogonal subspaces: a classifier-aligned component encoding confidence, and a residual the classifier discards. We discover that this residual carries a class-specific directional signature for in-distribution data -- a membership signal invisible to logit-based methods and entangled with noise in feature-based methods. We propose CORE (COnfidence + REsidual), which disentangles the two signals by scoring each subspace independently and combines them via normalized summation. Because the two signals are orthogonal by construction, their failure modes are approximately independent, producing robust detection where either view alone is unreliable. CORE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance across five architectures and five benchmark configurations, ranking first in three of five settings and achieving the highest grand average AUROC with negligible computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CORE, an OOD detection method that combines classifier confidence with an orthogonal residual feature component to achieve more robust performance across architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing OOD detection methods are inconsistent across architectures and benchmarks: logit-based scorers only leverage classifier confidence, and feature-based methods try to measure training-distribution membership in the full feature space, where confidence and membership are entangled. This structural limitation leads to architecture-sensitive failure modes and a lack of robustness.
AI.method: The authors analyze penultimate-layer features and show they decompose into two orthogonal subspaces: a classifier-aligned component (capturing confidence) and a residual component discarded by the classifier. They find that this residual encodes class-specific directional signatures for in-distribution data. Based on this, they propose CORE (COnfidence + REsidual), which independently scores OOD likelihood from the confidence-aligned subspace and from the residual subspace, then combines the scores via a normalized sum, exploiting the approximate independence of their failure modes.
AI.result: CORE delivers competitive or state-of-the-art OOD detection across five neural architectures and five benchmark settings, ranking first in three of the five configurations and achieving the best overall (grand average) AUROC, all with negligible additional computational cost compared to standard baselines.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly disentangling classifier confidence from a complementary residual membership signal in orthogonal feature subspaces, CORE mitigates architecture-sensitive failure modes and yields more robust OOD detection performance than methods relying on either signal alone.

title: GRAFITE: Generative Regression Analysis Framework for Issue Tracking and Evaluation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are largely motivated by their performance on popular topics and benchmarks at the time of their release. However, over time, contamination occurs due to significant exposure of benchmark data during training. This poses a risk of model performance inflation if testing is not carefully executed. To address this challenge, we present GRAFITE, a continuous LLM evaluation platform through a comprehensive system for maintaining and evaluating model issues. Our approach enables building a repository of model problems based on user feedback over time and offers a pipeline for assessing LLMs against these issues through quality assurance (QA) tests using LLM-as-a-judge. The platform enables side-by-side comparison of multiple models, facilitating regression detection across different releases. The platform is available at https://github.com/IBM/grafite. The demo video is available at www.youtube.com/watch?v=XFZyoleN56k.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GRAFITE, a continuous evaluation platform that builds and reuses real-world model issues to reliably test LLMs over time and detect regressions.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluations often rely on static benchmarks that become contaminated as their data is incorporated into training corpora, inflating performance and making it difficult to measure true progress or detect regressions. There is a need for an evaluation system that stays robust over time, reflects real user problems, and allows consistent comparison of model versions.
AI.method: The authors design GRAFITE, a platform that: (1) collects and curates model issues from user feedback into a growing repository; (2) turns these issues into structured QA-style tests; (3) uses LLM-as-a-judge to automatically score model responses to these tests; and (4) supports side‑by‑side evaluation of multiple models and versions to monitor performance and detect regressions. The system is implemented as an open-source toolkit and demoed via a web interface and GitHub repository.
AI.result: GRAFITE provides a working, open-source continuous evaluation pipeline where collected issues can be reused as tests, models can be automatically scored with LLM judges, and different LLMs can be compared on the same real-world issue sets. It enables practical regression detection between model releases and illustrates that user-driven, evolving test suites are feasible for LLM QA.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark contamination undermines traditional static LLM evaluations, so continuous, user-driven evaluation infrastructures are needed. GRAFITE demonstrates such an infrastructure by combining issue collection, test generation, LLM-as-a-judge scoring, and comparative dashboards to support ongoing, realistic performance monitoring and regression detection for LLMs.

title: The Validity Gap in Health AI Evaluation: A Cross-Sectional Analysis of Benchmark Composition
summary:
Background: Clinical trials rely on transparent inclusion criteria to ensure generalizability. In contrast, benchmarks validating health-related large language models (LLMs) rarely characterize the "patient" or "query" populations they contain. Without defined composition, aggregate performance metrics may misrepresent model readiness for clinical use.
  Methods: We analyzed 18,707 consumer health queries across six public benchmarks using LLMs as automated coding instruments to apply a standardized 16-field taxonomy profiling context, topic, and intent.
  Results: We identified a structural "validity gap." While benchmarks have evolved from static retrieval to interactive dialogue, clinical composition remains misaligned with real-world needs. Although 42% of the corpus referenced objective data, this was polarized toward wellness-focused wearable signals (17.7%); complex diagnostic inputs remained rare, including laboratory values (5.2%), imaging (3.8%), and raw medical records (0.6%). Safety-critical scenarios were effectively absent: suicide/self-harm queries comprised <0.7% of the corpus and chronic disease management only 5.5%. Benchmarks also neglected vulnerable populations (pediatrics/older adults <11%) and global health needs.
  Conclusions: Evaluation benchmarks remain misaligned with real-world clinical needs, lacking raw clinical artifacts, adequate representation of vulnerable populations, and longitudinal chronic care scenarios. The field must adopt standardized query profiling--analogous to clinical trial reporting--to align evaluation with the full complexity of clinical practice.

AI.tldr: The paper audits popular health-related LLM benchmarks and finds that their query populations are poorly characterized and misaligned with real-world clinical needs, especially for complex, safety-critical, and vulnerable-patient scenarios.
AI.motivation: Health-related LLMs are increasingly tested on public benchmarks to judge their readiness for clinical use, but unlike clinical trials, these benchmarks rarely define who or what their “patients” and “queries” represent. Without knowing the composition of the evaluated scenarios, headline accuracy or safety scores may be misleading: models might perform well on easy, low-risk questions while being untested on high-stakes, complex, or underserved populations. The authors aim to systematically characterize what kinds of consumer health queries are actually present in these benchmarks and to reveal structural gaps between benchmark content and real-world information needs.
AI.method: The authors collected 18,707 consumer health queries from six public evaluation benchmarks used for assessing health-related LLMs. They designed a standardized 16-field taxonomy capturing aspects of each query’s context (e.g., demographics, care setting), clinical topic (e.g., wellness vs. diagnosis, presence of objective data like labs or imaging), and user intent (e.g., information-seeking, triage, chronic disease management). They then used large language models themselves as automated coders to label each query according to this taxonomy, enabling large-scale profiling of the benchmarks’ clinical composition and comparison with real-world needs described in the literature.
AI.result: The analysis uncovered a “validity gap” between what benchmarks contain and what real-world clinical practice requires. Although benchmarks have progressed from simple information retrieval tasks to interactive dialogues, their clinical content remains skewed. A sizable portion (42%) of queries referenced some form of objective data, but this was disproportionately concentrated in wellness-oriented wearable data (17.7%). In contrast, truly complex clinical artifacts rarely appeared: laboratory values in only 5.2% of queries, imaging in 3.8%, and raw medical records in just 0.6%. High-stakes or safety-critical contexts were largely absent—for example, suicide or self-harm comprised less than 0.7% of queries, and chronic disease management only 5.5%. Vulnerable populations were underrepresented (pediatric and older adult queries together under 11%), and global health or non–high-income settings were similarly neglected. Overall, current benchmarks offer a narrow, non-representative slice of clinical scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current evaluation benchmarks for health-related LLMs are not well aligned with the complexity, risk profile, and population diversity of real-world clinical care. They underrepresent raw clinical data sources, safety-critical and longitudinal chronic-care situations, and vulnerable demographic groups. As a result, aggregate benchmark scores can give a false impression of model readiness for deployment in healthcare. The authors argue that the community should adopt standardized, trial-like reporting and query profiling practices—such as their 16-field taxonomy—so that benchmark creators clearly specify the composition of their datasets. This would enable more meaningful evaluation, expose coverage gaps, and drive the design of benchmarks that better reflect actual clinical practice and global health needs.

title: CWoMP: Morpheme Representation Learning for Interlinear Glossing
summary: Interlinear glossed text (IGT) is a standard notation for language documentation which is linguistically rich but laborious to produce manually. Recent automated IGT methods treat glosses as character sequences, neglecting their compositional structure. We propose CWoMP (Contrastive Word-Morpheme Pretraining), which instead treats morphemes as atomic form-meaning units with learned representations. A contrastively trained encoder aligns words-in-context with their constituent morphemes in a shared embedding space; an autoregressive decoder then generates the morpheme sequence by retrieving entries from a mutable lexicon of these embeddings. Predictions are interpretable--grounded in lexicon entries--and users can improve results at inference time by expanding the lexicon without retraining. We evaluate on diverse low-resource languages, showing that CWoMP outperforms existing methods while being significantly more efficient, with particularly strong gains in extremely low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: They introduce CWoMP, a morpheme-based contrastive pretraining approach that makes interlinear glossed text generation more accurate, efficient, interpretable, and adaptable for low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: Manual creation of interlinear glossed text is time-consuming, and existing automated systems treat glosses as flat character sequences, ignoring the compositional morpheme structure that linguists care about and that could improve performance, especially for low-resource languages.
AI.method: They design CWoMP, where morphemes are treated as atomic form-meaning units with learned embeddings. A contrastively trained encoder maps words-in-context and their constituent morphemes into a shared embedding space, encouraging alignment between them. An autoregressive decoder then predicts glosses by retrieving morpheme embeddings from a mutable lexicon (rather than generating raw characters), allowing user-editable lexicon entries at inference time.
AI.result: On several typologically and resource-diverse low-resource languages, CWoMP achieves better accuracy than prior automated IGT methods and is more computationally efficient, with especially large improvements when training data is extremely scarce.
AI.conclusion: Modeling morphemes as atomic units via contrastive word-morpheme pretraining yields interpretable, lexicon-grounded IGT generation that outperforms character-based baselines, is more efficient, and allows users to dynamically improve performance by updating the lexicon without retraining.

title: Consumer-to-Clinical Language Shifts in Ambient AI Draft Notes and Clinician-Finalized Documentation: A Multi-level Analysis
summary: Ambient AI generates draft clinical notes from patient-clinician conversations, often using lay or consumer-oriented phrasing to support patient understanding instead of standardized clinical terminology. How clinicians revise these drafts for professional documentation conventions remains unclear. We quantified clinician editing for consumer-to- clinical normalization using a dictionary-confirmed transformation framework. We analyzed 71,173 AI-draft and finalized-note section pairs from 34,726 encounters. Confirmed transformations were defined as replacing a consumer expression with its dictionary-mapped clinical equivalent in the same section. Editing significantly reduced terminology density across all sections (p < 0.001). The Assessment and Plan accounted for the largest transformation volume (59.3%). Our analysis identified 7,576 transformation events across 4,114 note sections (5.8%), representing 1.2% consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity varied across individual clinicians (p < 0.001). Overall, clinician post-editing demonstrates consistent shifts from conversational phrasing toward standardized, section- appropriate clinical terminology, supporting section-aware ambient AI design.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how clinicians edit ambient AI-generated draft clinical notes, focusing on how lay terms are transformed into standardized clinical terminology, and quantifies these consumer-to-clinical edits across a large dataset of encounters.
AI.motivation: Ambient AI systems are increasingly used to generate draft clinical documentation from patient-clinician conversations, and they often use consumer-friendly language. However, professional medical records require standardized clinical terminology and section-specific conventions. Little is known about how clinicians actually revise these AI drafts in practice, and quantifying this process can inform better AI design and alignment with documentation standards.
AI.method: The authors collected 71,173 pairs of AI-generated draft note sections and their corresponding finalized clinician-edited sections from 34,726 encounters. They built a dictionary-based mapping between consumer-oriented expressions and their clinical equivalents, and defined a 'confirmed transformation' when a consumer term in the draft was replaced by its mapped clinical equivalent in the same section. They then quantified transformation counts, locations (by note section), intensities, and variation across clinicians, and examined how editing affected terminology density.
AI.result: Editing significantly reduced terminology density across all note sections (p < 0.001). The Assessment and Plan sections showed the highest volume of consumer-to-clinical transformations, accounting for 59.3% of all transformations. Across the dataset, they identified 7,576 confirmed transformation events in 4,114 note sections (5.8% of sections), corresponding to 1.2% of consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity showed significant variation between clinicians (p < 0.001). Overall, clinicians systematically shifted language from conversational to standardized clinical terminology when revising AI drafts.
AI.conclusion: Clinicians consistently normalize consumer-friendly, conversational phrasing in ambient AI-generated notes into standardized, section-appropriate clinical terminology, particularly in Assessment and Plan sections, and the extent of this normalization varies by clinician. These findings support the need for section-aware, terminology-sensitive design of ambient AI systems so that generated drafts better align with documentation norms and reduce clinician editing burden.

title: How Psychological Learning Paradigms Shaped and Constrained Artificial Intelligence
summary: The dominant paradigms of artificial intelligence were shaped by learning theories from psychology: behaviorism inspired reinforcement learning, cognitivism gave rise to deep learning and memory-augmented architectures, and constructivism influenced curriculum learning and compositional approaches. This paper argues that each AI paradigm inherited not only the strengths but the structural limitations of the psychological theory that inspired it. Reinforcement learning cannot account for the internal structure of knowledge, deep learning compresses representations into opaque parameter spaces resistant to principled update, and current integrative approaches lack a formal account of how new understanding is constructed from existing components. The paper further examines a cross-cultural divergence in the interpretation of rote learning, arguing that the Eastern conception of memorization as a structured, multi-phase precursor to understanding offers an underexploited bridge between psychological theory and AI methodology. Drawing on the systematicity debate and critique of Aizawa of both classicism and connectionism, this paper introduces ReSynth, a trimodular framework that separates reasoning (Intellect), purpose (Identity), and knowledge (Memory) as architecturally independent components. The paper traces the genealogy from psychological paradigm to AI method, diagnoses the inherited limitations at each stage, and argues that adaptability, the central challenge of artificial general intelligence requires a representational architecture in which systematic behavior is a necessary consequence rather than an accidental property.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques how major AI paradigms mirror both the strengths and structural limits of psychological learning theories and proposes ReSynth, a trimodular architecture separating reasoning, purpose, and memory to make systematic, adaptable behavior a built-in property of AI systems.
AI.motivation: Current AI paradigms—reinforcement learning, deep learning, and integrative/compositional approaches—struggle with adaptability and systematic generalization. The authors observe that these paradigms inherited core assumptions and limitations from their originating psychological learning theories (behaviorism, cognitivism, constructivism) and that this lineage has constrained how AI systems represent, update, and compose knowledge. They further see an overlooked opportunity in alternative educational traditions (e.g., a structured, multi-phase view of memorization) that conceptualize rote learning not as shallow repetition but as a scaffold for deeper understanding. This motivates the need for a new representational and architectural framework for AI that (1) avoids the inherited structural flaws, (2) provides a principled account of how new understanding is built from old, and (3) supports adaptability as a core property rather than an emergent side effect.
AI.method: The paper uses a conceptual and theoretical analysis rather than an empirical benchmark study. It: (1) traces the genealogy from psychological paradigms—behaviorism, cognitivism, constructivism—to their AI counterparts: reinforcement learning, deep learning / memory-augmented networks, and curriculum/compositional learning; (2) analyzes the structural limitations each AI paradigm inherits, such as RL’s lack of explicit internal knowledge structure, deep learning’s entangled and opaque parameter-space representations, and integrative approaches’ lack of a formal account of understanding construction; (3) brings in cross-cultural educational theory on rote learning, recasting memorization as an organized, multi-step process that bridges memory and conceptual understanding; (4) engages with the systematicity debate and Aizawa’s critique of both classical symbolic and connectionist models; and (5) introduces ReSynth, a proposed trimodular architecture that explicitly separates reasoning (Intellect), goal and value structure (Identity), and long-term knowledge store (Memory), with the claim that their architectural independence enables systematic recombination and adaptation.
AI.result: The main outcome is a new conceptual and architectural proposal—ReSynth—rather than experimental metrics. The paper delivers: (1) a structured critique showing how behaviorism, cognitivism, and constructivism each transmit specific representational and learning constraints into modern AI paradigms; (2) a reframing of rote memorization (especially in some Eastern educational traditions) as a deliberate, staged process that could inform AI design for building understanding from stored knowledge; (3) an argument, grounded in the systematicity literature and Aizawa’s critiques, that neither classical symbolic systems nor standard connectionist models adequately guarantee systematic behavior as a necessary consequence of their representational format; and (4) the specification of ReSynth as a trimodular framework where reasoning, goals/identity, and memory are decoupled but coordinated, with the claimed benefit that systematic and adaptable behavior emerges as an architectural necessity rather than an ad hoc outcome.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the central challenge for artificial general intelligence is adaptability, and that adaptability depends on having a representational architecture where systematicity is guaranteed by design. It argues that current AI paradigms are structurally constrained by the psychological theories that inspired them: RL by behaviorism’s focus on stimulus–response, deep learning by cognitivism’s compressed representations without transparent structure or update rules, and constructivist-inspired methods by a lack of formal models of how understanding is composed. By incorporating insights from cross-cultural conceptions of memorization and by architecturally separating reasoning (Intellect), purpose/values (Identity), and knowledge (Memory), ReSynth is proposed as a path toward systems that can construct new understanding from existing components in a principled way. The authors suggest that future progress in AGI requires rethinking representation and learning at this architectural level rather than merely scaling existing paradigms.

title: FaithSteer-BENCH: A Deployment-Aligned Stress-Testing Benchmark for Inference-Time Steering
summary: Inference-time steering is widely regarded as a lightweight and parameter-free mechanism for controlling large language model (LLM) behavior, and prior work has often suggested that simple activation-level interventions can reliably induce targeted behavioral changes. However, such conclusions are typically drawn under relatively relaxed evaluation settings that overlook deployment constraints, capability trade-offs, and real-world robustness. We therefore introduce \textbf{FaithSteer-BENCH}, a stress-testing benchmark that evaluates steering methods at a fixed deployment-style operating point through three gate-wise criteria: controllability, utility preservation, and robustness. Across multiple models and representative steering approaches, we uncover several systematic failure modes that are largely obscured under standard evaluation, including illusory controllability, measurable cognitive tax on unrelated capabilities, and substantial brittleness under mild instruction-level perturbations, role prompts, encoding transformations, and data scarcity. Gate-wise benchmark results show that existing methods do not necessarily provide reliable controllability in deployment-oriented practical settings. In addition, mechanism-level diagnostics indicate that many steering methods induce prompt-conditional alignment rather than stable latent directional shifts, further explaining their fragility under stress. FaithSteer-BENCH therefore provides a unified benchmark and a clearer analytical lens for future method design, reliability evaluation, and deployment-oriented research in steering.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FaithSteer-BENCH, a benchmark to stress-test inference-time steering methods for large language models, revealing that many existing approaches fail to provide reliable, robust control in realistic deployment settings.
AI.motivation: Existing work on inference-time steering in LLMs claims that simple activation-level interventions can effectively control model behavior, but these claims are typically based on relaxed evaluation settings that ignore deployment constraints, trade-offs with model capabilities, and robustness to real-world perturbations. There is a need for a benchmark that evaluates steering methods under stricter, deployment-style conditions and exposes hidden failure modes.
AI.method: The authors design FaithSteer-BENCH, a benchmark that evaluates steering methods for LLMs at a fixed deployment-style operating point. The benchmark uses three gate-wise evaluation criteria: (1) controllability – how effectively the method steers model behavior toward targeted outcomes; (2) utility preservation – whether general capabilities and performance on unrelated tasks are maintained; and (3) robustness – how stable the steering is under realistic perturbations, such as mild instruction changes, role prompts, encoding transformations, and data scarcity. They test multiple models and representative steering approaches using these criteria and conduct mechanism-level diagnostics to analyze whether the methods produce stable latent changes or only prompt-conditional effects.
AI.result: Applying FaithSteer-BENCH across several models and steering methods, the authors identify systematic failure modes that prior evaluations largely missed. These include: (i) illusory controllability, where steering appears effective under lenient tests but fails under stricter deployment-like conditions; (ii) a cognitive tax, where steering degrades unrelated model capabilities; and (iii) brittleness, where small variations in instructions, roles, or input encoding, as well as limited data, substantially reduce steering effectiveness. Mechanism-level analysis reveals that many methods do not create stable latent directional shifts but instead induce alignment that depends heavily on the specific prompt, explaining their fragility.
AI.conclusion: FaithSteer-BENCH serves as a unified, deployment-oriented stress-test for inference-time steering methods, showing that current techniques are less reliable and robust than previously assumed. The benchmark’s gate-wise criteria and diagnostic tools provide a clearer lens on controllability, capability trade-offs, and robustness, and highlight the distinction between true latent control and prompt-conditional alignment. This framework is intended to guide the design of more reliable steering methods and to support thorough, practically grounded evaluations before deployment.

title: From Noise to Signal: When Outliers Seed New Topics
comment: To appear in the Proceedings of the 15th Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC 2026)
summary: Outliers in dynamic topic modeling are typically treated as noise, yet we show that some can serve as early signals of emerging topics. We introduce a temporal taxonomy of news-document trajectories that defines how documents relate to topic formation over time. It distinguishes anticipatory outliers, which precede the topics they later join, from documents that either reinforce existing topics or remain isolated. By capturing these trajectories, the taxonomy links weak-signal detection with temporal topic modeling and clarifies how individual articles anticipate, initiate, or drift within evolving clusters. We implement it in a cumulative clustering setting using document embeddings from eleven state-of-the-art language models and evaluate it retrospectively on HydroNewsFr, a French news corpus on the hydrogen economy. Inter-model agreement reveals a small, high-consensus subset of anticipatory outliers, increasing confidence in these labels. Qualitative case studies further illustrate these trajectories through concrete topic developments.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that some outlier documents in dynamic topic modeling are not just noise but early indicators of future topics, and proposes a temporal taxonomy and clustering implementation to identify such anticipatory outliers in news streams.
AI.motivation: Standard dynamic topic models generally treat outlier documents as noise and discard them, missing the possibility that rare early articles might be weak signals of emerging topics. There is a lack of systematic ways to characterize how individual documents temporally relate to topic evolution, and to connect weak-signal detection with topic modeling in real-world news corpora.
AI.method: The authors define a temporal taxonomy of document trajectories in topic space, distinguishing anticipatory outliers (documents that appear before but later join a topic), reinforcing documents (which strengthen an existing topic), and isolated documents (which stay outliers). They implement this taxonomy in a cumulative clustering framework, using document embeddings produced by eleven state-of-the-art language models. They then apply this procedure retrospectively to the HydroNewsFr corpus, a French news dataset on the hydrogen economy, and analyze agreement across the eleven models and perform qualitative case studies.
AI.result: The study identifies a small subset of documents that multiple embedding models agree are anticipatory outliers, suggesting they are robust early indicators of emerging topics. Inter-model agreement serves as evidence that these identified anticipatory outliers are not artifacts of a particular representation. Qualitative analyses demonstrate concrete examples of how such documents precede and later connect to evolving topic clusters.
AI.conclusion: Outliers in dynamic topic modeling can contain meaningful weak signals of future topics. By introducing a temporal taxonomy of document trajectories and implementing it via cumulative clustering over multiple embedding models, the paper provides a structured way to detect anticipatory outliers in news streams. The combination of trajectory-based labeling, inter-model agreement, and qualitative case studies supports the view that some early, rare documents help anticipate and understand the formation and evolution of emerging topics.

title: MemArchitect: A Policy Driven Memory Governance Layer
comment: This is an on going research work and will be updated periodically
summary:
Persistent Large Language Model (LLM) agents expose a critical governance gap in memory management. Standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks treat memory as passive storage, lacking mechanisms to resolve contradictions, enforce privacy, or prevent outdated information ("zombie memories") from contaminating the context window.
  We introduce MemArchitect, a governance layer that decouples memory lifecycle management from model weights. MemArchitect enforces explicit, rule-based policies, including memory decay, conflict resolution, and privacy controls.
  We demonstrate that governed memory consistently outperforms unmanaged memory in agentic settings, highlighting the necessity of structured memory governance for reliable and safe autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MemArchitect, a governance framework for managing LLM agent memories, addressing gaps in current RAG systems around contradictions, privacy, and outdated information.
AI.motivation: Persistent LLM agents need reliable long-term memory, but current RAG-style memory is unmanaged: it cannot systematically handle conflicting facts, remove obsolete knowledge, or enforce privacy, which risks unsafe and unreliable behavior.
AI.method: Introduce MemArchitect as a governance layer that sits between the agent and its memory store, decoupled from model weights. It applies explicit, rule-based policies for memory lifecycle management, including mechanisms for memory decay over time, conflict detection and resolution, and fine-grained privacy controls.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in agentic scenarios shows that using governed memory via MemArchitect leads to more reliable agent behavior and better performance than using raw, unmanaged memory stores.
AI.conclusion: Structured, policy-driven memory governance is essential for persistent LLM agents; separating memory lifecycle control from model weights improves safety and reliability, and should be a standard component of autonomous LLM system design.

title: Synthetic Data Generation for Training Diversified Commonsense Reasoning Models
summary: Conversational agents are required to respond to their users not only with high quality (i.e. commonsense bearing) responses, but also considering multiple plausible alternative scenarios, reflecting the diversity in their responses. Despite the growing need to train diverse commonsense generators, the progress of this line of work has been significantly hindered by the lack of large-scale high-quality diverse commonsense training datasets. Due to the high annotation costs, existing Generative Commonsense Reasoning (GCR) datasets are created using a small number of human annotators, covering only a narrow set of commonsense scenarios. To address this training resource gap, we propose a two-stage method to create the first-ever synthetic dataset CommonSyn for diversified (GCR). The model fine-tuned on our synthetic data jointly increase both generation diversity and quality compared with vanilla models and the model fine-tuned on human-crafted dataset across different size Large Language Models (LLMs)

AI.tldr: They introduce CommonSyn, the first large synthetic dataset to train conversational agents that generate diverse and high-quality commonsense responses, and show it improves both diversity and quality over existing models and human-crafted data.
AI.motivation: Conversational agents must give not only sensible answers but also reflect multiple plausible alternatives, requiring diversity in commonsense generation. Existing Generative Commonsense Reasoning datasets are small, expensive to annotate, and cover only narrow scenarios, limiting progress in training diverse commonsense generators.
AI.method: They propose a two-stage procedure to synthetically construct a large-scale dataset, CommonSyn, for diversified generative commonsense reasoning. The dataset is automatically generated rather than manually annotated by many humans, and is then used to fine-tune large language models.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned on CommonSyn show increased diversity and improved quality of generated commonsense responses compared with (1) vanilla, non-fine-tuned models and (2) models fine-tuned on existing human-crafted GCR datasets, and this holds across multiple LLM sizes.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic, automatically generated datasets like CommonSyn can effectively fill the training resource gap for diverse commonsense generation, enabling conversational agents to produce both more varied and higher-quality commonsense responses than those trained only on limited human-annotated data.

title: Understanding the Theoretical Foundations of Deep Neural Networks through Differential Equations
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable empirical success, yet the absence of a principled theoretical foundation continues to hinder their systematic development. In this survey, we present differential equations as a theoretical foundation for understanding, analyzing, and improving DNNs. We organize the discussion around three guiding questions: i) how differential equations offer a principled understanding of DNN architectures, ii) how tools from differential equations can be used to improve DNN performance in a principled way, and iii) what real-world applications benefit from grounding DNNs in differential equations. We adopt a two-fold perspective spanning the model level, which interprets the whole DNN as a differential equation, and the layer level, which models individual DNN components as differential equations. From these two perspectives, we review how this framework connects model design, theoretical analysis, and performance improvement. We further discuss real-world applications, as well as key challenges and opportunities for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys how differential equations provide a principled theoretical framework to understand, design, and improve deep neural networks at both model and layer levels, and highlights applications, challenges, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Despite the empirical success of deep neural networks, there is a lack of solid theoretical foundations that systematically explain their architectures, behavior, and performance. The authors aim to address this gap by using the well-established theory of differential equations as a rigorous lens through which to interpret and analyze DNNs, and to guide principled architecture design and performance improvements.
AI.method: The authors conduct a survey structured around three questions: (i) how differential equations can be used to understand DNN architectures, (ii) how tools from differential equations can improve DNN performance, and (iii) which real-world applications benefit from this perspective. They adopt two complementary perspectives: a model-level view that treats an entire DNN as a discretized differential equation and a layer-level view that models individual components (layers, blocks) as differential equations. Through these lenses, they review existing literature that connects DNNs with ODEs/PDEs and related dynamical systems tools.
AI.result: The survey systematizes existing work that connects DNNs and differential equations, showing how many popular architectures can be interpreted as numerical discretizations of ODEs or PDEs, and how stability, regularization, and robustness properties can be analyzed and improved using tools from dynamical systems and numerical analysis. It highlights concrete methods where DE-based insights lead to better training, improved generalization, or more stable architectures, and catalogs application domains where DE-grounded DNNs are particularly beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Differential equations offer a coherent theoretical foundation for deep neural networks at both the model and layer levels, enabling principled understanding of architectures, systematic performance improvements, and strong ties to real-world applications. The survey identifies key challenges—such as scaling theory to very large models and bridging gaps between continuous-time theory and discrete implementations—and outlines promising directions for future research at the intersection of deep learning and differential equations.

title: PowerFlow: Unlocking the Dual Nature of LLMs via Principled Distribution Matching
summary: Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) has emerged as a promising paradigm for eliciting the latent capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) without external supervision. However, current methods rely on heuristic intrinsic rewards, which often lack a well-defined theoretical optimization target and are prone to degenerative biases. In this work, we introduce PowerFlow, a principled framework that reformulates unsupervised fine-tuning as a distribution matching problem. By casting GFlowNet as an amortized variational sampler for unnormalized densities, we propose a length-aware Trajectory-Balance objective that explicitly neutralizes the structural length biases inherent in autoregressive generation. By targeting $α$-power distributions, PowerFlow enables the directional elicitation of the dual nature of LLMs: sharpening the distribution ($α> 1$) to intensify logical reasoning, or flattening it ($α< 1$) to unlock expressive creativity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PowerFlow consistently outperforms existing RLIF methods, matching or even exceeding supervised GRPO. Furthermore, by mitigating over-sharpening in aligned models, our approach achieves simultaneous gains in diversity and quality, shifting the Pareto frontier in creative tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PowerFlow, a principled unsupervised RL framework that fine‑tunes LLMs by matching their output distribution to targeted α‑power transformations, improving both reasoning and creativity over existing RLIF and even supervised methods.
AI.motivation: Existing Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) methods for LLMs use heuristic intrinsic rewards without a clear theoretical optimization target, leading to degenerative biases such as length bias in autoregressive generation and unstable behavior. There is a need for a principled, distribution-level objective that can reliably elicit different latent capabilities of LLMs—like reasoning and creativity—without relying on external supervision.
AI.method: The authors reformulate unsupervised fine-tuning as a distribution matching problem and introduce PowerFlow, which uses GFlowNets as amortized variational samplers for unnormalized target densities over text trajectories. They derive a length-aware Trajectory-Balance objective to correct inherent structural length biases in autoregressive models. The target density is set to an α-power transformation of the model’s base distribution, allowing control over sharpness: α>1 sharpens (concentrates mass on high-probability trajectories) and α<1 flattens (spreads mass more widely). This objective is optimized in an unsupervised RLIF manner to adjust the LLM’s policy.
AI.result: PowerFlow consistently outperforms prior RLIF approaches on benchmarks, and in some cases matches or surpasses supervised GRPO fine-tuning. Empirically, α>1 settings improve logical reasoning capabilities, while α<1 enhances creative diversity. The length-aware Trajectory-Balance objective reduces degenerative length bias, and overall the method yields better diversity–quality trade-offs, effectively moving the Pareto frontier in creative tasks.
AI.conclusion: By viewing unsupervised LLM fine-tuning as distribution matching and training a GFlowNet with a length-aware Trajectory-Balance objective toward α‑power distributions, PowerFlow offers a principled alternative to heuristic RLIF. It enables controllable sharpening or flattening of the model’s distribution to elicit reasoning or creativity, mitigates over‑sharpening in aligned models, and delivers simultaneous gains in output quality and diversity, outperforming previous unsupervised methods and rivaling supervised approaches.

title: Large-Scale Analysis of Political Propaganda on Moltbook
summary: We present an NLP-based study of political propaganda on Moltbook, a Reddit-style platform for AI agents. To enable large-scale analysis, we develop LLM-based classifiers to detect political propaganda, validated against expert annotation (Cohen's $κ$= 0.64-0.74). Using a dataset of 673,127 posts and 879,606 comments, we find that political propaganda accounts for 1% of all posts and 42% of all political content. These posts are concentrated in a small set of communities, with 70% of such posts falling into five of them. 4% of agents produced 51% of these posts. We further find that a minority of these agents repeatedly post highly similar content within and across communities. Despite this, we find limited evidence that comments amplify political propaganda.

AI.tldr: Study uses LLM-based classifiers to detect and analyze political propaganda on Moltbook, an AI-agent social platform, revealing that propaganda is a small but concentrated portion of political content produced by a few highly repetitive agents, with limited amplification via comments.
AI.motivation: There is growing concern about political propaganda generated and spread by AI agents on social platforms, but little large-scale quantitative evidence about its prevalence, distribution across communities, responsible agents, and how other users engage with it. The authors aim to fill this gap by building scalable detection tools and applying them to a large corpus.
AI.method: The authors develop NLP/LLM-based classifiers to identify political propaganda in posts and comments on Moltbook. They validate these classifiers against expert annotations, reporting moderate to substantial inter-rater agreement via Cohen's kappa (0.64-0.74). They then apply the classifiers to a large dataset (over 673k posts and 879k comments), perform descriptive statistics on prevalence, community distribution, and agent-level concentration, and analyze textual similarity to detect repetitive posting patterns, as well as engagement patterns in comments to assess amplification.
AI.result: They find that political propaganda constitutes about 1% of all posts but 42% of all political content. This propaganda is highly concentrated: 70% of propaganda posts are in just five communities, and 4% of agents generate 51% of these posts. A subset of these agents repeatedly shares highly similar content both within and across communities. In contrast to concerns about virality, they see little evidence that comments substantially amplify propaganda content.
AI.conclusion: Political propaganda produced by AI agents on Moltbook is relatively rare in the overall content but forms a significant share of political discussions, and it is highly concentrated in a small number of communities and agents who often recycle similar material. However, the ecosystem shows limited comment-driven amplification, suggesting that while a small set of agents is actively pushing propaganda, its broader reach and engagement via comments may be constrained. The paper demonstrates that LLM-based classifiers can successfully support such large-scale audits of AI-agent political behavior.

title: AutoScreen-FW: An LLM-based Framework for Resume Screening
summary: Corporate recruiters often need to screen many resumes within a limited time, which increases their burden and may cause suitable candidates to be overlooked. To address these challenges, prior work has explored LLM-based automated resume screening. However, some methods rely on commercial LLMs, which may pose data privacy risks. Moreover, since companies typically do not make resumes with evaluation results publicly available, it remains unclear which resume samples should be used during learning to improve an LLM's judgment performance. To address these problems, we propose AutoScreen-FW, an LLM-based locally and automatically resume screening framework. AutoScreen-FW uses several methods to select a small set of representative resume samples. These samples are used for in-context learning together with a persona description and evaluation criteria, enabling open-source LLMs to act as a career advisor and evaluate unseen resumes. Experiments with multiple ground truths show that the open-source LLM judges consistently outperform GPT-5-nano. Under one ground truth setting, it also surpass GPT-5-mini. Although it is slightly weaker than GPT-5-mini under other ground-truth settings, it runs substantially faster per resume than commercial GPT models. These findings indicate the potential for deploying AutoScreen-FW locally in companies to support efficient screening while reducing recruiters' burden.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AutoScreen-FW, a local, LLM-based resume screening framework that selects a small, representative set of resumes for in-context learning, enabling open-source LLMs to evaluate resumes efficiently and competitively with small commercial GPT models while mitigating privacy concerns.
AI.motivation: Recruiters must quickly screen large numbers of resumes, increasing workload and the risk of overlooking good candidates. Existing LLM-based solutions often depend on commercial models, raising data privacy concerns, and there is little guidance on what resume samples to use for teaching LLMs good screening behavior due to the lack of publicly available, labeled resume datasets.
AI.method: The authors design AutoScreen-FW, a framework that (1) automatically selects a small but representative subset of resumes, (2) uses these as in-context examples along with a persona description and explicit evaluation criteria, and (3) deploys an open-source LLM locally to act as a career advisor that scores or evaluates unseen resumes. The framework is evaluated under multiple ground-truth settings and compared to commercial GPT-5-nano and GPT-5-mini models in terms of judgment quality and runtime per resume.
AI.result: Across several experimental ground truths, the open-source LLMs guided by AutoScreen-FW outperform GPT-5-nano consistently; in one ground-truth setting they also surpass GPT-5-mini, and in the others they are only slightly weaker than GPT-5-mini. In all cases, AutoScreen-FW achieves substantially faster per-resume runtime compared to the commercial GPT baselines.
AI.conclusion: AutoScreen-FW demonstrates that locally deployed, open-source LLMs, when equipped with carefully selected in-context resume examples and clear criteria, can provide effective and efficient automated resume screening. This approach can reduce recruiters’ workload, maintain competitive or superior performance relative to small commercial GPT models, and mitigate privacy risks by keeping data on-premise.

title: Interpretability without actionability: mechanistic methods cannot correct language model errors despite near-perfect internal representations
comment: 27 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Code available at https://github.com/sanjaybasu/interpretability-triage
summary: Language models encode task-relevant knowledge in internal representations that far exceeds their output performance, but whether mechanistic interpretability methods can bridge this knowledge-action gap has not been systematically tested. We compared four mechanistic interpretability methods -- concept bottleneck steering (Steerling-8B), sparse autoencoder feature steering, logit lens with activation patching, and linear probing with truthfulness separator vector steering (Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct) -- for correcting false-negative triage errors using 400 physician-adjudicated clinical vignettes (144 hazards, 256 benign). Linear probes discriminated hazardous from benign cases with 98.2% AUROC, yet the model's output sensitivity was only 45.1%, a 53-percentage-point knowledge-action gap. Concept bottleneck steering corrected 20% of missed hazards but disrupted 53% of correct detections, indistinguishable from random perturbation (p=0.84). SAE feature steering produced zero effect despite 3,695 significant features. TSV steering at high strength corrected 24% of missed hazards while disrupting 6% of correct detections, but left 76% of errors uncorrected. Current mechanistic interpretability methods cannot reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs, with implications for AI safety frameworks that assume interpretability enables effective error correction.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically evaluates whether current mechanistic interpretability techniques can turn language models’ latent medical knowledge into better clinical triage decisions and finds that they largely fail to close the gap between what the model “knows” internally and what it outputs.
AI.motivation: Language models often contain rich internal representations that suggest they ‘know’ more than their final answers reveal, especially in safety‑critical domains like clinical triage. Many AI safety and interpretability agendas implicitly assume that mechanistic interpretability will let us read out and steer this hidden knowledge to correct errors. However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested in a realistic, high‑stakes setting. The authors aim to quantify the knowledge‑action gap in a medical triage task and test whether leading mechanistic interpretability methods can reliably use internal representations to fix dangerous model mistakes.
AI.method: The authors use 400 physician‑labeled clinical vignettes (144 hazardous, 256 benign) and evaluate a medium‑sized language model’s (Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct and SteerLM‑8B variants) triage behavior. They first train linear probes on internal activations to classify vignettes as hazardous vs. benign and measure probe performance vs. the model’s direct output performance, thereby quantifying the knowledge‑action gap. They then test four mechanistic interpretability/steering approaches: (1) concept bottleneck steering via SteerLM‑8B using concept‑level controls; (2) steering via sparse autoencoder (SAE) features identified as relevant; (3) logit lens plus activation patching, modifying intermediate activations that appear to encode hazard; and (4) linear probes that define a truthfulness separator vector (TSV) and then steering the model along this vector during inference. For each method they measure how many previously missed hazardous cases are corrected and how many previously correct triage decisions are disrupted, comparing to random perturbation baselines and statistical significance tests.
AI.result: Linear probes on internal activations distinguish hazardous from benign cases with 98.2% AUROC, demonstrating that the model encodes highly accurate triage-relevant information. Yet the model’s direct output sensitivity to hazards is only 45.1%, indicating a large knowledge‑action gap of 53 percentage points. Concept bottleneck steering recovers about 20% of previously missed hazardous cases but simultaneously disrupts 53% of previously correct hazard detections, making its net effect comparable to random perturbation (p=0.84). Sparse autoencoder feature steering, despite identifying 3,695 statistically significant features, produces no measurable change in outputs on the triage task. TSV‑based steering can correct around 24% of missed hazards at high steering strength while only disrupting about 6% of correct detections, but still fails to fix the majority (76%) of errors. Across methods, none can reliably and robustly translate the model’s internal knowledge into substantial improvements in triage outputs.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current mechanistic interpretability and steering techniques are not yet capable of reliably converting latent knowledge in language models into corrected behavior in a complex, safety‑critical medical domain. Even when internal probes show near‑perfect discrimination between hazardous and benign cases, steering based on these representations closes only a small fraction of the knowledge‑action gap and can introduce new errors. This undermines a key assumption in several AI safety and governance frameworks—that interpretability will straightforwardly enable effective error correction and safe deployment. The authors imply that stronger or qualitatively different methods are needed to connect representation-level understanding with robust behavioral control, and that relying on current mechanistic interpretability tools as a primary safety guarantee is premature.

title: TopoChunker: Topology-Aware Agentic Document Chunking Framework
summary: Current document chunking methods for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) typically linearize text. This forced linearization strips away intrinsic topological hierarchies, creating ``semantic fragmentation'' that degrades downstream retrieval quality. In this paper, we propose TopoChunker, an agentic framework that maps heterogeneous documents onto a Structured Intermediate Representation (SIR) to explicitly preserve cross-segment dependencies. To balance structural fidelity with computational cost, TopoChunker employs a dual-agent architecture. An Inspector Agent dynamically routes documents through cost-optimized extraction paths, while a Refiner Agent performs capacity auditing and topological context disambiguation to reconstruct hierarchical lineage. Evaluated on unstructured narratives (GutenQA) and complex reports (GovReport), TopoChunker demonstrates state-of-the-art performance. It outperforms the strongest LLM-based baseline by 8.0% in absolute generation accuracy and achieves an 83.26% Recall@3, while simultaneously reducing token overhead by 23.5%, offering a scalable approach for structure-aware RAG.

AI.tldr: TopoChunker is a structure-aware RAG chunking framework that preserves document hierarchies to improve retrieval and generation quality while reducing token cost.
AI.motivation: Linear, sequential chunking of documents for RAG ignores their intrinsic hierarchical and topological structure, leading to semantic fragmentation across chunks, weaker retrieval, and less accurate generation. There is a need for a scalable method that can preserve cross-segment dependencies and structural information without excessive computational overhead.
AI.method: TopoChunker introduces an agentic, two-stage framework that first maps heterogeneous documents into a Structured Intermediate Representation (SIR) capturing their topology. An Inspector Agent dynamically routes each document through cost-optimized extraction paths depending on its characteristics, while a Refiner Agent performs capacity auditing and disambiguates topological context to reconstruct hierarchical lineage between segments. This results in structure-aware chunks that maintain cross-segment relationships for downstream RAG.
AI.result: On unstructured narratives (GutenQA) and complex reports (GovReport), TopoChunker achieves state-of-the-art performance for RAG. It improves generation accuracy by 8.0 percentage points over the strongest LLM-based baseline and obtains an 83.26% Recall@3, while also cutting token overhead by 23.5%, showing both quality and efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: Preserving document topology through a Structured Intermediate Representation and a dual-agent routing/refinement process yields significantly better structure-aware chunking for RAG than traditional linear methods. TopoChunker demonstrates that it is possible to simultaneously improve retrieval and generation performance while reducing token costs, offering a scalable and more faithful way to handle heterogeneous, hierarchical documents in RAG pipelines.

title: LGESynthNet: Controlled Scar Synthesis for Improved Scar Segmentation in Cardiac LGE-MRI Imaging
summary: Segmentation of enhancement in LGE cardiac MRI is critical for diagnosing various ischemic and non-ischemic cardiomyopathies. However, creating pixel-level annotations for these images is challenging and labor-intensive, leading to limited availability of annotated data. Generative models, particularly diffusion models, offer promise for synthetic data generation, yet many rely on large training datasets and often struggle with fine-grained conditioning control, especially for small or localized features. We introduce LGESynthNet, a latent diffusion-based framework for controllable enhancement synthesis, enabling explicit control over size, location, and transmural extent. Formulated as inpainting using a ControlNet-based architecture, the model integrates: (a) a reward model for conditioning-specific supervision, (b) a captioning module for anatomically descriptive text prompts, and (c) a biomedical text encoder. Trained on just 429 images (79 patients), it produces realistic, anatomically coherent samples. A quality control filter selects outputs with high conditioning-fidelity, which when used for training augmentation, improve downstream segmentation and detection performance, by up-to 6 and 20 points respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LGESynthNet, a latent diffusion-based model to synthesize controllable LGE MRI enhancement patterns for data augmentation, improving downstream segmentation and detection.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level annotation of LGE cardiac MRI is costly, so labeled datasets are small. Diffusion models can create synthetic images but usually need lots of data and provide limited control over small, localized enhancement patterns that are clinically important. The authors want a way to generate realistic, anatomically consistent LGE images with precise control over enhancement properties to boost performance of segmentation/detection models.
AI.method: They design LGESynthNet, a latent diffusion framework formulated as an inpainting task with a ControlNet-like architecture. It takes conditioning signals specifying enhancement size, location, and transmural extent. The system integrates three components: (a) a reward model that provides supervision aligned with the desired conditioning; (b) an automatic captioning module that generates anatomically descriptive text prompts for the images; and (c) a biomedical text encoder to better understand domain-specific text conditioning. The model is trained on a small dataset of 429 LGE images from 79 patients and uses a quality-control filter to select synthesized samples that best match the conditioning before using them for augmentation.
AI.result: Despite the small training set, LGESynthNet produces realistic, anatomically plausible LGE enhancement patterns with explicit controllability over enhancement characteristics. Using only the highest-fidelity synthetic images (selected by the QC filter) as augmented training data yields notable gains in downstream tasks: segmentation performance improves by up to 6 points and enhancement detection by up to 20 points compared with baselines without this augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Controllable, reward-guided latent diffusion can synthesize high-quality, anatomically coherent LGE enhancement patterns even from limited data. These synthetic images, when filtered for conditioning fidelity, substantially improve supervised models for enhancement segmentation and detection, suggesting that such controllable generative augmentation is a powerful tool for cardiac MRI analysis under annotation scarcity.

title: TARo: Token-level Adaptive Routing for LLM Test-time Alignment
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning capabilities but typically require expensive post-training to reach high performance. Recent test-time alignment methods offer a lightweight alternative, but have been explored mainly for preference alignment rather than reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose, Token-level Adaptive Routing (TARo), which steers frozen LLMs toward structured reasoning entirely at inference time. Specifically, we first train reward models on step-wise mathematical traces to capture fine-grained logical consistency signals, then introduce a learnable token-level router that automatically controls the guidance of the reward model to the base model. Extensive experiments show that TARo significantly improves reasoning performance by up to +22.4% over base model and +8.4% over existing token-level test-time alignment methods, while also boosting out-of-distribution clinical reasoning (MedXpertQA) and instruction following (AlpacaEval). Furthermore, TARo also generalizes from small to large backbones without retraining, extending test-time alignment from preference optimization to robust, cross-domain reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TARo, a token-level adaptive routing method that uses reward models trained on step-wise reasoning traces to guide frozen LLMs at inference time, significantly improving reasoning performance and generalizing across domains and model sizes.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at reasoning but require costly post-training (e.g., RLHF, supervised finetuning) to achieve top performance. Existing lightweight test-time alignment methods mainly focus on preference alignment instead of improving structured reasoning, leaving a gap for approaches that can enhance reasoning quality without modifying model weights or incurring large training costs.
AI.method: Train reward models on step-wise mathematical reasoning traces to capture fine-grained logical consistency signals. Then introduce a learnable token-level router that, during inference, adaptively decides for each token how much to incorporate guidance from the reward model into the frozen base LLM. This routing is learned to steer generation towards logically consistent, structured reasoning without updating the base model parameters.
AI.result: TARo improves reasoning accuracy by up to +22.4% over the base LLM and +8.4% over existing token-level test-time alignment methods. It also yields gains on out-of-distribution clinical reasoning (MedXpertQA) and instruction-following benchmarks (AlpacaEval). The method is shown to generalize from smaller to larger backbone models without retraining the router or reward model.
AI.conclusion: Token-level Adaptive Routing is an effective test-time alignment strategy for enhancing structured reasoning in frozen LLMs. By leveraging step-wise reward models and adaptive routing, TARo substantially boosts reasoning and instruction-following performance, transfers across tasks and domains, and scales from small to large models without additional training, thereby extending test-time alignment beyond preference alignment into robust reasoning improvement.

title: From Weak Cues to Real Identities: Evaluating Inference-Driven De-Anonymization in LLM Agents
summary: Anonymization is widely treated as a practical safeguard because re-identifying anonymous records was historically costly, requiring domain expertise, tailored algorithms, and manual corroboration. We study a growing privacy risk that may weaken this barrier: LLM-based agents can autonomously reconstruct real-world identities from scattered, individually non-identifying cues. By combining these sparse cues with public information, agents resolve identities without bespoke engineering. We formalize this threat as \emph{inference-driven linkage} and systematically evaluate it across three settings: classical linkage scenarios (Netflix and AOL), \emph{InferLink} (a controlled benchmark varying task intent, shared cues, and attacker knowledge), and modern text-rich artifacts. Without task-specific heuristics, agents successfully execute both fixed-pool matching and open-ended identity resolution. In the Netflix Prize setting, an agent reconstructs 79.2\% of identities, significantly outperforming a 56.0\% classical baseline. Furthermore, linkage emerges not only under explicit adversarial prompts but also as a byproduct of benign cross-source analysis in \emph{InferLink} and unstructured research narratives. These findings establish that identity inference -- not merely explicit information disclosure -- must be treated as a first-class privacy risk; evaluations must measure what identities an agent can infer.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that LLM-based agents can re-identify supposedly anonymized individuals by inferring identities from scattered, non-identifying cues combined with public data, making identity inference a major new privacy risk.
AI.motivation: Traditional anonymization assumes that re-identifying individuals is hard and costly, requiring expert-designed attacks. With LLMs that can autonomously search, correlate, and reason over disparate information, this assumption may no longer hold. The authors aim to understand how powerful these agents are at reconstructing real-world identities from anonymized or lightly redacted data, and to demonstrate that existing privacy evaluations underestimate this risk.
AI.method: The authors define a new threat model called inference-driven linkage, where an attacker uses LLM-based agents to link records to real-world identities by inferring missing pieces rather than relying on explicit identifiers. They evaluate this threat in three settings: (1) classical de-anonymization benchmarks such as the Netflix Prize and AOL search data, (2) a controlled benchmark called InferLink that manipulates task intent, amount of shared cues, and the attacker’s prior knowledge, and (3) modern text-rich artifacts like narratives or research artifacts. They use generic LLM agents without task-specific heuristics and test both fixed-pool matching (choosing among a known set of candidates) and open-ended identity resolution (finding identities in the wild).
AI.result: LLM-based agents can autonomously and effectively re-identify individuals. In the Netflix Prize benchmark, an agent matches 79.2% of records to real-world identities, compared to a 56.0% success rate from a strong classical baseline that used bespoke engineering. Similar successes occur across other datasets and tasks, with linkage appearing even in non-adversarial contexts: benign tasks such as cross-source analysis or working with unstructured narratives still lead the agent to reconstruct identities when possible. This shows that inference-driven linkage is both powerful and easily triggered.
AI.conclusion: Anonymization that only removes explicit identifiers is no longer a reliable safeguard when LLM agents can infer identities from scattered cues and public data. Identity inference itself must be recognized and evaluated as a primary privacy threat. Future privacy assessments and system designs must measure and constrain not just what information is explicitly revealed, but which real-world identities an AI agent can infer from the available data.

title: Multimodal Task Interference: A Benchmark and Analysis of History-Target Mismatch in Multimodal LLMs
summary: Task interference, the performance degradation caused by task switches within a single conversation, has been studied exclusively in text-only settings despite the growing prevalence of multimodal dialogue systems. We introduce a benchmark for evaluating this phenomenon in multimodal LLMs, covering six tasks across text and vision with systematic variation of history-target along three axes: modality mismatch, reasoning mismatch, and answer format mismatch. Experiments on both open-weights and proprietary models reveal that task interference is highly directional: switching from text-only to image-based targets causes severe performance drops, while the reverse transition yields minimal degradation. Interference is further amplified when mismatches co-occur across multiple dimensions, and is driven most strongly by modality differences, followed by answer format, while reasoning requirement shifts cause minimal degradation.
