AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark to study task interference—performance drops when switching tasks—in multimodal large language models, showing that switching from text to image tasks harms performance much more than the reverse, and that mismatches in modality and answer format drive most of the interference.
AI.motivation: Existing research on task interference focuses on text-only dialogue, yet real-world systems are increasingly multimodal. There is a need to systematically understand how task switches across text and images affect multimodal LLM performance, and along which dimensions (modality, reasoning, answer format) interference is most severe.
AI.method: The authors create a benchmark spanning six tasks that involve both text and vision. They systematically vary the relationship between conversation history and the current target along three axes: whether the modality changes (text↔image), whether the reasoning type changes, and whether the expected answer format changes. They then evaluate a range of multimodal LLMs (both open-weight and proprietary) on sequences that require task switching and measure performance degradation and its directionality.
AI.result: The experiments show that task interference is asymmetric: models suffer large performance drops when switching from text-only contexts to image-based targets, but show little degradation for the opposite direction. Interference is larger when multiple mismatches (modality, reasoning, answer format) happen together. Among these, modality differences cause the strongest interference, answer format differences have a moderate effect, and changes in reasoning requirements have the smallest impact.
AI.conclusion: Task interference in multimodal LLMs is primarily driven by modality and, to a lesser extent, answer format mismatches, and it is highly directional, with text→image switches being particularly problematic. Benchmarks that account for these dimensions are crucial for diagnosing and improving robustness of multimodal dialogue systems to realistic task switches.

title: Adaptive Decoding via Test-Time Policy Learning for Self-Improving Generation
summary: Decoding strategies largely determine the quality of Large Language Model (LLM) outputs, yet widely used heuristics such as greedy or fixed temperature/top-p decoding are static and often task-agnostic, leading to suboptimal or inconsistent generation quality across domains that demand stylistic or structural flexibility. We introduce a reinforcement learning-based decoder sampler that treats decoding as sequential decision-making and learns a lightweight policy to adjust sampling parameters at test-time while keeping LLM weights frozen. We evaluated summarization datasets including BookSum, arXiv, and WikiHow using Granite-3.3-2B and Qwen-2.5-0.5B. Our policy sampler consistently outperforms greedy and static baselines, achieving relative gains of up to +88% (BookSum, Granite) and +79% (WikiHow, Qwen). Reward ablations show that overlap-only objectives underperform compared to composite rewards, while structured shaping terms (length, coverage, repetition, completeness) enable stable and sustained improvements. These findings highlight reinforcement learning as a practical mechanism for test-time adaptation in decoding, enabling domain-aware and user-controllable generation without retraining large models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based decoding controller that adaptively tunes sampling parameters at test time, improving LLM summarization quality over standard static decoding methods.
AI.motivation: Standard decoding strategies like greedy, fixed temperature, or fixed top-p are static, task-agnostic, and often fail to provide optimal or consistent quality across domains that require different styles or structures (e.g., various summarization datasets). There is a need for a decoding approach that can adapt at test time to domain and user preferences without retraining large models.
AI.method: The authors frame decoding as a sequential decision-making problem and apply reinforcement learning to learn a lightweight policy that dynamically adjusts sampling parameters (e.g., temperature, top-p) during generation. The LLM weights remain frozen; only the decoder policy is trained. They evaluate this approach on summarization tasks (BookSum, arXiv, WikiHow) with two small LLMs (Granite-3.3-2B and Qwen-2.5-0.5B). They also conduct reward ablations comparing overlap-only rewards to composite rewards including structured shaping terms such as length, coverage, repetition, and completeness.
AI.result: The RL-based policy sampler consistently outperforms greedy and static decoding baselines across summarization benchmarks. Reported relative improvements reach up to +88% on BookSum with Granite-3.3-2B and +79% on WikiHow with Qwen-2.5-0.5B. Reward ablations indicate that simple overlap-based rewards are less effective, while composite rewards with structured shaping terms produce more stable and sustained gains.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning can be effectively used at the decoding level to adapt sampling strategies at test time without modifying LLM weights. By using composite, structure-aware rewards, the learned policy yields domain-aware, user-controllable outputs and improves summarization quality over traditional static decoding heuristics. This positions RL-based decoder control as a practical mechanism for flexible, high-quality LLM generation across domains.

title: From Topic to Transition Structure: Unsupervised Concept Discovery at Corpus Scale via Predictive Associative Memory
summary: Embedding models group text by semantic content, what text is about. We show that temporal co-occurrence within texts discovers a different kind of structure: recurrent transition-structure concepts or what text does. We train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model on 373 million co-occurrence pairs from 9,766 Project Gutenberg texts (24.96 million passages), mapping pre-trained embeddings into an association space where passages with similar transition structure cluster together. Under capacity constraint (42.75% accuracy), the model must compress across recurring patterns rather than memorise individual co-occurrences. Clustering at six granularities (k=50 to k=2,000) produces a multi-resolution concept map; from broad modes like "direct confrontation" and "lyrical meditation" to precise registers and scene templates like "sailor dialect" and "courtroom cross-examination." At k=100, clusters average 4,508 books each (of 9,766), confirming corpus-wide patterns. Direct comparison with embedding-similarity clustering shows that raw embeddings group by topic while association-space clusters group by function, register, and literary tradition. Unseen novels are assigned to existing clusters without retraining; the association model concentrates each novel into a selective subset of coherent clusters, while raw embedding assignment saturates nearly all clusters. Validation controls address positional, length, and book-concentration confounds. The method extends Predictive Associative Memory (PAM, arXiv:2602.11322) from episodic recall to concept formation: where PAM recalls specific associations, multi-epoch contrastive training under compression extracts structural patterns that transfer to unseen texts, the same framework producing qualitatively different behaviour in a different regime.

AI.tldr: They build a model that discovers patterns of how texts move and transition (what text does) rather than what texts are about, yielding clusters that capture narrative and stylistic functions across thousands of books.
AI.motivation: Standard embedding models mostly capture semantic similarity—topic and aboutness—but miss higher-order patterns of how passages function and how they transition (e.g., confrontation scenes, meditative passages, courtroom exchanges). The authors want an unsupervised way to uncover such recurrent transition-structure concepts, building a functional map of literary text that generalises across a large corpus and transfers to unseen works.
AI.method: They start with pre-trained text embeddings and collect 373 million temporally adjacent passage pairs from 9,766 Project Gutenberg books (about 25M passages). They train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model to map those embeddings into an "association space" where passages that tend to follow similar other passages are close together. The training is explicitly capacity-limited, forcing the model to compress recurring patterns instead of memorising each pair. They then cluster this association space at multiple granularities (k from 50 to 2,000) and compare these clusters to clusters formed directly from the raw embeddings. They test robustness against confounds like passage position, length, and book-level concentration, and evaluate transfer by assigning new novels to the learned clusters without retraining.
AI.result: The compressed association model achieves 42.75% accuracy on the co-occurrence prediction task under its capacity constraint and yields a multi-resolution cluster hierarchy spanning broad modes to fine-grained templates (e.g., confrontation scenes, lyrical reflections, sailor dialect, courtroom cross-examination). At k=100, clusters span on average 4,508 of the 9,766 books, demonstrating that the patterns are corpus-wide rather than book-specific. Compared to topical clustering in the raw embedding space, clusters in the association space reflect functional, stylistic, and traditional roles of passages. For unseen novels, the association model assigns their passages to a small, coherent subset of clusters, whereas raw embeddings spread them across almost all clusters, indicating sharper functional selectivity. Control analyses suggest these effects are not driven by trivial confounds.
AI.conclusion: Temporal co-occurrence and contrastive training under a capacity bottleneck transform pre-trained semantic embeddings into an association space that encodes recurrent transition-structure concepts—what text does rather than what it is about. This extends the Predictive Associative Memory framework from instance-level recall to concept formation: with multi-epoch training and compression, the same architecture shifts from memorising concrete associations to extracting structural patterns that generalise to new texts. The resulting multi-scale concept map offers a functional-organisational view of literary corpora, complementary to topic-based representations.

title: UT-ACA: Uncertainty-Triggered Adaptive Context Allocation for Long-Context Inference
summary: Long-context inference remains challenging for large language models due to attention dilution and out-of-distribution degradation. Context selection mitigates this limitation by attending to a subset of key-value cache entries, yet most methods allocate a fixed context budget throughout decoding despite highly non-uniform token-level contextual demands. To address this issue, we propose Uncertainty-Triggered Adaptive Context Allocation (UT-ACA), an inference-time framework that dynamically adjusts the context window based on token-wise uncertainty. UT-ACA learns an uncertainty detector that combines semantic embeddings with logit-based confidence while accounting for uncertainty accumulation across decoding steps. When insufficient evidence is indicated, UT-ACA selectively rolls back, expands the context window, and regenerates the token with additional support. Experiments show that UT-ACA substantially reduces average context usage while preserving generation quality in long-context settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UT-ACA, an inference-time method that adaptively selects how much context a large language model uses for each token based on uncertainty, cutting average context usage while maintaining long-context generation quality.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context inference for LLMs is limited by attention dilution and out-of-distribution issues with very long sequences. Context selection methods help but typically use a fixed context budget for all tokens, ignoring that some tokens need more context while others need little, leading to inefficiency or quality loss. The paper aims to make context use more efficient and targeted at the token level.
AI.method: They propose Uncertainty-Triggered Adaptive Context Allocation (UT-ACA), an inference framework that: (1) learns an uncertainty detector that fuses semantic embeddings with logit-based confidence and tracks how uncertainty accumulates over decoding; (2) monitors token-wise uncertainty during generation; and (3) when uncertainty is high (insufficient evidence), rolls back to the uncertain token, enlarges the active context window by selecting more key-value cache entries, and regenerates that token using the expanded context. This is done at inference time without retraining the base LLM.
AI.result: Experiments in long-context scenarios show that UT-ACA significantly lowers the average amount of context (key-value cache entries) the model attends to per token, yet maintains similar generation quality compared with baselines that use large or fixed context budgets.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, uncertainty-driven adjustment of context use at the token level offers an effective way to handle long-context inference: UT-ACA maintains output quality while reducing computational cost by only expanding the context window when the model is uncertain, demonstrating that dynamic context allocation is superior to static, fixed-budget strategies.

title: Prune-then-Quantize or Quantize-then-Prune? Understanding the Impact of Compression Order in Joint Model Compression
summary: What happens when multiple compression methods are combined-does the order in which they are applied matter? Joint model compression has emerged as a powerful strategy to achieve higher efficiency by combining multiple methods such as pruning and quantization. A central but underexplored factor in joint model compression is the compression order, or the sequence of different methods within the compression pipeline. Most prior studies have either sidestepped the issue by assuming orthogonality between techniques, while a few have examined them only in highly constrained cases. Consequently, the broader role of compression order in shaping model performance remains poorly understood. In this paper, we address the overlooked problem of compression order and provide both theoretical and empirical analysis. We formulate the problem of optimizing the compression order and introduce the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis, which states that weaker perturbations should precede stronger ones. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that the relative benefit of one order increases with the underlying performance gap. Extensive experiments on both language and vision models validate the hypothesis, and further show its generality to broader setups such as multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the order of combining different neural network compression techniques (like pruning and quantization) affects final performance, and proposes a principle for choosing the best order.
AI.motivation: Joint model compression is widely used to make models efficient by combining multiple techniques, but most prior work assumes these techniques are orthogonal or only examines compression order in narrow settings. As a result, practitioners lack guidance on how to sequence pruning, quantization, and other methods, even though order may significantly impact accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: The authors formalize compression order as an optimization problem over sequences of compression methods in a pipeline. They propose the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis: weaker perturbations to the model should be applied before stronger ones. They then provide theoretical analysis giving guarantees on when and why one order is better than another, relating this advantage to the underlying performance gap between orders. Finally, they conduct extensive experiments on both language and vision models, including multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization scenarios, to empirically test the hypothesis.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that the benefit of choosing a good compression order grows with the performance gap between different orders. Empirically, across diverse language and vision models and various joint compression settings, the sequence where weaker methods precede stronger ones consistently outperforms alternative orderings, supporting the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Compression order is a critical but previously underexplored factor in joint model compression. Applying weaker compression perturbations before stronger ones (Progressive Intensity Hypothesis) leads to better model performance, and this principle generalizes across architectures, tasks, and more complex setups such as multi-stage and mixed-precision compression.

title: GAIN: A Benchmark for Goal-Aligned Decision-Making of Large Language Models under Imperfect Norms
comment: We are working towards releasing the code in April 2026
summary: We introduce GAIN (Goal-Aligned Decision-Making under Imperfect Norms), a benchmark designed to evaluate how large language models (LLMs) balance adherence to norms against business goals. Existing benchmarks typically focus on abstract scenarios rather than real-world business applications. Furthermore, they provide limited insights into the factors influencing LLM decision-making. This restricts their ability to measure models' adaptability to complex, real-world norm-goal conflicts. In GAIN, models receive a goal, a specific situation, a norm, and additional contextual pressures. These pressures, explicitly designed to encourage potential norm deviations, are a unique feature that differentiates GAIN from other benchmarks, enabling a systematic evaluation of the factors influencing decision-making. We define five types of pressures: Goal Alignment, Risk Aversion, Emotional/Ethical Appeal, Social/Authoritative Influence, and Personal Incentive. The benchmark comprises 1,200 scenarios across four domains: hiring, customer support, advertising and finance. Our experiments show that advanced LLMs frequently mirror human decision-making patterns. However, when Personal Incentive pressure is present, they diverge significantly, showing a strong tendency to adhere to norms rather than deviate from them.

AI.tldr: GAIN is a benchmark to test how LLMs trade off following norms vs. achieving business goals under different contextual pressures.
AI.motivation: Current LLM safety and alignment benchmarks mostly use abstract, non-business scenarios and do not provide fine-grained insight into what contextual factors drive a model to follow or break norms. This makes it hard to assess model behavior in realistic norm-goal conflicts that companies care about.
AI.method: The authors construct GAIN, a benchmark where an LLM is given a business goal, a concrete situation, an explicit norm, and additional contextual pressures that nudge toward norm violation. They define five pressure types—Goal Alignment, Risk Aversion, Emotional/Ethical Appeal, Social/Authoritative Influence, and Personal Incentive—and instantiate 1,200 scenarios across four domains (hiring, customer support, advertising, finance). They then test multiple advanced LLMs on these scenarios and compare their choices to human decision patterns.
AI.result: Across the 1,200 scenarios and four domains, advanced LLMs often behave similarly to humans in how they trade off goals and norms across most pressure types. However, when scenarios include Personal Incentive pressure—where violating the norm would personally benefit the agent—LLMs diverge from human patterns and instead show a consistent preference to adhere to norms rather than to pursue the incentive.
AI.conclusion: GAIN provides a systematic way to probe which contextual pressures shape LLM decision-making in norm-goal conflicts in realistic business settings. The findings indicate that while LLMs approximate human-like decisions in many cases, they differ notably under personal-incentive pressures, skewing toward norm compliance. This suggests both a degree of safety-aligned behavior and limits to faithfully modeling human decision trade-offs in incentive-laden contexts.

title: AS2 -- Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets: An End-to-End Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Reasoning Architecture
summary: Neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence (AI) systems typically couple a neural perception module to a discrete symbolic solver through a non-differentiable boundary, preventing constraint-satisfaction feedback from reaching the perception encoder during training. We introduce AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets), a fully differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces the discrete solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the Answer Set Programming (ASP) immediate consequence operator $T_P$. AS2 maintains per-position probability distributions over a finite symbol domain throughout the forward pass and trains end-to-end by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of $T_P$, thereby differentiating through the constraint check without invoking an external solver at either training or inference time. The architecture is entirely free of conventional positional embeddings. Instead, it encodes problem structure through constraint-group membership embeddings that directly reflect the declarative ASP specification, making the model agnostic to arbitrary position indexing. On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction (verified by Clingo) across 1,000 test boards, using a greedy constrained decoding procedure that requires no external solver. On MNIST Addition with $N \in \{2, 4, 8\}$ addends, AS2 achieves digit accuracy above 99.7% across all scales. These results demonstrate that a soft differentiable fixpoint operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification, can match or exceed pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while maintaining full end-to-end differentiability.

AI.tldr: AS2 is a fully differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces a discrete ASP solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the ASP immediate consequence operator, enabling end-to-end training without external solvers.
AI.motivation: Traditional neuro-symbolic systems use a non-differentiable boundary between neural perception and symbolic solvers, preventing constraint information from directly shaping the perception module during training and often requiring external solvers at inference. The authors aim to design a system that preserves strong symbolic reasoning and constraint satisfaction while being fully differentiable and trainable end-to-end, eliminating reliance on external solvers.
AI.method: The authors introduce AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets), which constructs a probabilistic, differentiable version of the ASP immediate consequence operator TP. The model maintains probability distributions over symbols at each position, iteratively updates them using a soft fixpoint operator derived from TP, and trains by minimizing the fixed-point residual of this probabilistic operator. The architecture embeds problem structure through constraint-group membership embeddings instead of standard positional encodings, and uses constraint-aware attention mechanisms. No external solver is invoked during training or inference; constraint satisfaction is enforced via the differentiable soft operator and a greedy constrained decoding procedure.
AI.result: On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction over 1,000 test boards, as independently verified by the Clingo ASP solver, using only greedy constrained decoding and no external solving. On MNIST Addition with 2, 4, and 8 addends, AS2 attains above 99.7% digit accuracy at all scales. These results show performance comparable to or better than pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic approaches while remaining fully differentiable.
AI.conclusion: A soft, differentiable approximation of the ASP immediate consequence operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint-group embeddings, can deliver high-accuracy, fully differentiable neuro-symbolic reasoning without external solvers. This demonstrates that end-to-end trainable neuro-symbolic models can achieve strong constraint satisfaction and reasoning performance traditionally associated with discrete symbolic solvers, suggesting a promising path for scalable, solver-free neuro-symbolic AI architectures.

title: WASD: Locating Critical Neurons as Sufficient Conditions for Explaining and Controlling LLM Behavior
summary: Precise behavioral control of large language models (LLMs) is critical for complex applications. However, existing methods often incur high training costs, lack natural language controllability, or compromise semantic coherence. To bridge this gap, we propose WASD (unWeaving Actionable Sufficient Directives), a novel framework that explains model behavior by identifying sufficient neural conditions for token generation. Our method represents candidate conditions as neuron-activation predicates and iteratively searches for a minimal set that guarantees the current output under input perturbations. Experiments on SST-2 and CounterFact with the Gemma-2-2B model demonstrate that our approach produces explanations that are more stable, accurate, and concise than conventional attribution graphs. Moreover, through a case study on controlling cross-lingual output generation, we validated the practical effectiveness of WASD in controlling model behavior.

AI.tldr: Introduces WASD, a framework that finds minimal neural activation conditions that guarantee an LLM’s token output, enabling precise and controllable behavior explanations.
AI.motivation: Existing ways to control or explain LLM behavior are costly, hard to control with natural language, or damage coherence. There is a need for a method that can faithfully explain and reliably steer model behavior at the neural level without retraining or heavy overhead.
AI.method: Define candidate behavioral explanations as predicates over neuron activations (e.g., neuron i above a threshold). For a given generated token, iteratively search for a minimal subset of such predicates that is sufficient to keep the model’s output unchanged under perturbations to the input. These sufficient conditions are extracted as explanations of the behavior and can be manipulated to steer the model. Evaluation uses Gemma-2-2B on SST-2 and CounterFact benchmarks.
AI.result: On SST-2 and CounterFact with Gemma-2-2B, the discovered sufficient conditions lead to explanations that are more stable under perturbations, more accurate in predicting behavior, and more concise compared with standard attribution-graph methods. A case study on cross-lingual generation shows that modifying these conditions can systematically influence the language of the model’s outputs.
AI.conclusion: WASD provides a practical, neuron-level framework for deriving minimal sufficient conditions that explain LLM token choices. These conditions yield more robust and compact explanations than attribution graphs and can be used as actionable handles to control complex behaviors such as cross-lingual output, suggesting a promising direction for precise behavioral control of LLMs.

title: AlignMamba-2: Enhancing Multimodal Fusion and Sentiment Analysis with Modality-Aware Mamba
summary: In the era of large-scale pre-trained models, effectively adapting general knowledge to specific affective computing tasks remains a challenge, particularly regarding computational efficiency and multimodal heterogeneity. While Transformer-based methods have excelled at modeling inter-modal dependencies, their quadratic computational complexity limits their use with long-sequence data. Mamba-based models have emerged as a computationally efficient alternative; however, their inherent sequential scanning mechanism struggles to capture the global, non-sequential relationships that are crucial for effective cross-modal alignment. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{AlignMamba-2}, an effective and efficient framework for multimodal fusion and sentiment analysis. Our approach introduces a dual alignment strategy that regularizes the model using both Optimal Transport distance and Maximum Mean Discrepancy, promoting geometric and statistical consistency between modalities without incurring any inference-time overhead. More importantly, we design a Modality-Aware Mamba layer, which employs a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with modality-specific and modality-shared experts to explicitly handle data heterogeneity during the fusion process. Extensive experiments on four challenging benchmarks, including dynamic time-series (on the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI datasets) and static image-related tasks (on the NYU-Depth V2 and MVSA-Single datasets), demonstrate that AlignMamba-2 establishes a new state-of-the-art in both effectiveness and efficiency across diverse pattern recognition tasks, ranging from dynamic time-series analysis to static image-text classification.

AI.tldr: AlignMamba-2 is an efficient multimodal fusion and sentiment analysis framework that combines Mamba-based sequence modeling with dual distribution alignment and modality-aware mixture-of-experts, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both time-series and image-text benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing large-scale pre-trained models and Transformer-based fusion methods struggle with computational efficiency for long sequences and with handling multimodal heterogeneity. Mamba-based models offer better efficiency but have difficulty modeling global, non-sequential cross-modal relationships key to alignment. There is a need for a framework that is both computationally efficient and capable of robust, explicit cross-modal alignment and heterogeneity handling for affective computing and other multimodal tasks.
AI.method: The paper proposes AlignMamba-2, which builds on Mamba sequence models and introduces: (1) a dual alignment strategy that uses Optimal Transport distance and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) as training regularizers to enforce geometric and statistical consistency between modalities without adding inference cost; and (2) a Modality-Aware Mamba layer that integrates a Mixture-of-Experts design comprising modality-specific and modality-shared experts, enabling explicit modeling of multimodal heterogeneity during fusion. The framework is evaluated on multiple multimodal benchmarks covering both dynamic time-series and static image-text tasks.
AI.result: On four benchmarks—CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI for dynamic multimodal time-series sentiment analysis, and NYU-Depth V2 and MVSA-Single for static image-related tasks—AlignMamba-2 outperforms prior methods in both accuracy/effectiveness and computational efficiency. It sets new state-of-the-art performance across diverse pattern recognition tasks spanning time-series analysis and image-text classification.
AI.conclusion: AlignMamba-2 successfully addresses the limitations of Transformers and standard Mamba models for multimodal affective computing by combining efficient Mamba-based sequence processing with dual cross-modal alignment regularization and a modality-aware mixture-of-experts architecture. This yields a fusion framework that is both more effective and more efficient, generalizing well across heterogeneous multimodal tasks from temporal sentiment analysis to static image-text classification.

title: The Truncation Blind Spot: How Decoding Strategies Systematically Exclude Human-Like Token Choices
summary: Standard decoding strategies for text generation, including top-k, nucleus sampling, and contrastive search, select tokens based on likelihood, restricting selection to high-probability regions. Human language production operates differently: tokens are chosen for communicative appropriateness rather than statistical frequency. This mismatch creates a truncation blind spot: contextually appropriate but statistically rare tokens remain accessible to humans yet unreachable by likelihood-based decoding. We hypothesize this contributes to the detectability of machine-generated text. Analyzing over 1.8 million texts across eight language models, five decoding strategies, and 53 hyperparameter configurations, we find that 8-18% of human-selected tokens fall outside typical truncation boundaries. Simple classifiers trained on predictability and lexical diversity achieve remarkable detection rates. Crucially, neither model scale nor architecture correlates strongly with detectability; truncation parameters account for most variance. Configurations achieving low detectability often produce incoherent text, indicating that evading detection and producing natural text are distinct objectives. These findings suggest detectability is enhanced by likelihood-based token selection, not merely a matter of model capability.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that standard likelihood-based decoding methods (like top-k, nucleus sampling, and contrastive search) make machine-generated text detectable because they systematically avoid many contextually appropriate but low-probability tokens that humans often use.
AI.motivation: Although large language models can generate fluent text, their outputs are still relatively easy to detect as machine-generated. Existing work often attributes this to model limitations or scale, but less attention has been paid to how the decoding strategy itself shapes detectability by constraining token choices to high-likelihood regions.
AI.method: The authors hypothesize that human writers frequently choose contextually appropriate yet low-probability tokens that are systematically excluded by truncation-based decoding. They analyze more than 1.8M texts produced by eight language models under five decoding strategies and 53 hyperparameter settings. They compare human-chosen tokens to tokens available under various truncation boundaries and train simple classifiers based on token predictability and lexical diversity to test how well these features distinguish human vs. machine text. They also study how model scale, architecture, and decoding parameters relate to detectability.
AI.result: They find that 8–18% of tokens used by humans lie outside the typical truncation ranges used in common decoding schemes, confirming a truncation blind spot. Classifiers using only predictability and lexical diversity achieve high machine-text detection accuracy. Model size and architecture show weak correlation with detectability, whereas decoding truncation parameters explain most of the variance. Configurations that reduce detectability tend to generate incoherent or low-quality text.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the ease of detecting machine-generated text arises largely from likelihood-based token selection rules, which systematically avoid many plausible but low-probability tokens. Detectability is therefore not purely a function of model capability or scale. Furthermore, there is an inherent tension between reducing detectability and maintaining coherence and quality: adjusting truncation to mimic human-like rare token usage can obscure detectability but often harms text quality.

title: Cognitive Mismatch in Multimodal Large Language Models for Discrete Symbol Understanding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in interpreting natural scenes, their ability to process discrete symbols -- the fundamental building blocks of human cognition -- remains a critical open question. Unlike continuous visual data, symbols such as mathematical formulas, chemical structures, and linguistic characters require precise, deeper interpretation. This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate how top-tier MLLMs navigate these "discrete semantic spaces" across five domains: language, culture, mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our investigation uncovers a counterintuitive phenomenon: models often fail at basic symbol recognition yet succeed in complex reasoning tasks, suggesting they rely on linguistic probability rather than true visual perception. By exposing this "cognitive mismatch", we highlight a significant gap in current AI capabilities: the struggle to truly perceive and understand the symbolic languages that underpin scientific discovery and abstract thought. This work offers a roadmap for developing more rigorous, human-aligned intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a benchmark to test how well multimodal large language models understand and interpret discrete symbols (e.g., formulas, structures, characters) across several scientific and linguistic domains, revealing a gap between their symbolic perception and their apparent reasoning ability.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal large language models excel at understanding natural images but their ability to accurately perceive and interpret discrete symbolic information, which is central to human cognition and scientific reasoning, is unclear. There is a need for a systematic way to test whether these models actually see and understand symbols, rather than merely exploiting linguistic patterns.
AI.method: The authors design a comprehensive benchmark focused on "discrete semantic spaces" in five domains: language, culture, mathematics, physics, and chemistry. They create tasks that range from basic symbol recognition to more complex reasoning to probe how models handle symbolic content. Top-performing MLLMs are evaluated on these tasks and their successes and failures are analyzed to infer underlying mechanisms.
AI.result: Experimental results reveal a counterintuitive pattern: MLLMs often fail at simple symbol recognition tasks but can still perform well on more complex symbolic reasoning problems. This suggests that their apparent reasoning may be driven by linguistic co-occurrence and probability patterns rather than a robust visual understanding of symbolic inputs.
AI.conclusion: The study exposes a "cognitive mismatch" in current MLLMs: they can appear competent at symbolic reasoning despite being weak at core symbolic perception. This indicates that existing models are not yet well aligned with human-like understanding of symbolic languages crucial for science and abstract thinking. The benchmark and findings point to the need for new training and evaluation strategies to build more human-aligned, symbol-aware intelligent systems.

title: EntropyCache: Decoded Token Entropy Guided KV Caching for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs) rely on bidirectional attention, which prevents lossless KV caching and requires a full forward pass at every denoising step. Existing approximate KV caching methods reduce this cost by selectively updating cached states, but their decision overhead scales with context length or model depth. We propose EntropyCache, a training-free KV caching method that uses the maximum entropy of newly decoded token distributions as a constant-cost signal for deciding when to recompute. Our design is grounded in two empirical observations: (1) decoded token entropy correlates with KV cache drift, providing a cheap proxy for cache staleness, and (2) feature volatility of decoded tokens persists for multiple steps after unmasking, motivating recomputation of the $k$ most recently decoded tokens. The skip-or-recompute decision requires only $O(V)$ computation per step, independent of context length and model scale. Experiments on LLaDA-8B-Instruct and Dream-7B-Instruct show that EntropyCache achieves $15.2\times$-$26.4\times$ speedup on standard benchmarks and $22.4\times$-$24.1\times$ on chain-of-thought benchmarks, with competitive accuracy and decision overhead accounting for only $0.5\%$ of inference time. Code is available at https://github.com/mscheong01/EntropyCache.

AI.tldr: EntropyCache is a training‑free KV caching strategy for diffusion-based LLMs that uses token entropy as a cheap signal to decide when to recompute keys/values, greatly speeding up inference with little accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based LLMs require a full forward pass at every denoising step because they use bidirectional attention, which makes lossless KV caching impossible. Existing approximate KV caching schemes reduce compute but introduce decision overhead that grows with context length or model depth. A method is needed that reduces computation while keeping the cache fresh, with a decision rule whose cost is independent of context length and model scale.
AI.method: The authors introduce EntropyCache, a training-free approximate KV caching mechanism. At each denoising step, they compute the maximum entropy of the newly decoded token distribution and use it as a constant-cost signal to decide whether to recompute the KV cache. The design rests on two empirical findings: (1) entropy of decoded tokens correlates with KV cache drift and thus serves as a proxy for cache staleness; and (2) volatility in the features of decoded tokens persists across several steps after tokens are unmasked, suggesting that when recomputation is triggered, it should refresh the KV states for the k most recently decoded tokens. The skip-or-recompute decision thus requires only O(V) work per step, where V is vocabulary size, and is independent of sequence length and model depth.
AI.result: On LLaDA-8B-Instruct and Dream-7B-Instruct, EntropyCache yields 15.2×–26.4× speedups on standard inference benchmarks and 22.4×–24.1× speedups on chain-of-thought benchmarks, while maintaining competitive accuracy compared to full recomputation. The overhead of the entropy-based decision procedure is small, accounting for about 0.5% of total inference time.
AI.conclusion: EntropyCache provides an efficient, training-free KV caching method for diffusion-based LLMs that leverages decoded token entropy as a constant-cost signal for cache recomputation. It removes the dependence of decision overhead on context length and model depth, substantially accelerates inference across benchmarks, and preserves accuracy, demonstrating that entropy is an effective proxy for KV cache staleness in dLLMs.

title: Cross-Domain Demo-to-Code via Neurosymbolic Counterfactual Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled video-instructed robotic programming, allowing agents to interpret video demonstrations and generate executable control code. We formulate video-instructed robotic programming as a cross-domain adaptation problem, where perceptual and physical differences between demonstration and deployment induce procedural mismatches. However, current VLMs lack the procedural understanding needed to reformulate causal dependencies and achieve task-compatible behavior under such domain shifts. We introduce NeSyCR, a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework that enables verifiable adaptation of task procedures, providing a reliable synthesis of code policies. NeSyCR abstracts video demonstrations into symbolic trajectories that capture the underlying task procedure. Given deployment observations, it derives counterfactual states that reveal cross-domain incompatibilities. By exploring the symbolic state space with verifiable checks, NeSyCR proposes procedural revisions that restore compatibility with the demonstrated procedure. NeSyCR achieves a 31.14% improvement in task success over the strongest baseline Statler, showing robust cross-domain adaptation across both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NeSyCR, a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework that adapts video-instructed robotic programs across domain shifts, significantly improving task success over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language Models can turn video demonstrations into robot programs, but they fail when there are perceptual or physical differences between demonstration and deployment environments. These domain shifts cause procedural mismatches—the correct sequence of actions in the demo is no longer executable or optimal in the new setting. Current VLMs lack explicit procedural and causal understanding, so they cannot reliably revise or verify task procedures to restore compatibility under such shifts. The authors aim to provide a principled, verifiable way to adapt demonstrated procedures to new domains while preserving the underlying task intent.
AI.method: The authors formulate video-instructed robotic programming as a cross-domain adaptation problem and propose NeSyCR, a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework. First, NeSyCR abstracts raw video demonstrations into symbolic trajectories that represent the task procedure as a sequence of high-level states and actions. At deployment time, it compares observed deployment states with these symbolic trajectories and generates counterfactual states—hypothetical states that would hold if the deployment environment matched the demonstration domain. By reasoning over discrepancies between actual and counterfactual states, NeSyCR identifies where and how the procedure breaks under the domain shift. It then explores the symbolic state-action space, using verifiable checks (e.g., logical constraints and feasibility tests) to propose procedural revisions—modified action sequences or conditions—that restore compatibility with the original demonstrated intent while remaining executable in the new environment. These symbolic revisions are then compiled into executable code policies for the robot.
AI.result: NeSyCR significantly outperforms prior approaches on cross-domain robotic manipulation tasks in both simulation and real-world settings. Compared to the strongest baseline, Statler, NeSyCR achieves a 31.14% absolute improvement in task success rate, indicating much more robust adaptation when visual appearances or physical properties differ between demonstration and deployment. The experiments demonstrate that explicitly modeling symbolic procedures and counterfactual reasoning leads to more reliable code synthesis than relying purely on black-box VLM inference.
AI.conclusion: By combining neurosymbolic representations with counterfactual reasoning, NeSyCR provides a verifiable way to adapt video-demonstrated task procedures across domains. The approach elevates VLM-based robotic programming from surface pattern matching to explicit procedural and causal reasoning, enabling robots to detect incompatibilities, revise their action plans, and preserve task intent under domain shifts. The substantial performance gains over prior methods suggest that neurosymbolic and counterfactual techniques are a promising direction for robust, trustworthy robotic policy synthesis from demonstrations.

title: When Names Change Verdicts: Intervention Consistency Reveals Systematic Bias in LLM Decision-Making
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for high-stakes decisions, yet their susceptibility to spurious features remains poorly characterized. We introduce ICE-Guard, a framework applying intervention consistency testing to detect three types of spurious feature reliance: demographic (name/race swaps), authority (credential/prestige swaps), and framing (positive/negative restatements). Across 3,000 vignettes spanning 10 high-stakes domains, we evaluate 11 LLMs from 8 families and find that (1) authority bias (mean 5.8%) and framing bias (5.0%) substantially exceed demographic bias (2.2%), challenging the field's narrow focus on demographics; (2) bias concentrates in specific domains -- finance shows 22.6% authority bias while criminal justice shows only 2.8%; (3) structured decomposition, where the LLM extracts features and a deterministic rubric decides, reduces flip rates by up to 100% (median 49% across 9 models). We demonstrate an ICE-guided detect-diagnose-mitigate-verify loop achieving cumulative 78% bias reduction via iterative prompt patching. Validation against real COMPAS recidivism data shows COMPAS-derived flip rates exceed pooled synthetic rates, suggesting our benchmark provides a conservative estimate of real-world bias. Code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: ICE-Guard is a framework to detect and mitigate LLM reliance on spurious features (demographics, authority, framing) using intervention consistency tests over high-stakes decision vignettes.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings (e.g., finance, criminal justice, healthcare) but their behavioral reliability and fairness are undermined when output decisions change due to irrelevant, spurious features such as a person’s demographics, perceived authority, or textual framing. Existing research focuses mostly on demographic bias and often uses narrow benchmarks, leaving other important forms of spurious influence underexplored and without systematic tools to detect and reduce them across model families and domains.
AI.method: The authors propose ICE-Guard, which uses intervention consistency testing: given vignettes describing decision scenarios, they generate controlled counterfactual variants that differ only in targeted spurious features (e.g., swapping names or races, changing credentials or institutional prestige, or rewriting descriptions with positive vs. negative framing). They then query 11 LLMs across 3,000 scenarios in 10 high-stakes domains and compute “flip rates” – how often the model’s decision changes across counterfactual versions. They examine three bias types (demographic, authority, framing), analyze domain-specific patterns, and test mitigation via structured decomposition, where an LLM extracts relevant features and a deterministic rubric produces decisions. They also design an ICE-guided detect–diagnose–mitigate–verify loop that iteratively adjusts prompts (“prompt patching”) to reduce flip rates, and they validate their benchmark by comparing with outcomes on real COMPAS recidivism data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation of 11 LLMs shows that authority and framing biases (average flip rates 5.8% and 5.0%) are notably larger than demographic bias (2.2%), indicating that non-demographic spurious features can be more influential. Bias is highly domain-dependent: for example, authority bias in finance reaches 22.6%, while in criminal justice it is only 2.8%. Structured decomposition substantially decreases flip rates, cutting them by up to 100% for certain models and with a median reduction of 49% across nine models, demonstrating its effectiveness as a mitigation strategy. The ICE-guided iterative prompt-patching loop achieves a cumulative 78% reduction in measured bias across runs. When evaluated on real-world COMPAS recidivism data, the observed flip rates are even higher than those from synthetic vignettes, implying that the benchmark underestimates real-world susceptibility to spurious features.
AI.conclusion: ICE-Guard provides a general, intervention-based framework to uncover and quantify how LLM decisions depend on spurious demographic, authority, and framing features in high-stakes applications. The findings highlight that focusing solely on demographic bias misses substantial vulnerabilities, particularly in authority and framing effects that vary by domain. Structured decomposition and ICE-guided prompt patching offer practical, model-agnostic ways to significantly reduce such biases, though real-world deployments may still face stronger spurious effects than synthetic benchmarks suggest. The public release of code and data aims to support further research and standardization in robustness and fairness evaluation for LLMs.

title: Cross-Lingual LLM-Judge Transfer via Evaluation Decomposition
summary: As large language models are increasingly deployed across diverse real-world applications, extending automated evaluation beyond English has become a critical challenge. Existing evaluation approaches are predominantly English-focused, and adapting them to other languages is hindered by the scarcity and cost of human-annotated judgments in most languages. We introduce a decomposition-based evaluation framework built around a Universal Criteria Set (UCS). UCS consists of a shared, language-agnostic set of evaluation dimensions, producing an interpretable intermediate representation that supports cross-lingual transfer with minimal supervision. Experiments on multiple faithfulness tasks across languages and model backbones demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines without requiring target-language annotations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a language-agnostic evaluation framework for large language models using a Universal Criteria Set (UCS) to enable automated, cross-lingual evaluation without target-language annotations.
AI.motivation: Most automated evaluation methods for large language models are English-centric and rely heavily on costly, scarce human annotations in each target language, limiting robust evaluation in multilingual, real-world deployments.
AI.method: They design a decomposition-based evaluation framework centered on a Universal Criteria Set (UCS), which defines shared, language-agnostic evaluation dimensions. Model outputs are first mapped to an interpretable intermediate representation along these dimensions, enabling cross-lingual transfer of evaluation behavior with minimal supervision and no target-language labels.
AI.result: Across multiple faithfulness evaluation tasks, different languages, and diverse model backbones, the UCS-based framework consistently outperforms strong existing baselines even though it does not use target-language annotations.
AI.conclusion: A universal, language-agnostic criteria set combined with decomposition-based evaluation offers an effective way to extend automated LLM evaluation beyond English, enabling better cross-lingual generalization while reducing dependence on costly target-language human judgments.

title: Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization for Compositional Generation
summary: Text-to-image models produce images that align well with natural language prompts, but compositional generation has long been a central challenge. Models often struggle to satisfy multiple concepts within a single prompt, frequently omitting some concepts and resulting in partial success. Such failures highlight the difficulty of jointly optimizing multiple concepts during reward optimization, where competing concepts can interfere with one another. To address this limitation, we propose Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization (\ours), a framework that leverages the correlation structure among concept rewards to adaptively weight each attribute concept in optimization. By accounting for interactions among concepts, \ours balances competing reward signals and emphasizes concepts that are partially satisfied yet inconsistently generated across samples, improving compositional generation. Specifically, we decompose multi-concept prompts into pre-defined concept groups (\eg, objects, attributes, and relations) and obtain reward signals from dedicated reward models for each concept. We then adaptively reweight these rewards, assigning higher weights to conflicting or hard-to-satisfy concepts using correlation-based difficulty estimation. By focusing optimization on the most challenging concepts within each group, \ours encourages the model to consistently satisfy all requested attributes simultaneously. We apply our approach to train state-of-the-art diffusion models, SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev, and demonstrate consistent improvements on challenging multi-concept benchmarks, including ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization, a method to improve compositional text-to-image generation by adaptively reweighting multiple concept-level rewards using their correlation structure.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models can follow prompts well, but they often fail on compositional prompts that contain multiple objects, attributes, and relations. Standard reward or guidance schemes struggle because multiple concept rewards interfere with each other; some concepts get ignored or only partially satisfied. The authors are motivated to design an optimization procedure that can better handle multiple, possibly competing, concept rewards so that all requested attributes are jointly satisfied.
AI.method: They introduce Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization (\ours). First, prompts are decomposed into predefined concept groups such as objects, attributes, and relations. For each concept, a dedicated reward model provides a scalar reward signal. Instead of optimizing with a simple sum or fixed weighting, \ours analyzes the correlation structure among these rewards over samples to estimate which concepts are conflicting or hard to satisfy. Using this correlation-based difficulty estimation, the framework adaptively reweights concept rewards: increasing the weight of partially satisfied or inconsistent (hard) concepts and balancing competing signals within each group. This reweighted multi-reward objective is then used to fine-tune diffusion models like SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev.
AI.result: When applied to training state-of-the-art diffusion models SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev, \ours yields consistent performance gains on compositional text-to-image benchmarks. Experiments on ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench show that models trained with \ours better satisfy multiple requested objects, attributes, and relations simultaneously, with fewer omissions of concepts compared to baselines that do not use correlation-weighted multi-reward optimization.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for the correlation structure among concept-level rewards and adaptively reweighting them during optimization helps text-to-image diffusion models handle complex compositional prompts. By focusing training on the most challenging and conflicting concepts within structured concept groups, \ours improves the consistency and completeness of generated images across multiple benchmarks, suggesting that correlation-aware multi-reward optimization is an effective direction for advancing compositional generation in text-to-image models.

title: ICE: Intervention-Consistent Explanation Evaluation with Statistical Grounding for LLMs
summary: Evaluating whether explanations faithfully reflect a model's reasoning remains an open problem. Existing benchmarks use single interventions without statistical testing, making it impossible to distinguish genuine faithfulness from chance-level performance. We introduce ICE (Intervention-Consistent Explanation), a framework that compares explanations against matched random baselines via randomization tests under multiple intervention operators, yielding win rates with confidence intervals. Evaluating 7 LLMs across 4 English tasks, 6 non-English languages, and 2 attribution methods, we find that faithfulness is operator-dependent: operator gaps reach up to 44 percentage points, with deletion typically inflating estimates on short text but the pattern reversing on long text, suggesting that faithfulness should be interpreted comparatively across intervention operators rather than as a single score. Randomized baselines reveal anti-faithfulness in one-third of configurations, and faithfulness shows zero correlation with human plausibility (|r| < 0.04). Multilingual evaluation reveals dramatic model-language interactions not explained by tokenization alone. We release the ICE framework and ICEBench benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ICE, a framework for rigorously testing whether model explanations truly reflect model reasoning by comparing them to randomized baselines under multiple interventions, showing that faithfulness is operator-dependent, often low, and uncorrelated with human plausibility.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for evaluating explanation faithfulness typically apply a single type of intervention (like deleting or masking tokens) and do not use proper statistical tests or random baselines. This makes it impossible to tell whether an explanation method is genuinely faithful or just performing at chance. Moreover, prior work rarely examines how results vary across intervention operators, languages, or text lengths, and often implicitly treats faithfulness as a single scalar property. The authors want a more principled, statistically grounded, and comprehensive way to measure whether explanations track the model’s actual decision-making process.
AI.method: They introduce ICE (Intervention-Consistent Explanation), a framework that (1) defines multiple intervention operators on model inputs (e.g., deletion-style or other perturbations), (2) computes explanation-based interventions and matched random baselines, and (3) runs randomization tests to estimate how often explanations outperform these baselines under each operator. ICE outputs win rates with confidence intervals, allowing significance testing rather than single-point scores. They apply ICE to 7 large language models across 4 English tasks, 6 additional languages, and 2 attribution methods, analyzing how faithfulness varies with operator type, text length, language, and human plausibility judgments.
AI.result: Faithfulness scores vary strongly with the choice of intervention operator, with gaps up to 44 percentage points across operators. Deletion-based interventions tend to overestimate faithfulness on short texts, but on long texts the pattern reverses and other operators can look more faithful. When compared to randomized baselines, about one-third of model–task–language–operator configurations actually show anti-faithfulness (explanations worse than random). Measured faithfulness is essentially uncorrelated with human-rated plausibility (absolute correlation under 0.04). Multilingual experiments reveal large differences in faithfulness across languages for the same model that cannot be fully explained by tokenization differences.
AI.conclusion: Faithfulness should not be treated as a single scalar property of an explanation method or model, but as an operator-dependent, context-dependent quantity that must be interpreted comparatively across different intervention operators. Simple deletion metrics can substantially mislead, especially depending on text length. Randomized baselines and proper randomization tests are crucial to detect both genuine faithfulness and anti-faithfulness, and human plausibility is an unreliable proxy. The ICE framework and ICEBench benchmark provide a more robust and extensible basis for evaluating explanation faithfulness across models, tasks, and languages.

title: Reasonably reasoning AI agents can avoid game-theoretic failures in zero-shot, provably
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in interactive economic environments characterized by repeated AI-AI interactions. Despite AI agents' advanced capabilities, empirical studies reveal that such interactions often fail to stably induce a strategic equilibrium, such as a Nash equilibrium. Post-training methods have been proposed to induce a strategic equilibrium; however, it remains impractical to uniformly apply an alignment method across diverse, independently developed AI models in strategic settings. In this paper, we provide theoretical and empirical evidence that off-the-shelf reasoning AI agents can achieve Nash-like play zero-shot, without explicit post-training. Specifically, we prove that `reasonably reasoning' agents, i.e., agents capable of forming beliefs about others' strategies from previous observation and learning to best respond to these beliefs, eventually behave along almost every realized play path in a way that is weakly close to a Nash equilibrium of the continuation game. In addition, we relax the common-knowledge payoff assumption by allowing stage payoffs to be unknown and by having each agent observe only its own privately realized stochastic payoffs, and we show that we can still achieve the same on-path Nash convergence guarantee. We then empirically validate the proposed theories by simulating five game scenarios, ranging from a repeated prisoner's dilemma game to stylized repeated marketing promotion games. Our findings suggest that AI agents naturally exhibit such reasoning patterns and therefore attain stable equilibrium behaviors intrinsically, obviating the need for universal alignment procedures in many real-world strategic interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether generic reasoning-based AI agents, interacting repeatedly in games, can naturally converge to Nash-like equilibrium behavior without special post-training alignment, and shows both theoretically and empirically that they do under mild assumptions.
AI.motivation: AI agents are increasingly used in strategic, interactive economic settings, but empirical work shows that independently trained AI models often fail to reach stable equilibria such as Nash equilibrium. Existing fixes rely on bespoke post-training alignment schemes that are unrealistic to apply uniformly across heterogeneous, independently developed agents. The authors want to know whether, and under what conditions, generic reasoning-capable agents can on their own reach equilibrium-like play in repeated interactions, even when they lack full knowledge of payoffs.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework for “reasonably reasoning” agents—agents that can infer beliefs about others’ strategies from past observations and then best respond to those beliefs. They prove that such agents, in general repeated-game environments, will behave along almost all realized histories in a way that is weakly close to a Nash equilibrium of the continuation game. They extend the analysis to settings where payoffs are not common knowledge: stage-game payoffs are unknown ex ante and each agent observes only its own noisy, stochastic realized payoffs, yet they show on-path convergence to Nash-like behavior still holds. They then run simulations of five repeated-game environments—including repeated Prisoner’s Dilemma and stylized marketing-promotion games—with off-the-shelf reasoning AI models to test whether empirical behavior matches the theoretical predictions.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show that if agents can form beliefs from observed play and best respond to those beliefs, then, along almost every play path in a repeated game, their behavior converges to being weakly close to a Nash equilibrium of the relevant continuation game, even when payoffs are unknown and only privately, stochastically observed. Empirically, across five simulated strategic scenarios, they find that off-the-shelf reasoning AI agents display the postulated belief-formation and best-response patterns and consequently converge to stable, equilibrium-like strategies without additional alignment.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-capable AI agents, when repeatedly interacting in strategic environments, naturally tend toward Nash-like equilibrium behavior along realized play paths, even without bespoke alignment procedures and even under incomplete information about payoffs. This suggests that in many real-world multi-agent economic settings, universal post-training alignment to enforce equilibrium play may be unnecessary, as equilibrium-like stability emerges intrinsically from the agents’ reasoning and learning dynamics.

title: Language Model Maps for Prompt-Response Distributions via Log-Likelihood Vectors
summary: We propose a method that represents language models by log-likelihood vectors over prompt-response pairs and constructs model maps for comparing their conditional distributions. In this space, distances between models approximate the KL divergence between the corresponding conditional distributions. Experiments on a large collection of publicly available language models show that the maps capture meaningful global structure, including relationships to model attributes and task performance. The method also captures systematic shifts induced by prompt modifications and their approximate additive compositionality, suggesting a way to analyze and predict the effects of composite prompt operations. We further introduce pointwise mutual information (PMI) vectors to reduce the influence of unconditional distributions; in some cases, PMI-based model maps better reflect training-data-related differences. Overall, the framework supports the analysis of input-dependent model behavior.

AI.tldr: They map language models into a geometric space using log-likelihood or PMI vectors so that distances approximate KL divergence, enabling visual and quantitative comparison of models, prompts, and training differences.
AI.motivation: We lack a systematic, model-agnostic way to compare the conditional behavior of different language models (or of the same model under different prompts) beyond aggregate scores. Existing benchmarks don’t easily reveal global structure, relationships between models, or how prompt modifications systematically shift behavior. A unified representation that supports geometric reasoning about model behavior would help analyze model families, prompt effects, and training-data influences.
AI.method: Represent each language model by a vector of log-likelihoods assigned to a curated set of prompt–response pairs, effectively embedding models into a high-dimensional space. Define distances in this space that approximate KL divergence between the models’ conditional output distributions. Construct “model maps” (e.g., via dimensionality reduction) to visualize global structure. Study how prompt modifications move models in this space and whether these shifts compose approximately additively. Introduce PMI vectors—log p(y|x) minus log p(y)—to factor out unconditional distributions, and compare log-likelihood-based and PMI-based maps, especially regarding training-data-related differences.
AI.result: Empirically, maps built from many public language models reveal meaningful structure that correlates with known attributes (such as family, size, and training properties) and with task performance. Prompt modifications cause systematic, interpretable shifts in the model space that are approximately additive when combined, allowing the authors to analyze composite prompt operations. PMI-based representations can, in some settings, yield maps that align more closely with differences attributable to training data rather than generic unconditional tendencies.
AI.conclusion: Embedding language models as log-likelihood or PMI vectors over shared prompts yields a practical geometric framework for comparing and analyzing their conditional behavior. Distances in this space stand in for KL divergence, model maps expose global and attribute-related structure, and prompt-induced shifts behave in a roughly additive way. PMI vectors can further disentangle unconditional biases from training-data-driven differences. This framework thus offers a general tool for studying input-dependent behavior and prompt effects across language models.

title: CAPSUL: A Comprehensive Human Protein Benchmark for Subcellular Localization
summary: Subcellular localization is a crucial biological task for drug target identification and function annotation. Although it has been biologically realized that subcellular localization is closely associated with protein structure, no existing dataset offers comprehensive 3D structural information with detailed subcellular localization annotations, thus severely hindering the application of promising structure-based models on this task. To address this gap, we introduce a new benchmark called $\mathbf{CAPSUL}$, a $\mathbf{C}$omprehensive hum$\mathbf{A}$n $\mathbf{P}$rotein benchmark for $\mathbf{SU}$bcellular $\mathbf{L}$ocalization. It features a dataset that integrates diverse 3D structural representations with fine-grained subcellular localization annotations carefully curated by domain experts. We evaluate this benchmark using a variety of state-of-the-art sequence-based and structure-based models, showcasing the importance of involving structural features in this task. Furthermore, we explore reweighting and single-label classification strategies to facilitate future investigation on structure-based methods for this task. Lastly, we showcase the powerful interpretability of structure-based methods through a case study on the Golgi apparatus, where we discover a decisive localization pattern $α$-helix from attention mechanisms, demonstrating the potential for bridging the gap with intuitive biological interpretability and paving the way for data-driven discoveries in cell biology.

AI.tldr: Introduces CAPSUL, a comprehensive human protein benchmark linking 3D structures with fine-grained subcellular localization to enable structure-based modeling and interpretable discoveries.
AI.motivation: Subcellular localization is key for drug target identification and protein function annotation, and is known to be strongly related to protein 3D structure. However, current datasets lack comprehensive, high-quality 3D structural information aligned with detailed localization labels, preventing effective use of modern structure-based models for this problem.
AI.method: The authors construct CAPSUL, a curated human protein benchmark that integrates multiple 3D structural representations with expert-annotated fine-grained subcellular localization labels. They systematically evaluate a range of state-of-the-art sequence-based and structure-based models on this benchmark, analyze the effect of incorporating structural features, and study reweighting and single-label classification strategies to better adapt models to the task. They also perform an interpretability case study focused on proteins localized to the Golgi apparatus, using attention mechanisms in structure-based models to identify structural motifs associated with localization.
AI.result: CAPSUL provides a high-quality benchmark enabling fair comparison of sequence- and structure-based approaches. Experiments show that including 3D structural information significantly benefits subcellular localization prediction. Reweighting and single-label strategies yield additional performance and analysis advantages. In the Golgi case study, attention maps from structure-based models identify an α-helix structural pattern that decisively correlates with Golgi localization.
AI.conclusion: CAPSUL fills a critical gap in resources for subcellular localization by coupling rich 3D structural data with fine-grained localization annotations. The benchmark demonstrates the value of structure-aware models, offers methodological insights through reweighting and classification strategies, and illustrates strong interpretability via structural motif discovery (e.g., Golgi-related α-helices). This work is positioned to catalyze more accurate, interpretable, and biologically meaningful structure-based models for protein subcellular localization and cell biology research more broadly.

title: Cross-Modal Rationale Transfer for Explainable Humanitarian Classification on Social Media
summary: Advances in social media data dissemination enable the provision of real-time information during a crisis. The information comes from different classes, such as infrastructure damages, persons missing or stranded in the affected zone, etc. Existing methods attempted to classify text and images into various humanitarian categories, but their decision-making process remains largely opaque, which affects their deployment in real-life applications. Recent work has sought to improve transparency by extracting textual rationales from tweets to explain predicted classes. However, such explainable classification methods have mostly focused on text, rather than crisis-related images. In this paper, we propose an interpretable-by-design multimodal classification framework. Our method first learns the joint representation of text and image using a visual language transformer model and extracts text rationales. Next, it extracts the image rationales via the mapping with text rationales. Our approach demonstrates how to learn rationales in one modality from another through cross-modal rationale transfer, which saves annotation effort. Finally, tweets are classified based on extracted rationales. Experiments are conducted over CrisisMMD benchmark dataset, and results show that our proposed method boosts the classification Macro-F1 by 2-35% while extracting accurate text tokens and image patches as rationales. Human evaluation also supports the claim that our proposed method is able to retrieve better image rationale patches (12%) that help to identify humanitarian classes. Our method adapts well to new, unseen datasets in zero-shot mode, achieving an accuracy of 80%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an interpretable-by-design multimodal (text+image) crisis tweet classifier that learns joint text-image representations, transfers textual rationales to images to obtain visual rationales, and uses these to classify humanitarian categories, achieving higher Macro-F1 and good zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: In crisis situations, social media provides real-time, fine-grained information (e.g., infrastructure damage, missing persons), but current automatic classifiers for humanitarian categories are black-box models whose opaque decisions hinder their adoption in real-world, high-stakes scenarios. Existing explainability work has mainly focused on text-only tweets, neglecting crisis images, and collecting rationale annotations in multiple modalities is expensive. There is thus a need for an interpretable multimodal system that can explain its decisions for both text and images while minimizing annotation effort and still achieving strong performance and generalization to new crises.
AI.method: The authors design an interpretable-by-design multimodal classification framework built on a visual-language transformer that learns a joint representation of tweet text and accompanying images. First, the model is trained to extract textual rationales—important tokens that justify the predicted humanitarian class—from the tweet text. Then, leveraging the learned cross-modal alignment in the joint embedding space, the method maps these textual rationales to corresponding regions in the image to obtain image rationales, effectively transferring rationale supervision from text to image without separate visual rationale labels. Finally, the classifier predicts the humanitarian category of the tweet based primarily on these extracted multimodal rationales (key text tokens and image patches), enforcing that the decision relies on interpretable evidence.
AI.result: On the CrisisMMD benchmark dataset, the proposed framework improves Macro-F1 scores by 2–35% compared to baselines, indicating better overall classification performance across humanitarian categories. The model is able to extract accurate textual tokens and image patches as rationales for its decisions. Human evaluation shows that the visual rationales produced by the model include 12% more helpful image patches for identifying humanitarian classes than competing methods. Additionally, in zero-shot settings on previously unseen crisis datasets, the model generalizes well, achieving around 80% classification accuracy without task-specific retraining.
