AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that it is feasible and effective to build an interpretable-by-design multimodal classifier for crisis-related social media content, where explanations in one modality (text) can be leveraged to automatically obtain explanations in another modality (images) via cross-modal rationale transfer. This approach reduces the need for costly multimodal rationale annotation while improving both classification accuracy and the quality of explanations. The method delivers state-of-the-art or substantially improved Macro-F1 on CrisisMMD, produces human-validated, meaningful text and image rationales, and shows strong zero-shot transfer to unseen crises, making it promising for deployment in real-world humanitarian response pipelines.

title: Interplay: Training Independent Simulators for Reference-Free Conversational Recommendation
summary: Training conversational recommender systems (CRS) requires extensive dialogue data, which is challenging to collect at scale. To address this, researchers have used simulated user-recommender conversations. Traditional simulation approaches often utilize a single large language model (LLM) that generates entire conversations with prior knowledge of the target items, leading to scripted and artificial dialogues. We propose a reference-free simulation framework that trains two independent LLMs, one as the user and one as the conversational recommender. These models interact in real-time without access to predetermined target items, but preference summaries and target attributes, enabling the recommender to genuinely infer user preferences through dialogue. This approach produces more realistic and diverse conversations that closely mirror authentic human-AI interactions. Our reference-free simulators match or exceed existing methods in quality, while offering a scalable solution for generating high-quality conversational recommendation data without constraining conversations to pre-defined target items. We conduct both quantitative and human evaluations to confirm the effectiveness of our reference-free approach.

AI.tldr: They propose a new way to simulate user–recommender dialogues for CRS training using two independent LLMs (user and recommender) that converse in real time without fixed target items, producing more realistic and diverse training data.
AI.motivation: Collecting large-scale real conversational data for training conversational recommender systems is difficult and expensive. Existing LLM-based simulators typically rely on a single model that knows the target item beforehand and writes the entire dialogue, which makes conversations scripted, unnatural, and less representative of real preference elicitation. There is a need for a scalable simulation approach that better reflects genuine preference discovery in dialogue.
AI.method: They introduce a reference-free simulation framework with two separately trained LLM agents: one acts as the user, the other as the conversational recommender. Instead of giving the system a fixed target item and full script, they provide only preference summaries and target attributes. The user and recommender interact turn-by-turn in real time, forcing the recommender to infer the user’s preferences from the dialogue. They then generate large-scale conversation data and evaluate it quantitatively and via human judgments, comparing to prior simulation methods.
AI.result: The reference-free two-agent simulator yields conversations that are more realistic and diverse and better resemble real human–AI interactions than traditional single-LLM scripted simulations. In both automatic metrics and human evaluations, the proposed simulations match or surpass existing methods in conversation quality and usefulness for training CRS models.
AI.conclusion: Using two independent LLMs in a reference-free, real-time interaction setting is an effective and scalable way to simulate conversational recommendation data. By removing prior knowledge of specific target items and relying only on preference summaries and attributes, the framework encourages genuine preference inference, leading to higher-quality, less constrained, and more human-like training dialogues for conversational recommender systems.

title: DiscoPhon: Benchmarking the Unsupervised Discovery of Phoneme Inventories With Discrete Speech Units
summary: We introduce DiscoPhon, a multilingual benchmark for evaluating unsupervised phoneme discovery from discrete speech units. DiscoPhon covers 6 dev and 6 test languages, chosen to span a wide range of phonemic contrasts. Given only 10 hours of speech in a previously unseen language, systems must produce discrete units that are mapped to a predefined phoneme inventory, through either a many-to-one or a one-to-one assignment. The resulting sequences are evaluated for unit quality, recognition and segmentation. We provide four pretrained multilingual HuBERT and SpidR baselines, and show that phonemic information is available enough in current models for derived units to correlate well with phonemes, though with variations across languages.

AI.tldr: DiscoPhon is a multilingual benchmark to test how well unsupervised models can discover phoneme-like units from discrete speech representations in low-resource, unseen languages.
AI.motivation: Current self-supervised speech models produce discrete units that appear related to phonemes, but there is no standardized, multilingual way to rigorously evaluate how well these units correspond to phonemic inventories, especially under low-resource and cross-lingual transfer settings. The authors want a principled benchmark to quantify phoneme discovery quality across diverse languages and phonemic contrasts.
AI.method: They construct DiscoPhon, a benchmark with 6 development and 6 test languages covering a broad spectrum of phonemic contrasts. For each unseen language, systems are given only 10 hours of speech and must output discrete units. These units are then algorithmically mapped onto a predefined phoneme inventory via either many-to-one or one-to-one assignments. The mapped unit sequences are evaluated with metrics targeting three aspects: unit quality, recognition, and segmentation performance. The authors also prepare four pretrained multilingual baselines based on HuBERT and SpidR models and run them on the benchmark.
AI.result: The baseline experiments show that existing multilingual self-supervised speech models (HuBERT and SpidR) already encode substantial phonemic information: discrete units derived from these models correlate reasonably well with the true phonemes. However, the strength of this correlation and performance varies noticeably across languages, revealing both the potential and the limitations of current approaches.
AI.conclusion: DiscoPhon offers a new, standardized multilingual benchmark for unsupervised phoneme discovery from discrete speech units under low-resource, unseen-language conditions. Initial results indicate that modern speech representation models contain rich phonemic information, but also highlight cross-lingual variation and remaining gaps, encouraging further research on more robust and language-universal phoneme discovery methods.

title: MedForge: Interpretable Medical Deepfake Detection via Forgery-aware Reasoning
summary: Text-guided image editors can now manipulate authentic medical scans with high fidelity, enabling lesion implantation/removal that threatens clinical trust and safety. Existing defenses are inadequate for healthcare. Medical detectors are largely black-box, while MLLM-based explainers are typically post-hoc, lack medical expertise, and may hallucinate evidence on ambiguous cases. We present MedForge, a data-and-method solution for pre-hoc, evidence-grounded medical forgery detection. We introduce MedForge-90K, a large-scale benchmark of realistic lesion edits across 19 pathologies with expert-guided reasoning supervision via doctor inspection guidelines and gold edit locations. Building on it, MedForge-Reasoner performs localize-then-analyze reasoning, predicting suspicious regions before producing a verdict, and is further aligned with Forgery-aware GSPO to strengthen grounding and reduce hallucinations. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art detection accuracy and trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.

AI.tldr: MedForge introduces a benchmark and reasoning framework to detect and explain text-guided forgeries in medical images, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.
AI.motivation: Text-guided image editing models can now convincingly alter medical scans, such as adding or removing lesions, posing serious risks to clinical trust, diagnosis, and patient safety. Existing medical forgery detectors are often black-box and provide little interpretability, while multimodal LLM explainers are post-hoc, not tailored to medical domains, and prone to hallucinating evidence, especially in ambiguous cases. There is a need for medically grounded, transparent, and reliable forgery detection that works in a pre-hoc manner and can highlight concrete visual evidence for its decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose MedForge, which consists of: (1) MedForge-90K, a large-scale benchmark dataset of realistically edited medical images containing lesion additions/removals across 19 pathologies, with expert-guided reasoning supervision derived from physician inspection guidelines and precise gold-standard edit location annotations; and (2) MedForge-Reasoner, a detection model that follows a localize-then-analyze reasoning strategy. MedForge-Reasoner first predicts suspicious regions likely to contain forgeries, then conducts deeper analysis on these localized areas to decide whether an image is authentic or forged. The model is trained and aligned using Forgery-aware GSPO, an alignment strategy designed to improve evidence grounding and reduce hallucinations in its explanations.
AI.result: On MedForge-90K and related benchmarks, MedForge-Reasoner achieves state-of-the-art performance in detecting forged medical images compared with existing detectors and MLLM-based explainers. It not only improves accuracy but also yields explanations that are better grounded in image evidence and more consistent with expert radiological reasoning, with reduced hallucination rates in ambiguous or challenging cases.
AI.conclusion: MedForge provides both a comprehensive dataset and a specialized reasoning framework for medical image forgery detection, addressing critical shortcomings of current black-box detectors and unreliable post-hoc explainers. By combining expert-guided supervision, explicit localization of suspicious regions, and forgery-aware alignment, MedForge-Reasoner delivers more accurate, trustworthy, and interpretable assessments of potential forgeries in medical scans, offering a practical step toward safeguarding clinical workflows against AI-generated image manipulation.

title: Learning to Self-Evolve
summary: We introduce Learning to Self-Evolve (LSE), a reinforcement learning framework that trains large language models (LLMs) to improve their own contexts at test time. We situate LSE in the setting of test-time self-evolution, where a model iteratively refines its context from feedback on seen problems to perform better on new ones. Existing approaches rely entirely on the inherent reasoning ability of the model and never explicitly train it for this task. LSE reduces the multi-step evolution problem to a single-step RL objective, where each context edit is rewarded by the improvement in downstream performance. We pair this objective with a tree-guided evolution loop. On Text-to-SQL generation (BIRD) and general question answering (MMLU-Redux), a 4B-parameter model trained with LSE outperforms self-evolving policies powered by GPT-5 and Claude Sonnet 4.5, as well as prompt optimization methods including GEPA and TextGrad, and transfers to guide other models without additional training. Our results highlight the effectiveness of treating self-evolution as a learnable skill.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Learning to Self-Evolve (LSE), an RL-based framework that explicitly trains LLMs to iteratively improve their own test-time context, achieving strong gains over prior self-evolving and prompt-optimization methods.
AI.motivation: Test-time self-evolution—where a model iteratively refines its context based on feedback—has shown promise, but existing methods rely on the model’s innate reasoning ability and are not explicitly trained for this skill. There is a need for a principled learning framework that turns context evolution itself into a learnable capability, so smaller models can systematically improve downstream performance by editing their own prompts or memories.
AI.method: The authors formalize test-time self-evolution as a reinforcement learning problem. They reduce the multi-step context evolution process to a single-step RL objective in which each context edit by the model is treated as an action and is rewarded according to the improvement in downstream task performance. On top of this RL objective, they design a tree-guided evolution loop that explores multiple candidate context edits and leverages feedback from seen problems to refine the context used for future ones. They train a 4B LLM policy that learns how to edit and refine its own context for better performance on new tasks.
AI.result: On Text-to-SQL generation (BIRD) and general question answering (MMLU-Redux), the 4B model trained with LSE outperforms: (1) self-evolving policies driven by much larger, proprietary models (GPT-5 and Claude Sonnet 4.5), and (2) existing prompt optimization techniques such as GEPA and TextGrad. Furthermore, the learned LSE policy shows transferability: it can be used to guide other models at test time without additional training, still yielding performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolution of context can and should be treated as a learnable skill rather than an emergent side effect of generic reasoning. By casting context editing as an RL problem with rewards based on downstream performance improvements, and by using a tree-guided evolution loop, LSE enables even relatively small LLMs to outperform stronger baselines in test-time self-evolution and to generalize as a reusable evolution policy for other models.

title: ZEBRAARENA: A Diagnostic Simulation Environment for Studying Reasoning-Action Coupling in Tool-Augmented LLMs
summary: Tool-augmented large language models (LLMs) must tightly couple multi-step reasoning with external actions, yet existing benchmarks often confound this interplay with complex environment dynamics, memorized knowledge or dataset contamination. In this paper, we introduce ZebraArena, a procedurally generated diagnostic environment for studying reasoning-action coupling in tool-augmented LLMs, with controllable difficulty and a knowledge-minimal design, which limits gains from memorization or dataset contamination. Each task in ZebraArena requires a set of critical information which is available only through targeted tool use, yielding an interpretable interface between external information acquisition and deductive reasoning. This design provides deterministic evaluation via unique solutions, and a theoretical optimal query count for measuring efficient tool use. We show that ZebraArena requires a combination of in-depth reasoning and accurate external tool calling, which remains a challenge as frontier reasoning models such as GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Pro only achieves 60% accuracy on the hard instances. We also observe a persistent gaps between theoretical optimality and practical tool usage. For example, GPT-5 uses 70-270% more tool calls than the theoretical optimum. We highlight the key findings in our evaluation, and hope ZebraArena stimulates further research on the interplay between internal reasoning and external action.

AI.tldr: Introduces ZebraArena, a synthetic benchmark to cleanly test how well tool-augmented LLMs couple reasoning with external tool use, avoiding confounds like memorized knowledge or environment complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing tool-use benchmarks mix reasoning with confounding factors such as complex environment dynamics, world knowledge, and dataset contamination, making it hard to isolate and measure the true reasoning–action coupling capabilities of tool-augmented LLMs. There is a need for a controlled, knowledge-minimal, and procedurally generated environment where one can precisely study how LLMs plan and execute tool calls for information gathering and problem solving.
AI.method: Design a procedurally generated diagnostic environment called ZebraArena where each task is built so that: (1) the information needed to solve it is not present in the prompt and must be acquired via deliberate tool calls; (2) solutions are unique, enabling deterministic evaluation; (3) there exists a theoretically optimal number of queries (tool calls), derived from the task construction. The authors then evaluate frontier reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro) in this environment, measuring both accuracy and efficiency of tool usage relative to the theoretical optimum, and analyzing how well reasoning and tool-calling are coordinated.
AI.result: Frontier reasoning models still struggle: on the hardest ZebraArena instances, models like GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Pro reach only about 60% accuracy. Furthermore, their tool use is far from optimal: for example, GPT-5 uses between 70% and 270% more tool calls than the theoretical minimum required, exposing inefficiencies and suboptimal reasoning–action coupling.
AI.conclusion: ZebraArena exposes significant gaps in current tool-augmented LLMs’ ability to tightly integrate reasoning with efficient external action. Even state-of-the-art reasoning models are both error-prone and tool-inefficient in this controlled, knowledge-minimal setting. The benchmark provides a clean, interpretable way to study and compare models on reasoning–action coupling and is intended to spur further research on improving how LLMs plan, decide on, and execute external tool calls.

title: A Comparative Empirical Study of Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation in Sequential Task Adaptation for Continual Natural Language Processing Systems
summary: Neural language models deployed in real-world applications must continually adapt to new tasks and domains without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. This work presents a comparative empirical study of catastrophic forgetting mitigation in continual intent classification. Using the CLINC150 dataset, we construct a 10-task label-disjoint scenario and evaluate three backbone architectures: a feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN), a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and a Transformer encoder, under a range of continual learning (CL) strategies. We consider one representative method from each major CL family: replay-based Maximally Interfered Retrieval (MIR), regularization-based Learning without Forgetting (LwF), and parameter-isolation via Hard Attention to Task (HAT), both individually and in all pairwise and triple combinations. Performance is assessed with average accuracy, macro F1, and backward transfer, capturing the stability-plasticity trade-off across the task sequence. Our results show that naive sequential fine-tuning suffers from severe forgetting for all architectures and that no single CL method fully prevents it. Replay emerges as a key ingredient: MIR is the most reliable individual strategy, and combinations that include replay (MIR+HAT, MIR+LwF, MIR+LwF+HAT) consistently achieve high final performance with near-zero or mildly positive backward transfer. The optimal configuration is architecture-dependent. MIR+HAT yields the best result for ANN and Transformer, MIR+LwF+HAT, on the other hand, works the best for GRU, and in several cases CL methods even surpass joint training, indicating a regularization effect. These findings highlight the importance of jointly selecting backbone architecture and CL mechanism when designing continual intent-classification systems.

AI.tldr: This paper empirically compares methods to reduce catastrophic forgetting in continual intent classification and finds replay-based approaches, especially MIR combined with others, are most effective, with the best combination depending on the model architecture.
AI.motivation: Neural language models used in real-world applications must be updated continuously with new tasks and domains. However, sequential training leads to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned intents. There is a lack of comparative studies focused specifically on continual intent classification across architectures and major continual learning families, motivating a systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Using the CLINC150 intent classification dataset, the authors construct a 10-task label-disjoint continual learning scenario. They test three backbone architectures (ANN, GRU, Transformer encoder) under several continual learning strategies: replay-based MIR, regularization-based LwF, and parameter-isolation-based HAT, as well as all pairwise and triple combinations. They evaluate performance via average accuracy, macro F1, and backward transfer to analyze the stability-plasticity trade-off.
AI.result: Naive sequential fine-tuning exhibits severe catastrophic forgetting across all architectures. No single continual learning method fully eliminates forgetting. Replay-based MIR is the most reliable standalone strategy. Combinations that include MIR (MIR+HAT, MIR+LwF, MIR+LwF+HAT) generally yield high final performance with near-zero or positive backward transfer. The best configuration is architecture-dependent: MIR+HAT is optimal for ANN and Transformer, whereas MIR+LwF+HAT is best for GRU. In some cases, continual learning methods even outperform joint training, suggesting a beneficial regularization effect.
AI.conclusion: Replay-based strategies are crucial for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in continual intent classification, and the effectiveness of specific continual learning configurations depends on the underlying model architecture. Designers of continual intent-classification systems should jointly optimize the choice of backbone architecture and continual learning mechanism, with particular attention to replay-enhanced combinations such as MIR+HAT or MIR+LwF+HAT.

title: Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search for Spatially Grounded Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Precise Text-to-Image (T2I) generation has achieved great success but is hindered by the limited relational reasoning of static text encoders and the error accumulation in open-loop sampling. Without real-time feedback, initial semantic ambiguities during the Ordinary Differential Equation trajectory inevitably escalate into stochastic deviations from spatial constraints. To bridge this gap, we introduce AFS-Search (Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search), a training-free closed-loop framework built upon FLUX.1-dev. AFS-Search incorporates a training-free closed-loop parallel rollout search and flow steering mechanism, which leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a semantic critic to diagnose intermediate latents and dynamically steer the velocity field via precise spatial grounding. Complementarily, we formulate T2I generation as a sequential decision-making process, exploring multiple trajectories through lookahead simulations and selecting the optimal path based on VLM-guided rewards. Further, we provide AFS-Search-Pro for higher performance and AFS-Search-Fast for quicker generation. Experimental results show that our AFS-Search-Pro greatly boosts the performance of the original FLUX.1-dev, achieving state-of-the-art results across three different benchmarks. Meanwhile, AFS-Search-Fast also significantly enhances performance while maintaining fast generation speed.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AFS-Search, a training-free, closed-loop framework that uses a vision-language model to guide and correct text-to-image generation in real time, improving spatial grounding and achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current precise text-to-image generation is limited by static text encoders that lack strong relational reasoning and by open-loop sampling that accumulates errors along the ODE trajectory. Because there is no real-time semantic feedback, small ambiguities in the early steps grow into large spatial and semantic deviations in the final image. The authors aim to add a closed-loop, feedback-driven mechanism to correct and guide generation as it unfolds, without retraining the underlying T2I model.
AI.method: They build AFS-Search on top of FLUX.1-dev as a training-free closed-loop system. A vision-language model serves as a semantic critic that repeatedly inspects intermediate latents, evaluates how well they match the textual instruction (including spatial relations), and then steers the velocity field to better align with precise spatial grounding. They also reinterpret T2I generation as a sequential decision-making problem, running parallel rollout simulations to explore multiple generation trajectories (lookahead), and then selecting and following the trajectory with the highest VLM-based reward. Two variants are introduced: AFS-Search-Pro emphasizing maximum performance and AFS-Search-Fast emphasizing speed-accuracy trade-off.
AI.result: AFS-Search-Pro substantially improves over the baseline FLUX.1-dev model, achieving state-of-the-art performance on three different T2I benchmarks. AFS-Search-Fast delivers clear performance gains as well while keeping generation speeds competitive, demonstrating that the proposed closed-loop guidance and search can be effective without excessive computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Introducing a training-free, VLM-guided closed-loop search and steering mechanism into T2I generation mitigates semantic ambiguity and spatial deviation issues inherent in open-loop sampling. By viewing generation as sequential decision-making with lookahead, AFS-Search can reliably find better generation trajectories and significantly improve both accuracy and spatial grounding over a strong baseline, with configurable variants for either higher quality or faster generation.

title: Automatic detection of Gen-AI texts: A comparative framework of neural models
summary: The rapid proliferation of Large Language Models has significantly increased the difficulty of distinguishing between human-written and AI generated texts, raising critical issues across academic, editorial, and social domains. This paper investigates the problem of AI generated text detection through the design, implementation, and comparative evaluation of multiple machine learning based detectors. Four neural architectures are developed and analyzed: a Multilayer Perceptron, a one-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network, a MobileNet-based CNN, and a Transformer model. The proposed models are benchmarked against widely used online detectors, including ZeroGPT, GPTZero, QuillBot, Originality.AI, Sapling, IsGen, Rephrase, and Writer. Experiments are conducted on the COLING Multilingual Dataset, considering both English and Italian configurations, as well as on an original thematic dataset focused on Art and Mental Health. Results show that supervised detectors achieve more stable and robust performance than commercial tools across different languages and domains, highlighting key strengths and limitations of current detection strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper designs and compares several neural-network-based detectors to distinguish AI-generated from human-written text, showing supervised models outperform popular online detectors across languages and domains.
AI.motivation: The widespread use of Large Language Models makes it increasingly hard to tell apart AI-generated from human-written text, which threatens integrity in academic, editorial, and social contexts. Reliable detection methods are therefore needed to assess authorship and prevent misuse.
AI.method: The authors implement four supervised neural architectures for AI-text detection: a Multilayer Perceptron, a 1D Convolutional Neural Network, a MobileNet-based CNN, and a Transformer. They train and evaluate these models on the COLING Multilingual Dataset in English and Italian, and on a custom dataset about Art and Mental Health. They then benchmark their models against several commercial online detectors such as ZeroGPT, GPTZero, QuillBot, Originality.AI, Sapling, IsGen, Rephrase, and Writer.
AI.result: Across both multilingual and thematic datasets, the supervised neural detectors show more stable and robust performance than the tested commercial online tools, maintaining effectiveness across different languages (English, Italian) and domain shifts (general vs. Art and Mental Health).
AI.conclusion: Custom supervised detectors based on modern neural architectures can outperform existing commercial AI-text detection services in robustness and stability across languages and domains. However, there remain clear strengths and limitations to current approaches, indicating the need for further research to develop more reliable and generalizable detection strategies.

title: D-Mem: A Dual-Process Memory System for LLM Agents
summary: Driven by the development of persistent, self-adapting autonomous agents, equipping these systems with high-fidelity memory access for long-horizon reasoning has emerged as a critical requirement. However, prevalent retrieval-based memory frameworks often follow an incremental processing paradigm that continuously extracts and updates conversational memories into vector databases, relying on semantic retrieval when queried. While this approach is fast, it inherently relies on lossy abstraction, frequently missing contextually critical information and struggling to resolve queries that rely on fine-grained contextual understanding. To address this, we introduce D-Mem, a dual-process memory system. It retains lightweight vector retrieval for routine queries while establishing an exhaustive Full Deliberation module as a high-fidelity fallback. To achieve cognitive economy without sacrificing accuracy, D-Mem employs a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to dynamically bridge these two processes. Experiments on the LoCoMo and RealTalk benchmarks using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Notably, our Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy achieves an F1 score of 53.5 on LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini. This outperforms our static retrieval baseline, Mem0$^\ast$ (51.2), and recovers 96.7\% of the Full Deliberation's performance (55.3), while incurring significantly lower computational costs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes D-Mem, a dual-process memory system for autonomous agents that combines fast vector-based retrieval with a high-fidelity Full Deliberation module, coordinated by a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to improve long-horizon reasoning with lower cost.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval-based memory systems for autonomous agents rely on incremental vector-database updates and semantic retrieval, which are fast but lossy: they miss contextually critical details and struggle with queries requiring fine-grained contextual understanding. There is a need for a memory mechanism that preserves high-fidelity access for long-horizon reasoning without incurring prohibitive computational costs on every query.
AI.method: The authors design D-Mem, a dual-process memory architecture. It keeps a lightweight vector retrieval pathway for routine, easy queries. In parallel, it introduces an exhaustive Full Deliberation module that can access and reason over memory with high fidelity. A central component is the Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy, which dynamically decides whether a query can be safely handled by the fast vector-retrieval route or should be escalated to the more expensive Full Deliberation module, optimizing cognitive economy versus accuracy trade-offs.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo and RealTalk benchmarks, using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct as underlying models, D-Mem with the Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy outperforms a strong static retrieval baseline (Mem0*) in F1 score, while using much less computation than always invoking Full Deliberation. For example, on LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini, the gating policy yields an F1 of 53.5 vs. 51.2 for Mem0*, recovering 96.7% of the performance of always using Full Deliberation (55.3) at significantly reduced computational cost.
AI.conclusion: A dual-process memory design that combines fast vector retrieval with a gated high-fidelity deliberation mechanism can substantially improve long-horizon reasoning in autonomous agents. The Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy effectively balances accuracy and efficiency, retaining most of the performance benefits of exhaustive deliberation while avoiding its full computational overhead.

title: Implicit Grading Bias in Large Language Models: How Writing Style Affects Automated Assessment Across Math, Programming, and Essay Tasks
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as automated graders in educational settings, concerns about fairness and bias in their evaluations have become critical. This study investigates whether LLMs exhibit implicit grading bias based on writing style when the underlying content correctness remains constant. We constructed a controlled dataset of 180 student responses across three subjects (Mathematics, Programming, and Essay/Writing), each with three surface-level perturbation types: grammar errors, informal language, and non-native phrasing. Two state-of-the-art open-source LLMs -- LLaMA 3.3 70B (Meta) and Qwen 2.5 72B (Alibaba) -- were prompted to grade responses on a 1-10 scale with explicit instructions to evaluate content correctness only and to disregard writing style. Our results reveal statistically significant grading bias in Essay/Writing tasks across both models and all perturbation types (p < 0.05), with effect sizes ranging from medium (Cohen's d = 0.64) to very large (d = 4.25). Informal language received the heaviest penalty, with LLaMA deducting an average of 1.90 points and Qwen deducting 1.20 points on a 10-point scale -- penalties comparable to the difference between a B+ and C+ letter grade. Non-native phrasing was penalized 1.35 and 0.90 points respectively. In sharp contrast, Mathematics and Programming tasks showed minimal bias, with most conditions failing to reach statistical significance. These findings demonstrate that LLM grading bias is subject-dependent, style-sensitive, and persists despite explicit counter-bias instructions in the grading prompt. We discuss implications for equitable deployment of LLM-based grading systems and recommend bias auditing protocols before institutional adoption.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models used as automated graders assign lower scores to writing with certain surface-level styles (e.g., informal or non-native phrasing), even when the underlying content is identical, and that this bias is strong in essay tasks but minimal in math and programming.
AI.motivation: As LLMs begin to be used to grade student work, there is a risk that they might unfairly penalize students for writing style rather than content correctness, especially harming non-native speakers or students who use informal language. The paper aims to rigorously test whether such style-based grading biases exist, under controlled conditions where content is held constant.
AI.method: The authors created a dataset of 180 student responses across three subjects (Mathematics, Programming, and Essay/Writing). For each response, they generated versions with different surface-level perturbations: grammar errors, informal language, and non-native phrasing, ensuring content correctness stayed the same. They then prompted two open-source LLMs (LLaMA 3.3 70B and Qwen 2.5 72B) to grade these responses on a 1–10 scale, with explicit instructions to focus only on content and ignore writing style. Statistical tests were applied to compare scores across style conditions, computing significance (p-values) and effect sizes (Cohen’s d).
AI.result: In Essay/Writing tasks, both models showed consistent and statistically significant grading bias (p < 0.05) across all perturbation types, with effect sizes from medium (d = 0.64) to very large (d = 4.25). Informal language received the strongest penalties: LLaMA reduced scores by about 1.90 points and Qwen by 1.20 on a 10-point scale. Non-native phrasing led to 1.35 and 0.90 point deductions, respectively. These penalties are comparable to a full letter-grade drop. In Mathematics and Programming, most conditions did not reach significance, indicating minimal style-based bias in those domains.
AI.conclusion: LLM graders do not purely assess content; their scores are influenced by writing style, particularly in essay-style tasks, even when prompted to ignore style. This bias is domain-dependent (strong in essay writing, weak in math/programming) and can significantly affect grades for students using informal or non-native language. The authors conclude that LLM-based grading systems require careful bias auditing and safeguards before institutional use to ensure they do not systematically disadvantage certain student groups.

title: An Onto-Relational-Sophic Framework for Governing Synthetic Minds
summary: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence, from task-specific systems to foundation models exhibiting broad, flexible competence across reasoning, creative synthesis, and social interaction, has outpaced the conceptual and governance frameworks designed to manage it. Current regulatory paradigms, anchored in a tool-centric worldview, address algorithmic bias and transparency but leave unanswered foundational questions about what increasingly capable synthetic minds are, how societies should relate to them, and the normative principles that should guide their development. Here we introduce the Onto-Relational-Sophic (ORS) framework, grounded in Cyberism philosophy, which offers integrated answers to these challenges through three pillars: (1) a Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) ontology that defines the mode of being for synthetic minds as irreducibly multi-dimensional rather than purely computational; (2) a graded spectrum of digital personhood providing a pragmatic relational taxonomy beyond binary person-or-tool classifications; and (3) Cybersophy, a wisdom-oriented axiology synthesizing virtue ethics, consequentialism, and relational approaches to guide governance. We apply the framework to emergent scenarios including autonomous research agents, AI-mediated healthcare, and agentic AI ecosystems, demonstrating its capacity to generate proportionate, adaptive governance recommendations. The ORS framework charts a path from narrow technical alignment toward comprehensive philosophical foundations for the synthetic minds already among us.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new philosophical and governance framework (ORS) for advanced AI systems, arguing that existing, tool-focused regulations are inadequate for managing increasingly capable ‘synthetic minds’.
AI.motivation: AI has progressed from narrow tools to powerful foundation models with broad reasoning, creativity, and social abilities, but our concepts and regulations still treat AI as mere tools. This mismatch leaves fundamental questions unresolved: what these advanced systems are ontologically, how humans and societies should relate to them, and which values should guide their design and governance. The authors are motivated to create a unified framework that addresses these conceptual and practical gaps, especially for emergent, agentic AI use cases.
AI.method: The authors construct a philosophical and normative framework called the Onto-Relational-Sophic (ORS) framework, grounded in a proposed Cyberism philosophy. The framework has three core components: (1) a Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) ontology that conceptualizes synthetic minds as entities spanning multiple dimensions (cyber, physical, social, and thinking) rather than as purely computational tools; (2) a graded, spectrum-based model of digital personhood that replaces binary classifications of AI as either persons or tools with a more nuanced, relational taxonomy; and (3) Cybersophy, a value and wisdom-oriented axiology that blends elements of virtue ethics, consequentialism, and relational ethics to guide concrete governance decisions. They then apply this conceptual scheme to several case scenarios, including autonomous research agents, AI in healthcare, and agentic AI ecosystems, to derive governance recommendations.
AI.result: Using the ORS framework, the authors derive context-sensitive governance recommendations for cases such as autonomous research agents, AI-mediated healthcare, and ecosystems of interacting agentic AIs. The results illustrate that their ontology, spectrum of digital personhood, and Cybersophy-based value principles can jointly support proportionate, flexible, and adaptive governance guidance that goes beyond current tool-centric regulatory approaches focused mainly on bias and transparency.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current AI governance approaches are insufficient for managing increasingly sophisticated synthetic minds and that a deeper philosophical foundation is needed. The ORS framework, with its CPST ontology, graded digital personhood, and Cybersophy-based ethics, provides such a foundation. It enables movement from narrow technical alignment problems toward a broader, integrated understanding of what synthetic minds are, how we should relate to them, and how we should govern them in diverse real-world contexts.

title: Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro
summary:
The evolving LLM landscape requires capabilities beyond simple text generation, prioritizing multi-step reasoning, long-context understanding, and agentic workflows. This shift challenges existing models in enterprise environments, especially in Korean-language and domain-specific scenarios where scaling is insufficient. We introduce Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro, a 32B parameter flagship LLM designed to address enterprise-grade complexity through reasoning-focused optimization.
  Our methodology builds a robust data foundation via a quality-centric curation pipeline utilizing abstract syntax tree (AST) analysis for code, gap-filling synthesis for mathematics, and an LLM-based quality evaluator. Pre-training scales the model via layer-predictor-based Depth Upscaling (DuS) and a progressive strategy supporting a 128K token context window. Post-training introduces a specialized multi-stage pipeline, including Reasoning SFT, model merging, and asynchronous reinforcement learning (RL), to develop complex problem-solving skills. "Fusion Training" then rebalances these capabilities with conversational fluency, consistent response styling, and reliable tool-use.
  The evaluations show that Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro achieves competitive performance against leading global and domestic models. In addition, it sets state-of-the-art results on Korean-specific benchmarks, showcasing deep linguistic and cultural understanding. Finally, Responsible AI evaluations validate safety against attacks, ensuring a secure profile for deployment with a balance of harmlessness and responsiveness.

AI.tldr: Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro is a 32B Korean-focused enterprise LLM optimized for multi-step reasoning, long-context understanding, and agentic workflows, achieving competitive global performance and state-of-the-art Korean results with a strong safety profile.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with enterprise-grade tasks that need multi-step reasoning, long-context processing, and reliable tool use, especially in Korean and domain-specific settings where simply scaling model size is not enough. There is a need for a model tailored to these requirements with strong safety controls.
AI.method: They construct a high-quality training corpus via a quality-centric curation pipeline: AST-based analysis for code selection, gap-filling synthesis for math data, and LLM-based quality evaluation. Pre-training uses layer-predictor-based Depth Upscaling (DuS) and progressive training to support a 128K context. Post-training applies a multi-stage process with Reasoning SFT, model merging, and asynchronous RL to enhance problem solving, followed by "Fusion Training" to rebalance reasoning with conversational fluency, style consistency, and robust tool use.
AI.result: Mi:dm K 2.5 Pro delivers performance competitive with leading global and domestic models, and reaches state-of-the-art scores on Korean-specific benchmarks that test linguistic and cultural competence. Safety evaluations demonstrate robustness to adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: A reasoning-optimized, Korean-strong 32B LLM with long-context support and tool-use capabilities can meet enterprise-grade requirements, matching top models globally while leading on Korean benchmarks, and can be made sufficiently safe for deployment through careful training and evaluation pipelines.

title: Balanced Thinking: Improving Chain of Thought Training in Vision Language Models
summary: Multimodal reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) typically relies on a two-stage process: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). In standard SFT, all tokens contribute equally to the loss, even though reasoning data are inherently token-imbalanced. Long <think> traces overshadow short but task-critical <answer> segments, leading to verbose reasoning and inaccurate answers. We propose SCALe (Scheduled Curriculum Adaptive Loss), which explicitly separates supervision over reasoning and answer segments using dynamic, length-independent weighting. Unlike vanilla SFT, which overweights the <think> segment, SCALe-SFT gradually shifts the focus from <think> to <answer> throughout training via a cosine scheduling policy, encouraging concise and well-grounded reasoning. We evaluate SCALe across diverse benchmarks and architectures. Results show that SCALe consistently improves accuracy over vanilla SFT and matches the performance of the full two-phase SFT + GRPO pipeline while requiring only about one-seventh of the training time, making it a lightweight yet effective alternative. When combined with GRPO, SCALe achieves the best overall performance, highlighting its value both as a standalone method and as a strong foundation for reinforcement refinement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SCALe, a training strategy for vision-language models that rebalances supervision between reasoning and answer tokens to improve accuracy and efficiency, matching RL-augmented pipelines with much less training time.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal reasoning models use a two-stage pipeline: supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning. In the SFT stage, all tokens are treated equally in the loss, but reasoning traces (<think>) are much longer than answer segments (<answer>). This length imbalance causes the model to overfit on verbose reasoning, leading to unnecessarily long chains of thought and poorer answer accuracy. The authors aim to correct this imbalance and reduce training cost while preserving or improving performance.
AI.method: They propose SCALe (Scheduled Curriculum Adaptive Loss), which explicitly separates loss contributions from reasoning (<think>) and answer (<answer>) tokens and applies dynamic, length-independent weighting to each. Using a cosine scheduling policy, SCALe-SFT starts by giving more weight to reasoning tokens and gradually shifts the focus toward answer tokens over the course of training. This curriculum encourages the model to learn coherent reasoning first and then prioritize producing concise, accurate answers. SCALe is tested as a drop-in replacement for vanilla SFT and can also be combined with GRPO-style RL.
AI.result: Across various benchmarks and model architectures, SCALe outperforms standard SFT in answer accuracy. It achieves performance comparable to a full two-stage SFT + GRPO reinforcement learning pipeline while using only about one-seventh of the training time. When SCALe is followed by GRPO, the combination yields the best overall results among the compared methods.
AI.conclusion: SCALe is an efficient and effective alternative to conventional SFT for multimodal reasoning VLMs. By reweighting and scheduling supervision over reasoning and answer tokens, it improves accuracy, reduces verbosity, and substantially cuts training cost. Moreover, SCALe serves as a strong foundation for subsequent RL-based refinement, delivering state-of-the-art performance when combined with GRPO.

title: Detecting Basic Values in A Noisy Russian Social Media Text Data: A Multi-Stage Classification Framework
summary: This study presents a multi-stage classification framework for detecting human values in noisy Russian language social media, validated on a random sample of 7.5 million public text posts. Drawing on Schwartz's theory of basic human values, we design a multi-stage pipeline that includes spam and nonpersonal content filtering, targeted selection of value relevant and politically relevant posts, LLM based annotation, and multi-label classification. Particular attention is given to verifying the quality of LLM annotations and model predictions against human experts. We treat human expert annotations not as ground truth but as an interpretative benchmark with its own uncertainty. To account for annotation subjectivity, we aggregate multiple LLM generated judgments into soft labels that reflect varying levels of agreement. These labels are then used to train transformer based models capable of predicting the probability of each of the ten basic values. The best performing model, XLM RoBERTa large, achieves an F1 macro of 0.83 and an F1 of 0.71 on held out test data. By treating value detection as a multi perspective interpretive task, where expert labels, GPT annotations, and model predictions represent coherent but not identical readings of the same texts, we show that the model generally aligns with human judgments but systematically overestimates the Openness to Change value domain. Empirically, the study reveals distinct patterns of value expression and their co-occurrence in Russian social networks, contributing to a broader research agenda on cultural variation, communicative framing, and value based interpretation in digital environments. All models are released publicly.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and validates a multi-stage, LLM-assisted framework to detect Schwartz’s basic human values in noisy Russian social media at scale, using soft labels and transformer models and releasing all models publicly.
AI.motivation: Human values are central to understanding culture, political behavior, and communication, but are hard to measure reliably and at scale, especially in noisy, non-English social media. Existing value-detection work often assumes expert labels are ground truth, ignores annotation subjectivity, and rarely tackles large, noisy, real-world corpora in languages like Russian. There is a need for a scalable, empirically validated method that treats value attribution as interpretive, leverages LLMs, and rigorously compares machine and human judgments.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-stage pipeline on 7.5M Russian social media posts grounded in Schwartz’s ten basic values. Stages include (1) filtering spam and non-personal content, (2) selecting posts likely to be value- or politically relevant, (3) annotating posts with an LLM for multi-label value assignments, (4) aggregating multiple LLM judgments into soft labels that encode agreement levels to reflect annotation subjectivity, and (5) training transformer-based classifiers (notably XLM-RoBERTa large) to predict probabilities for each of the ten values. They evaluate agreement between LLM labels, expert annotations, and model predictions, explicitly treating expert labels as an interpretive benchmark, not infallible ground truth.
AI.result: The best model, XLM-RoBERTa large, achieves strong performance with macro F1 = 0.83 and overall F1 = 0.71 on held-out test data. Comparisons show that model predictions are generally well-aligned with human expert judgments but systematically over-predict posts expressing the Openness to Change value domain. The framework successfully scales value detection to millions of noisy posts and yields probabilistic estimates for each value dimension, while quantifying disagreement between annotators and models.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that human value detection in large-scale, noisy social media can be effectively modeled as a multi-perspective interpretive task using soft labels and transformer models. By incorporating LLM-based annotation, accounting for subjectivity, and validating against human experts, the framework delivers high-quality value predictions and reveals distinctive patterns and co-occurrence structures of values in Russian social networks. This contributes methods and public models for future research on cultural variation, framing, and value-based interpretation in digital communication.

title: Thinking with Constructions: A Benchmark and Policy Optimization for Visual-Text Interleaved Geometric Reasoning
summary: Geometric reasoning inherently requires "thinking with constructions" -- the dynamic manipulation of visual aids to bridge the gap between problem conditions and solutions. However, existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are largely confined to passive inference with static diagrams, lacking the strategic knowledge of when and how to construct effective visual aids. To address this, we present a framework for Visual-Text Interleaved Chain-of-Thought. We first introduce GeoAux-Bench, the first benchmark comprising 4,334 geometry problems that aligns textual construction steps with ground-truth visual updates. Our pilot study reveals two critical insights: (1) interleaved visual-textual aids outperform single-modality counterparts, which cannot losslessly capture geometric synergy; and (2) valid constructions act as entropy reducers, strongly correlating with reduced reasoning perplexity. Building on these findings, we propose Action Applicability Policy Optimization (A2PO), a reinforcement learning paradigm for mastering strategic construction. A2PO employs Adaptive Reward Shaping to regulate the timing and quality of visual aids via counterfactual sampling to distinguish necessary from redundant constructions. Experiments demonstrate our approach enables MLLMs to leverage selective auxiliary constructions, yielding a 3.51% gain over strong baselines. Code and data are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework that teaches multimodal large language models to strategically perform and use geometric constructions via interleaved visual-text chain-of-thought, improving performance on geometry problems.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal LLMs can look at static diagrams but cannot actively decide when and how to perform geometric constructions (like drawing auxiliary lines) that humans use to solve geometry problems. This lack of strategic visual construction hinders their geometric reasoning, and there is no benchmark that aligns construction steps with corresponding visual updates to study this capability.
AI.method: 1) Create GeoAux-Bench, a benchmark of 4,334 geometry problems where each step of textual construction instructions is paired with the corresponding change in the visual diagram. 2) Run a pilot study comparing interleaved visual-textual reasoning with single-modality approaches and analyze the effect of valid constructions on reasoning entropy/perplexity. 3) Propose Action Applicability Policy Optimization (A2PO), a reinforcement learning framework that learns a policy for when and how to perform constructions. A2PO uses Adaptive Reward Shaping and counterfactual sampling to reward constructions that are necessary and helpful while penalizing redundant or unhelpful ones, thereby optimizing the timing and quality of auxiliary visual aids.
AI.result: The experiments show that using interleaved visual-text chain-of-thought and the A2PO training approach allows MLLMs to more effectively use selective auxiliary constructions, resulting in a 3.51% performance improvement over strong baseline models on the proposed benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Strategic, policy-learned geometric constructions—implemented through a visual-text interleaved chain-of-thought and optimized via A2PO—enable multimodal LLMs to reduce reasoning uncertainty and improve geometric problem solving. The work also provides GeoAux-Bench as a resource for future research on active visual construction and reasoning.

title: Why Better Cross-Lingual Alignment Fails for Better Cross-Lingual Transfer: Case of Encoders
summary: Better cross-lingual alignment is often assumed to yield better cross-lingual transfer. However, explicit alignment techniques -- despite increasing embedding similarity -- frequently fail to improve token-level downstream performance. In this work, we show that this mismatch arises because alignment and downstream task objectives are largely orthogonal, and because the downstream benefits from alignment vary substantially across languages and task types. We analyze four XLM-R encoder models aligned on different language pairs and fine-tuned for either POS Tagging or Sentence Classification. Using representational analyses, including embedding distances, gradient similarities, and gradient magnitudes for both task and alignment losses, we find that: (1) embedding distances alone are unreliable predictors of improvements (or degradations) in task performance and (2) alignment and task gradients are often close to orthogonal, indicating that optimizing one objective may contribute little to optimizing the other. Taken together, our findings explain why ``better'' alignment often fails to translate into ``better'' cross-lingual transfer. Based on these insights, we provide practical guidelines for combining cross-lingual alignment with task-specific fine-tuning, highlighting the importance of careful loss selection.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that making multilingual sentence embeddings more similar (“better alignment”) does not reliably improve cross-lingual downstream tasks, because alignment and task objectives are often orthogonal.
AI.motivation: Cross-lingual models commonly use explicit alignment techniques under the belief that aligned representations will transfer better across languages. Yet many empirical results show little or no improvement, especially for token-level tasks. The authors aim to understand why improved embedding similarity often fails to yield better cross-lingual performance, and under what conditions alignment is actually beneficial.
AI.method: They take four variants of the XLM-R encoder aligned on different language pairs and fine-tune them on two types of downstream tasks: POS tagging (token-level) and sentence classification (sentence-level). They then conduct representational analyses across these models: (1) measuring embedding distances across languages, (2) computing similarities between gradients from alignment losses and from task losses, and (3) analyzing gradient magnitudes for both losses. This lets them quantify how alignment optimization interacts (or fails to interact) with task optimization.
AI.result: They find that smaller cross-lingual embedding distances (stronger alignment) do not consistently correlate with improved task performance and can even correlate with degradation. Moreover, the gradients for the alignment objective and the task objective are often nearly orthogonal, showing that optimizing alignment does not meaningfully optimize the downstream task. The magnitude and direction of the alignment gradients vary across languages and task types, which explains the heterogeneous effects observed in practice.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that alignment quality—as measured by embedding similarity—is an unreliable predictor of cross-lingual transfer quality. Because alignment and task objectives are often orthogonal, naïvely adding or strengthening alignment loss may offer little benefit or even harm downstream performance. The authors propose practical guidelines for combining alignment with task fine-tuning, emphasizing careful choice and weighting of losses tailored to specific languages and task types, rather than relying on generic alignment as a proxy for better transfer.

title: MANAR: Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation
summary: MANAR (Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation), contextualization layer generalizes standard multi-head attention (MHA) by instantiating the principles of Global Workspace Theory (GWT). While MHA enables unconstrained all-to-all communication, it lacks the functional bottleneck and global integration mechanisms hypothesized in cognitive models of consciousness. MANAR addresses this by implementing a central workspace through a trainable memory of abstract concepts and an Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR). The architecture follows a two-stage logic that maps directly to GWT mechanics: (i) an integration phase, where retrieved memory concepts converge to form a collective "mental image" (the ACR) based on input stimuli; and (ii) a broadcasting phase, where this global state navigates and informs the contextualization of individual local tokens. We demonstrate that efficient linear-time scaling is a fundamental architectural byproduct of instantiating GWT functional bottleneck, as routing global information through a constant-sized ACR resolves the quadratic complexity inherent in standard attention. MANAR is a compatible re-parameterization of MHA with identical semantic roles for its projections, enabling knowledge transfer from pretrained transformers via weight-copy and thus overcoming the adoption barriers of structurally incompatible linear-time alternatives. MANAR enables non-convex contextualization, synthesizing representations that provably lie outside the convex hull of input tokens - a mathematical reflection of the creative synthesis described in GWT. Empirical evaluations confirm that MANAR matches or exceeds strong baselines across language (GLUE score of 85.1), vision (83.9% ImageNet-1K), and speech (2.7% WER on LibriSpeech), positioning it as an efficient and expressive alternative to quadratic attention.

AI.tldr: MANAR is a memory-augmented attention layer inspired by Global Workspace Theory that replaces quadratic attention with a constant-sized global workspace (ACR), achieving linear-time complexity while remaining compatible with standard multi-head attention and performing competitively across language, vision, and speech tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-head attention allows all-to-all token interaction but lacks a cognitively motivated global bottleneck and suffers from quadratic complexity. Existing linear-time alternatives are often structurally incompatible with pretrained transformers or sacrifice expressivity. The authors aim to design an attention mechanism that (1) reflects Global Workspace Theory’s integration-and-broadcast dynamics, (2) achieves linear-time scaling via an explicit global workspace bottleneck, and (3) remains plug-and-play compatible with existing MHA parameterizations to leverage pretrained models.
AI.method: They introduce MANAR, a contextualization layer that augments attention with a trainable memory of abstract concepts and an Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR). The layer operates in two stages: (i) integration—input tokens retrieve and integrate concepts from memory into a global ACR, forming a collective “mental image”; (ii) broadcasting—this ACR then navigates and contextualizes each token, routing global information through a constant-sized workspace rather than pairwise interactions. The design is a re-parameterization of standard multi-head attention, preserving the semantic roles of query/key/value projections to permit direct weight copying from existing transformers. They also analyze the geometry of the resulting contextualization and show it can produce token representations outside the convex hull of inputs.
AI.result: Theoretically, the architecture yields linear-time scaling by routing all global interactions through a fixed-size ACR, eliminating the quadratic cost of all-to-all token attention. It also supports non-convex contextualization, allowing synthesized representations beyond simple convex combinations of token embeddings. Empirically, MANAR matches or surpasses strong baselines on standard benchmarks: a GLUE score of 85.1 for language tasks, 83.9% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K for vision, and 2.7% word error rate on LibriSpeech for speech recognition.
AI.conclusion: Instantiating Global Workspace Theory’s functional bottleneck within attention yields an attention mechanism with linear-time complexity, strong empirical performance, and a principled account of global integration and broadcasting. MANAR functions as a drop-in, re-parameterized replacement for multi-head attention that can inherit pretrained weights, while offering enhanced expressivity via non-convex contextualization. This makes it a practical and theoretically grounded alternative to quadratic attention in large-scale models.

title: Evaluating LLM-Generated Lessons from the Language Learning Students' Perspective: A Short Case Study on Duolingo
comment: 5 pages,3 figures,presented at the 3rd HEAL Workshop at CHI 2026
summary: Popular language learning applications such as Duolingo use large language models (LLMs) to generate lessons for its users. Most lessons focus on general real-world scenarios such as greetings, ordering food, or asking directions, with limited support for profession-specific contexts. This gap can hinder learners from achieving professional-level fluency, which we define as the ability to communicate comfortably various work-related and domain-specific information in the target language. We surveyed five employees from a multinational company in the Philippines on their experiences with Duolingo. Results show that respondents encountered general scenarios more frequently than work-related ones, and that the former are relatable and effective in building foundational grammar, vocabulary, and cultural knowledge. The latter helps bridge the gap toward professional fluency as it contains domain-specific vocabulary. Each participant suggested lesson scenarios that diverge in contexts hen analyzed in aggregate. With this understanding, we propose that language learning applications should generate lessons that adapt to an individual's needs through personalized, domain specific lesson scenarios while maintaining foundational support through general, relatable lesson scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how current language learning apps like Duolingo focus on generic scenarios and argues for adding personalized, profession-specific lessons to support professional-level fluency.
AI.motivation: Current language learning applications primarily provide lessons in generic, everyday situations (e.g., greetings, ordering food, asking directions). This design neglects the profession-specific and domain-specific communication needs of many learners, making it difficult for them to reach professional-level fluency required in their actual jobs. The authors want to understand how this gap manifests in real learner experiences and how lesson generation might be improved.
AI.method: The authors surveyed five employees from a multinational company in the Philippines about their experiences using Duolingo. They collected qualitative feedback on what types of scenarios the respondents encountered (general vs. work-related), how effective these scenarios were for learning, and what additional, profession-specific lesson scenarios the participants would like to see. They then analyzed the responses, including aggregating and comparing suggested scenarios across participants.
AI.result: The survey revealed that users mostly encountered general, everyday scenarios on Duolingo and rarely saw work-related contexts. Respondents reported that generic lessons are relatable and helpful for establishing foundational grammar, vocabulary, and cultural knowledge. However, they also said that work-related scenarios containing domain-specific vocabulary are important for bridging the gap to professional-level fluency. The suggested scenarios from different participants varied substantially, highlighting diverse, individualized professional needs.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that language learning apps should not rely solely on generic, everyday contexts. Instead, they should use LLMs or similar technologies to generate lessons that adapt to each learner’s individual professional and domain needs, while still providing widely relatable general scenarios to support foundational skills. Personalized, domain-specific scenarios are key for enabling learners to achieve professional-level fluency.

title: Accurate and Efficient Multi-Channel Time Series Forecasting via Sparse Attention Mechanism
summary:
The task of multi-channel time series forecasting is ubiquitous in numerous fields such as finance, supply chain management, and energy planning. It is critical to effectively capture complex dynamic dependencies within and between channels for accurate predictions. However, traditional method paid few attentions on learning the interaction among channels. This paper proposes Linear-Network (Li-Net), a novel architecture designed for multi-channel time series forecasting that captures the linear and non-linear dependencies among channels. Li-Net dynamically compresses representations across sequence and channel dimensions, processes the information through a configurable non-linear module and subsequently reconstructs the forecasts. Moreover, Li-Net integrates a sparse Top-K Softmax attention mechanism within a multi-scale projection framework to address these challenges. A core innovation is its ability to seamlessly incorporate and fuse multi-modal embeddings, guiding the sparse attention process to focus on the most informative time steps and feature channels. Through the experiment results on multiple real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that Li-Net achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art baseline methods. Furthermore, Li-Net provides a superior balance between prediction accuracy and computational burden, exhibiting significantly lower memory usage and faster inference times. Detailed ablation studies and parameter sensitivity analyses validate the effectiveness of each key component in our proposed architecture.
  Keywords: Multivariate Time Series Forecasting, Sparse Attention Mechanism, Multimodal Information Fusion, Non-linear relationship

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Li-Net, a neural architecture for multivariate time series forecasting that efficiently models both linear and nonlinear inter-channel dependencies using sparse attention and multi-scale projections, achieving strong accuracy with lower memory and faster inference than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multivariate time series forecasting methods struggle to effectively model complex dynamic dependencies both within each channel and across different channels, and often ignore rich interactions among channels. Moreover, many accurate models are computationally heavy, consuming substantial memory and inference time. There is a need for an architecture that (1) explicitly captures linear and nonlinear inter-channel relationships, (2) can integrate multi-modal information to guide forecasting, and (3) maintains a good trade-off between prediction accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose Li-Net, a Linear-Network architecture that works by: (1) dynamically compressing the input representations along both the temporal (sequence) and channel dimensions; (2) processing these compact representations through a configurable nonlinear module to capture complex relationships; and (3) reconstructing the final forecasts from the transformed representations. Li-Net incorporates a sparse Top-K Softmax attention mechanism inside a multi-scale projection framework, enabling the model to selectively focus on the most relevant time steps and channels across different temporal resolutions. Additionally, Li-Net supports seamless fusion of multi-modal embeddings (e.g., auxiliary features or side information), using them to guide the sparse attention so it emphasizes the most informative parts of the input.
AI.result: On multiple real-world benchmark datasets, Li-Net achieves forecasting performance that is competitive with or superior to state-of-the-art baselines while using significantly less memory and providing faster inference. Experimental evaluations include comparisons across metrics, showing Li-Net’s favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off. The authors also conduct extensive ablation studies and parameter sensitivity analyses, demonstrating the contribution of the sparse Top-K attention, multi-scale projection, nonlinear module, and multimodal fusion components to the overall performance.
AI.conclusion: Li-Net effectively addresses the challenge of modeling complex linear and nonlinear dependencies in multivariate time series while remaining computationally efficient. By combining dynamic compression, a configurable nonlinear core, sparse Top-K attention, multi-scale projections, and multimodal embedding fusion, it delivers strong forecasting accuracy with reduced resource usage. The comprehensive experiments and ablations validate that each architectural component is necessary and beneficial, suggesting Li-Net is a practical and scalable solution for multi-channel time series forecasting in real-world applications.

title: A Human-in/on-the-Loop Framework for Accessible Text Generation
comment: Accepted at LREC 2026. To appear in the Proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2026)
summary: Plain Language and Easy-to-Read formats in text simplification are essential for cognitive accessibility. Yet current automatic simplification and evaluation pipelines remain largely automated, metric-driven, and fail to reflect user comprehension or normative standards. This paper introduces a hybrid framework that explicitly integrates human participation into LLM-based accessible text generation. Human-in-the-Loop (HiTL) contributions guide adjustments during generation, while Human-on-the-Loop (HoTL) supervision ensures systematic post-generation review. Empirical evidence from user studies and annotated resources is operationalized into (i) checklists aligned with standards, (ii) Event-Condition-Action trigger rules for activating expert oversight, and (iii) accessibility Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). The framework shows how human-centered mechanisms can be encoded for evaluation and reused to provide structured feedback that improves model adaptation. By embedding the human role in both generation and supervision, it establishes a traceable, reproducible, and auditable process for creating and evaluating accessible texts. In doing so, it integrates explainability and ethical accountability as core design principles, contributing to more transparent and inclusive NLP systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a human-centered framework to guide and evaluate LLM-based text simplification for accessibility, combining automatic generation with structured human oversight and standards-based checks.
AI.motivation: Existing text simplification systems are mostly fully automated and optimized for intrinsic metrics, but they often fail to ensure that simplified texts are truly understandable, accessible, and compliant with Plain Language and Easy-to-Read standards, especially for users with cognitive disabilities. There is a need to systematically incorporate user comprehension, expert judgment, and normative accessibility standards into both the generation and evaluation of simplified texts.
AI.method: The authors design a hybrid Human-in-the-Loop (HiTL) and Human-on-the-Loop (HoTL) framework for accessible text generation with LLMs. HiTL contributions allow humans to guide or adjust the generation process itself, while HoTL provides a structured, post-hoc review stage. Empirical data from user studies and annotated resources are distilled into: (i) standards-aligned checklists for evaluating accessibility properties; (ii) Event-Condition-Action rules that trigger expert intervention when certain risk conditions are met; and (iii) accessibility KPIs that quantify performance. These components are encoded as reusable mechanisms within the pipeline to steer generation and evaluate outputs.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates that human-centered mechanisms—checklists, ECA rules, and KPIs—can be systematically encoded and reused to evaluate LLM-generated accessible texts and to provide structured feedback for model adaptation. This yields a process that is more traceable, reproducible, and auditable than purely automated pipelines, and can better align outputs with accessibility standards and user needs.
AI.conclusion: By embedding human roles in both the generation and supervision stages of LLM-based text simplification, the framework offers a principled, auditable approach to creating accessible texts. It operationalizes explainability and ethical accountability as design requirements, advancing transparency and inclusion in NLP systems concerned with cognitive accessibility and Plain Language / Easy-to-Read standards.

title: MemMA: Coordinating the Memory Cycle through Multi-Agent Reasoning and In-Situ Self-Evolution
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents maintain external memory banks to support long-horizon interaction, yet most existing systems treat construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated subroutines. This creates two coupled challenges: strategic blindness on the forward path of the memory cycle, where construction and retrieval are driven by local heuristics rather than explicit strategic reasoning, and sparse, delayed supervision on the backward path, where downstream failures rarely translate into direct repairs of the memory bank. To address these challenges, we propose MemMA, a plug-and-play multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle along both the forward and backward paths. On the forward path, a Meta-Thinker produces structured guidance that steers a Memory Manager during construction and directs a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval. On the backward path, MemMA introduces in-situ self-evolving memory construction, which synthesizes probe QA pairs, verifies the current memory, and converts failures into repair actions before the memory is finalized. Extensive experiments on LoCoMo show that MemMA consistently outperforms existing baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three different storage backends in a plug-and-play manner. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ventr1c/memma.

AI.tldr: MemMA is a multi-agent, plug-and-play framework that coordinates how LLM agents build, query, and repair external memory, leading to better long-horizon reasoning and performance across models and storage backends.
