AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented LLM agents use separate, heuristic-driven components for memory construction, retrieval, and usage. This separation causes two main issues: (1) strategic blindness, where memory is built and queried without global task-level planning; and (2) sparse, delayed supervision, where downstream task failures rarely translate into targeted fixes to the memory content. A unified, strategically guided, and self-correcting approach to memory management is needed for robust long-horizon interaction.
AI.method: The paper introduces MemMA, a coordinated multi-agent framework with three key components: (1) a Meta-Thinker that generates high-level, structured guidance for memory operations; (2) a Memory Manager that uses this guidance to strategically construct and organize the external memory bank; and (3) a Query Reasoner that performs iterative, guided retrieval from memory. Additionally, MemMA includes an in-situ self-evolving memory construction mechanism that synthesizes probe QA pairs, evaluates existing memory against them, identifies failures, and converts these into explicit repair actions before memory is finalized. The framework is designed to be plug-and-play across different LLM backbones and storage backends.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark, MemMA consistently outperforms prior memory-augmented LLM baselines. The gains hold across multiple underlying LLMs and across three distinct memory storage backends. The improvements demonstrate that coordinating the full memory cycle and adding self-evolving repair leads to better long-horizon performance and more effective memory usage.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating the entire memory cycle—construction, retrieval, and repair—via a multi-agent framework substantially improves the effectiveness of memory-augmented LLM agents. MemMA shows that explicit meta-level guidance and in-situ self-evolving memory repair can be integrated in a plug-and-play way to boost performance across models and storage systems, offering a general solution to strategic blindness and weak supervision in long-horizon memory management.

title: Progressive Training for Explainable Citation-Grounded Dialogue: Reducing Hallucination to Zero in English-Hindi LLMs
comment: 30 pages, 15 figures, 11 tables. Comprehensive study across 6 LLMs (250M-7B parameters) with explainability analysis. Code and data available upon request
summary: Knowledge-grounded dialogue systems aim to generate informative, contextually relevant responses by conditioning on external knowledge sources. However, most existing approaches focus exclusively on English, lack explicit citation mechanisms for verifying factual claims, and offer limited transparency into model decision-making. We present XKD-Dial, a progressive four-stage training pipeline for explainable, knowledge-grounded dialogue generation in a bilingual (English-Hindi) setting, comprising: (1) multilingual adaptation, (2) English dialogue SFT with citation grounding, (3) bilingual dialogue SFT, and (4) GRPO alignment with citation-aware rewards. We evaluate six models spanning encoder-decoder (250M-3B) and decoder-only (1B-7B) architectures at every pipeline stage. Our key contributions are: (i) three post-hoc explainability analyses - cross-attention alignment, Integrated Gradients attribution, and occlusion-based causal grounding - applied systematically across the training trajectory to reveal how citation behaviour is learned, not only whether it is learned; (ii) citation-grounded SFT reduces hallucination to 0.0% for encoder-decoder models from Stage 2 onward; (iii) the progressive pipeline prevents catastrophic forgetting while improving Hindi capabilities; (iv) smaller models match larger models on English after SFT; and (v) GRPO provides marginal improvement over well-designed SFT for structured citation tasks. We evaluate across six automatic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, FactScore, Citation-F1, and hallucination rate).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces XKD-Dial, a four-stage training pipeline for explainable, citation-grounded, bilingual (English-Hindi) dialogue systems, showing that citation-aware SFT can nearly eliminate hallucinations and that smaller models can match larger ones on English while improving Hindi without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge-grounded dialogue systems are mostly English-only, lack explicit citation mechanisms for verifying facts, and provide limited transparency into how models ground their responses in external knowledge. The authors want a bilingual (English-Hindi) system that not only cites sources but is also explainable in how it learns and uses citation behavior over training.
AI.method: They propose XKD-Dial, a progressive four-stage pipeline: (1) multilingual adaptation of base models; (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on English dialogue data with explicit citation grounding; (3) SFT on bilingual (English-Hindi) dialogue data; and (4) GRPO-based alignment using citation-aware reward functions. They train and evaluate six models, including encoder-decoder (250M–3B) and decoder-only (1B–7B), and apply three post-hoc explainability analyses—cross-attention alignment, Integrated Gradients, and occlusion-based causal grounding—at each stage to study how citation behavior is acquired. They measure performance with BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, FactScore, Citation-F1, and hallucination rate.
AI.result: Citation-grounded SFT from Stage 2 onward brings hallucination down to 0.0% for encoder-decoder models. The staged pipeline maintains prior abilities (avoids catastrophic forgetting) while strengthening Hindi performance. After SFT, smaller models can match larger ones on English tasks. GRPO alignment gives only marginal improvements beyond what well-designed SFT already achieves for structured citation behaviors. The explainability analyses characterize how models progressively learn to align attention and attributions with cited knowledge.
AI.conclusion: A progressive, citation-aware training pipeline can yield bilingual, knowledge-grounded dialogue systems that are both highly factual and more interpretable. Explicit citation-grounded SFT is particularly effective at eliminating hallucinations in encoder-decoder models, and careful staging allows smaller models to perform competitively and gain Hindi abilities without catastrophic forgetting. Additional GRPO alignment offers limited extra benefit for structured citation tasks beyond strong SFT.

title: Analysis Of Linguistic Stereotypes in Single and Multi-Agent Generative AI Architectures
summary: Many works in the literature show that LLM outputs exhibit discriminatory behaviour, triggering stereotype-based inferences based on the dialect in which the inputs are written. This bias has been shown to be particularly pronounced when the same inputs are provided to LLMs in Standard American English (SAE) and African-American English (AAE). In this paper, we replicate existing analyses of dialect-sensitive stereotype generation in LLM outputs and investigate the effects of mitigation strategies, including prompt engineering (role-based and Chain-Of-Thought prompting) and multi-agent architectures composed of generate-critique-revise models. We define eight prompt templates to analyse different ways in which dialect bias can manifest, such as suggested names, jobs, and adjectives for SAE or AAE speakers. We use an LLM-as-judge approach to evaluate the bias in the results. Our results show that stereotype-bearing differences emerge between SAE- and AAE-related outputs across all template categories, with the strongest effects observed in adjective and job attribution. Baseline disparities vary substantially by model, with the largest SAE-AAE differential observed in Claude Haiku and the smallest in Phi-4 Mini. Chain-Of-Thought prompting proved to be an effective mitigation strategy for Claude Haiku, whereas the use of a multi-agent architecture ensured consistent mitigation across all the models. These findings suggest that for intersectionality-informed software engineering, fairness evaluation should include model-specific validation of mitigation strategies, and workflow-level controls (e.g., agentic architectures involving critique models) in high-impact LLM deployments. The current results are exploratory in nature and limited in scope, but can lead to extensions and replications by increasing the dataset size and applying the procedure to different languages or dialects.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models (LLMs) show different stereotype-laden outputs depending on whether input text is in Standard American English (SAE) or African-American English (AAE), and evaluates mitigation methods such as prompt engineering and multi-agent critique-revise workflows.
AI.motivation: Prior work shows LLMs often respond differently to inputs written in different dialects, especially SAE vs AAE, leading to discriminatory and stereotype-based outputs. This is problematic for fairness, especially in software systems that rely on LLMs and must work equitably across dialects. The authors aim to systematically replicate prior findings, quantify dialect-sensitive bias across several prompt types and models, and assess practical mitigation strategies that practitioners could adopt.
AI.method: The authors: (1) construct eight prompt templates reflecting various possible stereotype-bearing outputs (e.g., suggesting names, jobs, adjectives) for speakers using SAE or AAE; (2) query multiple LLMs with SAE vs AAE variants of the same prompts; (3) use an LLM-as-judge framework to evaluate bias by comparing the outputs associated with each dialect; and (4) test mitigation approaches including role-based prompting, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, and multi-agent generate–critique–revise pipelines, measuring how each affects SAE–AAE disparities across models and template categories.
AI.result: Across all prompt categories, the models show dialect-sensitive differences, with more pronounced stereotype-laden patterns for AAE than SAE. Adjective and job attribution prompts exhibit the strongest disparities. The magnitude and direction of bias vary substantially across models: Claude Haiku shows the largest SAE–AAE differential, while Phi-4 Mini shows the smallest. Chain-of-Thought prompting substantially reduces disparity for Claude Haiku, and multi-agent generate–critique–revise architectures provide robust, consistent mitigation across all evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit measurable dialect-based stereotyping between SAE and AAE across multiple prompt types and models, confirming and extending prior evidence of dialect bias. Mitigation effectiveness is highly model-specific: some biases can be reduced via prompt engineering (e.g., CoT), but more reliable reductions come from workflow-level controls such as multi-agent critique–revise pipelines. For intersectionality-informed software engineering, fairness evaluations should be model- and workflow-aware, validating mitigation strategies per model and incorporating agentic architectures in high-impact applications. The study is exploratory and limited in scale but provides a framework that can be expanded with larger datasets and additional dialects or languages.

title: Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents
summary:
We introduce \emph{Memento-Skills}, a generalist, continually-learnable LLM agent system that functions as an \emph{agent-designing agent}: it autonomously constructs, adapts, and improves task-specific agents through experience. The system is built on a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with \emph{stateful prompts}, where reusable skills (stored as structured markdown files) serve as persistent, evolving memory. These skills encode both behaviour and context, enabling the agent to carry forward knowledge across interactions.
  Starting from simple elementary skills (like Web search and terminal operations), the agent continually improves via the \emph{Read--Write Reflective Learning} mechanism introduced in \emph{Memento~2}~\cite{wang2025memento2}. In the \emph{read} phase, a behaviour-trainable skill router selects the most relevant skill conditioned on the current stateful prompt; in the \emph{write} phase, the agent updates and expands its skill library based on new experience. This closed-loop design enables \emph{continual learning without updating LLM parameters}, as all adaptation is realised through the evolution of externalised skills and prompts.
  Unlike prior approaches that rely on human-designed agents, Memento-Skills enables a generalist agent to \emph{design agents end-to-end} for new tasks. Through iterative skill generation and refinement, the system progressively improves its own capabilities. Experiments on the \emph{General AI Assistants} benchmark and \emph{Humanity's Last Exam} demonstrate sustained gains, achieving 26.2\% and 116.2\% relative improvements in overall accuracy, respectively. Code is available at https://github.com/Memento-Teams/Memento-Skills.

AI.tldr: Memento-Skills is a memory-based, continually-learnable LLM agent that designs and improves specialized sub-agents by evolving external skill files rather than changing model weights.
AI.motivation: Most agentic LLM systems rely on static, human-designed agent architectures and require model fine-tuning or parameter updates to improve, which is costly and inflexible. The authors want a generalist agent that can autonomously design, adapt, and improve task-specific agents over time, while avoiding direct LLM retraining and leveraging persistent memories across tasks.
AI.method: They build a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with stateful prompts and a library of reusable skills stored as structured markdown files. Each skill encodes behavior (how to act, tools to use, decision policies) plus contextual knowledge. The agent uses the Read–Write Reflective Learning mechanism: in the read phase, a trainable skill router chooses the most relevant skill based on the current stateful prompt; in the write phase, the agent updates or creates new skills from recent experience, thus expanding and refining its skill library. This closed loop enables continual adaptation via externalized skills and prompt evolution instead of changing LLM parameters.
AI.result: On the General AI Assistants benchmark and Humanity's Last Exam, the system shows sustained performance improvements as it learns, with 26.2% and 116.2% relative gains in overall accuracy, respectively, compared to its initial or baseline performance.
AI.conclusion: External, skill-based memory combined with a read–write reflective learning loop allows an LLM agent to act as an agent-designing agent, continually constructing and refining task-specific agents without modifying its underlying parameters, yielding substantial and sustained performance gains on challenging benchmarks.

title: RADIUS: Ranking, Distribution, and Significance - A Comprehensive Alignment Suite for Survey Simulation
summary: Simulation of surveys using LLMs is emerging as a powerful application for generating human-like responses at scale. Prior work evaluates survey simulation using metrics borrowed from other domains, which are often ad hoc, fragmented, and non-standardized, leading to results that are difficult to compare. Moreover, existing metrics focus mainly on accuracy or distributional measures, overlooking the critical dimension of ranking alignment. In practice, a simulation can achieve high accuracy while still failing to capture the option most preferred by humans - a distinction that is critical in decision-making applications. We introduce RADIUS, a comprehensive two-dimensional alignment suite for survey simulation that captures: 1) RAnking alignment and 2) DIstribUtion alignment, each complemented by statistical Significance testing. RADIUS highlights the limitations of existing metrics, enables more meaningful evaluation of survey simulation, and provides an open-source implementation for reproducible and comparable assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RADIUS, a standardized evaluation suite for LLM-based survey simulation that jointly measures ranking and distribution alignment with humans, along with significance testing.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM-based survey simulations rely on ad hoc, non-standard metrics mostly focused on accuracy or marginal distributions. These metrics are hard to compare across studies and miss whether models correctly identify the most preferred option, which is crucial for decision-making use cases.
AI.method: The authors design RADIUS, a two-dimensional alignment framework for survey simulation. It separately measures (1) ranking alignment—how well model-simulated preference orderings match human rankings—and (2) distribution alignment—how closely simulated response distributions mirror human response distributions. Both dimensions are paired with appropriate statistical significance tests and implemented in an open-source toolkit.
AI.result: Using RADIUS, the authors show that some LLM survey simulations can look strong on traditional accuracy or distributional metrics yet still fail to match human-preferred rankings, revealing important failure modes that previous metrics overlook.
AI.conclusion: RADIUS provides a more comprehensive, standardized, and statistically grounded way to evaluate LLM survey simulations by jointly assessing ranking and distribution alignment. This framework exposes limitations of prior metrics and supports reproducible, comparable evaluation across studies via an open-source implementation.

title: NeuroGame Transformer: Gibbs-Inspired Attention Driven by Game Theory and Statistical Physics
comment: This work has been submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics for possible publication
summary: Standard attention mechanisms in transformers are limited by their pairwise formulation, which hinders the modeling of higher-order dependencies among tokens. We introduce the NeuroGame Transformer (NGT) to overcome this by reconceptualizing attention through a dual perspective: tokens are treated simultaneously as players in a cooperative game and as interacting spins in a statistical physics system. Token importance is quantified using two complementary game-theoretic concepts -- Shapley values for global, permutation-based attribution and Banzhaf indices for local, coalition-level influence. These are combined via a learnable gating parameter to form an external magnetic field, while pairwise interaction potentials capture synergistic relationships. The system's energy follows an Ising Hamiltonian, with attention weights emerging as marginal probabilities under the Gibbs distribution, efficiently computed via mean-field equations. To ensure scalability despite the exponential coalition space, we develop importance-weighted Monte Carlo estimators with Gibbs-distributed weights. This approach avoids explicit exponential factors, ensuring numerical stability for long sequences. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees and characterize the fairness-sensitivity trade-off governed by the interpolation parameter. Experimental results demonstrate that the NeuroGame Transformer achieves strong performance across SNLI, and MNLI-matched, outperforming some major efficient transformer baselines. On SNLI, it attains a test accuracy of 86.4\% (with a peak validation accuracy of 86.6\%), surpassing ALBERT-Base and remaining highly competitive with RoBERTa-Base. Code is available at https://github.com/dbouchaffra/NeuroGame-Transformer.

AI.tldr: NeuroGame Transformer (NGT) redefines attention by combining game theory (Shapley, Banzhaf) and statistical physics (Ising model) to model higher‑order token dependencies efficiently, yielding competitive or better performance than strong baselines on NLI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard transformer attention is pairwise and struggles to directly represent higher-order token interactions and fair token importance attribution, especially for long sequences where naive modeling of coalitions is exponentially expensive and numerically unstable.
AI.method: Model tokens simultaneously as players in a cooperative game and spins in an Ising system. Use Shapley values for global token attribution and Banzhaf indices for local coalition influence, combine them via a learned gating parameter to define an external magnetic field, and model pairwise token interactions as Ising couplings. Derive attention weights as marginal probabilities under a Gibbs distribution, approximated efficiently via mean-field equations. To handle the exponential coalition space, employ importance-weighted Monte Carlo estimators with Gibbs-distributed weights and provide theoretical convergence analysis and a characterization of the fairness-sensitivity trade-off controlled by the interpolation parameter.
AI.result: NeuroGame Transformer achieves strong empirical performance on natural language inference tasks (SNLI and MNLI-matched), surpassing some efficient transformer baselines like ALBERT-Base and approaching or matching RoBERTa-Base, with SNLI test accuracy of 86.4% and peak validation accuracy of 86.6%.
AI.conclusion: Recasting attention as a cooperative game plus Ising system enables principled, higher-order and fairness-aware token importance modeling while remaining scalable via mean-field and importance-weighted Monte Carlo estimation. This yields practical performance gains and demonstrates that game-theoretic and statistical-physics perspectives can improve transformer attention design for long-sequence NLP tasks.

title: Hypothesis-Conditioned Query Rewriting for Decision-Useful Retrieval
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves Large Language Models (LLMs) by grounding generation in external, non-parametric knowledge. However, when a task requires choosing among competing options, simply grounding generation in broadly relevant context is often insufficient to drive the final decision. Existing RAG methods typically rely on a single initial query, which often favors topical relevance over decision-relevant evidence, and therefore retrieves background information that can fail to discriminate among answer options. To address this issue, here we propose Hypothesis-Conditioned Query Rewriting (HCQR), a training-free pre-retrieval framework that reorients RAG from topic-oriented retrieval to evidence-oriented retrieval. HCQR first derives a lightweight working hypothesis from the input question and candidate options, and then rewrites retrieval into three targeted queries that seek evidence to: (1) support the hypothesis, (2) distinguish it from competing alternatives, and (3) verify salient clues in the question. This approach enables context retrieval that is more directly aligned with answer selection, allowing the generator to confirm or overturn the initial hypothesis based on the retrieved evidence. Experiments on MedQA and MMLU-Med show that HCQR consistently outperforms single-query RAG and re-rank/filter baselines, improving average accuracy over Simple RAG by 5.9 and 3.6 points, respectively. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HCQR-1C2E.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hypothesis-Conditioned Query Rewriting (HCQR), a training-free pre-retrieval framework that improves RAG for decision-making tasks by generating hypothesis-driven, evidence-oriented queries, leading to more discriminative context retrieval and better answer accuracy on medical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG pipelines usually issue a single initial retrieval query derived from the question. This tends to prioritize general topical relevance rather than evidence that truly discriminates between candidate answers, which is especially problematic in tasks that involve choosing among competing options (e.g., multiple-choice questions in specialized domains like medicine). The authors are motivated to make retrieval more decision- and evidence-focused so LLMs can select answers based on targeted supporting and contrasting information rather than broad background context.
AI.method: The authors propose Hypothesis-Conditioned Query Rewriting (HCQR), a training-free pre-retrieval framework that operates before the standard RAG retrieval step. Given a question and its candidate options, HCQR: (1) induces a lightweight initial “working hypothesis” about the likely correct answer; (2) rewrites the retrieval process into three specialized queries aimed at different evidential roles: a query to find evidence supporting the working hypothesis, a query to find evidence distinguishing the hypothesis from alternative options, and a query to verify key clues contained in the question itself. These three queries are then used to retrieve multiple, complementary contexts that are fed to the generator LLM, which can then either confirm or overturn the initial hypothesis in light of the gathered evidence. The framework does not require additional training and is applied as a drop-in pre-retrieval module on top of existing RAG setups.
AI.result: On two medical multiple-choice benchmarks, MedQA and MMLU-Med, HCQR systematically outperforms standard single-query RAG and stronger baselines that use re-ranking or filtering of retrieved passages. Relative to a Simple RAG baseline, HCQR yields average accuracy gains of 5.9 percentage points on MedQA and 3.6 percentage points on MMLU-Med, indicating more effective use of retrieved knowledge for answer selection in complex, evidence-heavy domains.
AI.conclusion: HCQR demonstrates that restructuring retrieval around a hypothesis and explicit evidential roles—supporting, distinguishing, and verifying—can substantially improve RAG performance on decision-centric tasks without any additional training. By shifting from topic-oriented to evidence-oriented retrieval and providing the generator with more discriminative context, HCQR enables LLMs to make more accurate, evidence-grounded choices. The approach is simple, training-free, and broadly compatible with existing RAG pipelines, suggesting its utility as a general enhancement for multi-option question-answering in knowledge-intensive domains such as medicine.

title: A Concept is More Than a Word: Diversified Unlearning in Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
summary: Concept unlearning has emerged as a promising direction for reducing the risks of harmful content generation in text-to-image diffusion models by selectively erasing undesirable concepts from a model's parameters. Existing approaches typically rely on keywords to identify the target concept to be unlearned. However, we show that this keyword-based formulation is inherently limited: a visual concept is multi-dimensional, can be expressed in diverse textual forms, and often overlap with related concepts in the latent space, making keyword-only unlearning, which imprecisely indicate the target concept is brittle and prone to over-forgetting. This occurs because a single keyword represents only a narrow point estimate of the concept, failing to cover its full semantic distribution and entangled variations in the latent space. To address this limitation, we propose Diversified Unlearning, a distributional framework that represents a concept through a set of contextually diverse prompts rather than a single keyword. This richer representation enables more precise and robust unlearning. Through extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and state-of-the-art baselines, we demonstrate that integrating Diversified Unlearning as an add-on component into existing unlearning pipelines consistently achieves stronger erasure, better retention of unrelated concepts, and improved robustness against adversarial recovery attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new framework, Diversified Unlearning, to more robustly remove unwanted concepts from text-to-image diffusion models by using many diverse prompts instead of a single keyword.
AI.motivation: Keyword-based concept unlearning in diffusion models is brittle because visual concepts are multi-dimensional, expressed via many textual variants, and entangled in latent space, leading to incomplete removal and over-forgetting of related but desired content. There is a need for a more expressive representation of the target concept to be unlearned.
AI.method: They introduce Diversified Unlearning, a distributional framework that models each concept with a set of contextually diverse prompts instead of one keyword. This set better approximates the semantic distribution of the concept in latent space and is plugged in as an add-on module to existing unlearning pipelines to guide parameter updates during unlearning.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and against state-of-the-art baselines, integrating Diversified Unlearning into standard unlearning procedures yields stronger removal of the target concept, less damage to unrelated concepts, and higher resistance to adversarial attempts to recover the erased concept.
AI.conclusion: Representing concepts via a distribution of diverse textual prompts rather than a single keyword enables more precise and robust concept unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models, mitigating over-forgetting and improving safety and reliability of content moderation in generative systems.

title: What Really Controls Temporal Reasoning in Large Language Models: Tokenisation or Representation of Time?
summary: We present MultiTempBench, a multilingual temporal reasoning benchmark spanning three tasks, date arithmetic, time zone conversion, and temporal relation extraction across five languages (English, German, Chinese, Arabic, and Hausa) and multiple calendar conventions (Gregorian, Hijri, and Chinese Lunar). MultiTempBench contains $15,000$ examples built by translating $750$ curated English questions and expanding each into controlled date-format variants. We evaluate 20 LLMs and introduce the multilingual Date Fragmentation Ratio (mDFR), calibrated with human severity ratings, together with geometric-probing analyses of internal temporal representations. We find tokenisation quality of temporal artefacts is a resource-dependent bottleneck: in low-resource languages and rarer calendar formats, fragmentation disrupts Year/Month/Day separation and accuracy collapses, while high-resource settings are often robust to digit-level splitting. Beyond tokenisation, crossed mixed-effects regression shows that temporal linearity is the strongest predictor of temporal reasoning in high-resource languages, whereas fragmentation is the stronger predictor in low-resource languages. Code is available at: https://github.com/gagan3012/mtb

AI.tldr: MultiTempBench is a multilingual benchmark for testing LLM temporal reasoning across languages, calendars, and tasks, revealing how tokenization and temporal structure affect performance.
AI.motivation: Existing temporal reasoning benchmarks are mostly monolingual, focus on the Gregorian calendar, and under-represent low-resource languages and alternative calendars. This makes it hard to understand how LLMs handle time-related tasks in realistic, global settings, and to diagnose failure modes tied to tokenization and internal temporal representations. The paper aims to fill this gap with a controlled, multilingual, multi-calendar evaluation suite and analytic tools.
AI.method: The authors build MultiTempBench, a benchmark of 15,000 examples derived from 750 carefully designed English temporal questions, which are translated into four additional languages and systematically varied by date format. The tasks include date arithmetic, time zone conversion, and temporal relation extraction, and cover Gregorian, Hijri, and Chinese Lunar calendars. They evaluate 20 large language models on this benchmark. To analyze failure modes, they introduce a multilingual Date Fragmentation Ratio (mDFR) that quantifies how tokenization splits temporal expressions, calibrate it with human-rated severity of fragmentation, and conduct geometric probing to study internal temporal representations. They additionally apply crossed mixed-effects regression to disentangle the contributions of temporal linearity and tokenization fragmentation to model performance across resource levels and languages.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that tokenization quality for temporal expressions is a key bottleneck, especially in low-resource languages and less common calendar formats. In these settings, heavy fragmentation of date tokens disrupts clean representation of year, month, and day, leading to drastic drops in reasoning accuracy. In contrast, high-resource languages and common formats show strong robustness even when digits are split across tokens. Regression analyses indicate that in high-resource languages, a model’s temporal linearity—how well its internal representations encode ordered temporal structure—is the main predictor of temporal reasoning performance. In low-resource languages, however, the degree of tokenization fragmentation is more predictive of success or failure.
AI.conclusion: MultiTempBench exposes how multilingual and multi-calendar temporal reasoning in LLMs is constrained by both tokenization and internal temporal structure. It demonstrates that resource level and calendar/language-specific tokenization strongly shape performance, with fragmentation critically harming low-resource scenarios. The findings suggest that improving tokenization for temporal artefacts, especially in under-resourced languages and alternative calendars, and enhancing temporally linear internal representations are key paths toward more reliable, globally applicable temporal reasoning in LLMs. The released benchmark and code provide a standard platform for future modeling and tokenization advances.

title: Proceedings of the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind
summary: This volume includes a selection of papers presented at the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind held at AAAI 2026 in Singapore on 26th January 2026. The purpose of this volume is to provide an open access and curated anthology for the ToM and AI research community.

AI.tldr: Anthology of selected papers from the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind (ToM) at AAAI 2026.
AI.motivation: To create an openly accessible, curated collection of current research at the intersection of Artificial Intelligence and Theory of Mind, fostering community building and knowledge sharing.
AI.method: Editorial selection and compilation of workshop papers from the 2nd ToM & AI workshop at AAAI 2026 into a single volume.
AI.result: A published open-access volume that aggregates a subset of the most relevant or representative workshop contributions for the ToM and AI community.
AI.conclusion: This volume serves as a curated resource and reference point for ongoing and future research on integrating Theory of Mind into Artificial Intelligence, strengthening collaboration and dissemination in this area.

title: MoRI: Learning Motivation-Grounded Reasoning for Scientific Ideation in Large Language Models
summary: Scientific ideation aims to propose novel solutions within a given scientific context. Existing LLM-based agentic approaches emulate human research workflows, yet inadequately model scientific reasoning, resulting in surface-level conceptual recombinations that lack technical depth and scientific grounding. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{MoRI} (\textbf{Mo}tivation-grounded \textbf{R}easoning for Scientific \textbf{I}deation), a framework that enables LLMs to explicitly learn the reasoning process from research motivations to methodologies. The base LLM is initialized via supervised fine-tuning to generate a research motivation from a given context, and is subsequently trained under a composite reinforcement learning reward that approximates scientific rigor: (1) entropy-aware information gain encourages the model to uncover and elaborate high-complexity technical details grounded in ground-truth methodologies, and (2) contrastive semantic gain constrains the reasoning trajectory to maintain conceptually aligned with scientifically valid solutions. Empirical results show that MoRI significantly outperforms strong commercial LLMs and complex agentic baselines across multiple dimensions, including novelty, technical rigor, and feasibility. The code will be made available on \href{https://github.com/ECNU-Text-Computing/IdeaGeneration}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: MoRI is an LLM training framework that teaches models to reason from research motivations to concrete methodologies, using reinforcement learning rewards tailored to scientific rigor, yielding better, more grounded scientific ideas than strong baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs used for scientific ideation often mimic human workflows but lack genuine scientific reasoning, leading to shallow, combinatorial ideas that are not technically deep or rigorously grounded. There is a need for a method that explicitly structures and improves the reasoning from recognizing research gaps (motivations) to proposing feasible, scientifically sound methods.
AI.method: MoRI first performs supervised fine-tuning so the base LLM can generate coherent research motivations from scientific context. Then it applies reinforcement learning with a composite reward designed to approximate scientific rigor: (1) an entropy-aware information gain term that pushes the model to explore and elaborate high-complexity, technically detailed content that remains anchored to ground-truth methodologies; and (2) a contrastive semantic gain term that keeps the reasoning trajectory conceptually close to scientifically valid solutions while discouraging drift into invalid or irrelevant directions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate that MoRI produces research ideas that score higher than strong commercial LLMs and sophisticated agentic baselines along several key axes: novelty of ideas, technical rigor and depth, and practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the reasoning path from research motivation to methodology and optimizing it with scientifically oriented RL rewards enables LLMs to generate more novel, rigorous, and feasible scientific ideas. MoRI offers a principled framework for improving scientific ideation beyond surface-level concept recombination, and the released code will facilitate further research in this direction.

title: dTRPO: Trajectory Reduction in Policy Optimization of Diffusion Large Language Models
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) introduce a new paradigm for language generation, which in turn presents new challenges for aligning them with human preferences. In this work, we aim to improve the policy optimization for dLLMs by reducing the cost of the trajectory probability calculation, thereby enabling scaled-up offline policy training. We prove that: (i) under reference policy regularization, the probability ratio of the newly unmasked tokens is an unbiased estimate of that of intermediate diffusion states, and (ii) the probability of the full trajectory can be effectively estimated with a single forward pass of a re-masked final state. By integrating these two trajectory reduction strategies into a policy optimization objective, we propose Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization (dTRPO). We evaluate dTRPO on 7B dLLMs across instruction-following and reasoning benchmarks. Results show that it substantially improves the core performance of state-of-the-art dLLMs, achieving gains of up to 9.6% on STEM tasks, up to 4.3% on coding tasks, and up to 3.0% on instruction-following tasks. Moreover, dTRPO exhibits strong training efficiency due to its offline, single-forward nature, and achieves improved generation efficiency through high-quality outputs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization (dTRPO), a sample- and compute-efficient offline alignment method for diffusion LLMs that approximates full diffusion trajectories using only newly unmasked tokens and a single re-masked final state.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Large Language Models require computing probabilities over long diffusion trajectories for policy optimization, which is computationally expensive and limits scalable offline alignment with human feedback or preference data. The authors aim to reduce this cost while preserving the correctness of the optimization objective so that large dLLMs can be better aligned and trained more efficiently.
AI.method: The authors derive two key theoretical results under reference policy regularization: (1) the probability ratio for the newly unmasked tokens at each step is an unbiased estimator of the ratio over the corresponding intermediate diffusion state, and (2) the probability of the entire diffusion trajectory can be estimated from a single forward pass on a re-masked final state. Using these, they construct Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization (dTRPO), an offline policy optimization objective that avoids explicit rollout of the full diffusion trajectory and instead uses reduced statistics that can be computed in one forward pass of the model.
AI.result: On 7B-parameter diffusion LLMs, dTRPO significantly boosts performance on multiple benchmarks: up to 9.6% improvement on STEM reasoning tasks, up to 4.3% on coding tasks, and up to 3.0% on general instruction-following benchmarks compared with state-of-the-art dLLMs. It also demonstrates strong training efficiency due to its offline, single-forward-pass design and leads to faster or more efficient generation thanks to higher-quality outputs requiring fewer attempts or shorter sampling.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization offers an effective way to align diffusion LLMs with human preferences by making trajectory probability estimation much cheaper while remaining theoretically sound. This enables scalable offline policy training and yields consistent performance gains across reasoning, coding, and instruction-following tasks, suggesting that trajectory-reduced objectives are a promising direction for training and aligning diffusion-based language models.

title: Parallelograms Strike Back: LLMs Generate Better Analogies than People
summary: Four-term word analogies (A:B::C:D) are classically modeled geometrically as ''parallelograms,'' yet recent work suggests this model poorly captures how humans produce analogies, with simple local-similarity heuristics often providing a better account (Peterson et al., 2020). But does the parallelogram model fail because it is a bad model of analogical relations, or because people are not very good at generating relation-preserving analogies? We compared human and large language model (LLM) analogy completions on the same set of analogy problems from (Peterson et al., 2020). We find that LLM-generated analogies are reliably judged as better than human-generated ones, and are also more closely aligned with the parallelogram structure in a distributional embedding space (GloVe). Crucially, we show that the improvement over human analogies was driven by greater parallelogram alignment and reduced reliance on accessible words rather than enhanced sensitivity to local similarity. Moreover, the LLM advantage is driven not by uniformly superior responses by LLMs, but by humans producing a long tail of weak completions: when only modal (most frequent) responses by both systems are compared, the LLM advantage disappears. However, greater parallelogram alignment and lower word frequency continue to predict which LLM completions are rated higher than those of humans. Overall, these results suggest that the parallelogram model is not a poor account of word analogy. Rather, humans may often fail to produce completions that satisfy this relational constraint, whereas LLMs do so more consistently.

AI.tldr: Paper tests whether the classic geometrical “parallelogram” model of word analogies is bad, or whether humans are just bad at generating relation-preserving analogies, by comparing humans and LLMs on the same analogy tasks.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed that simple local-similarity heuristics often explain human four-term word analogy responses better than the classic parallelogram model, suggesting the model might be wrong. However, it was unclear whether the problem lies in the theoretical model of analogical relations or in human performance limitations when producing analogies. With LLMs now able to generate fluent analogies, they provide an opportunity to test how well the parallelogram model can work when the generator is not constrained by human retrieval biases and vocabulary accessibility.
AI.method: Use four-term word analogy completion tasks (A:B::C:?) from Peterson et al. (2020) and collect completions from humans and from a large language model. Evaluate: (1) human vs LLM completions via human quality judgments, (2) alignment of each completion with the parallelogram structure in a distributional semantic space (GloVe embeddings), and (3) reliance on local similarity and on high-frequency, easily accessible words. Additionally, compare full response distributions vs just modal (most frequent) responses from both humans and LLMs to see where the advantage originates.
AI.result: LLM-produced analogy completions are judged by humans to be better than human-produced completions overall. LLM completions also show stronger alignment with the parallelogram geometry in GloVe space and rely less on highly frequent, easily accessible words, without showing increased dependence on simple local-similarity heuristics. The overall LLM advantage disappears when comparing only the modal responses of humans and LLMs, indicating that the superiority comes from avoiding the long tail of poor human responses. Within LLM outputs, stronger parallelogram alignment and lower word frequency predict higher-rated completions compared with human ones.
AI.conclusion: The classic parallelogram model is not inherently a poor model of analogical relations in word embeddings. Instead, humans often fail to produce completions that satisfy its relational constraints, due in part to retrieval and accessibility biases, whereas LLMs more consistently generate parallelogram-aligned, lower-frequency analogy completions. This suggests that the model remains a useful account of analogy structure and that observed deviations in human data may partly reflect human performance limitations rather than deep flaws in the geometrical model itself.

title: Can LLM generate interesting mathematical research problems?
summary: This paper is the second one in a series of work on the mathematical creativity of LLM. In the first paper, the authors proposed three criteria for evaluating the mathematical creativity of LLM and constructed a benchmark dataset to measure it. This paper further explores the mathematical creativity of LLM, with a focus on investigating whether LLM can generate valuable and cutting-edge mathematical research problems. We develop an agent to generate unknown problems and produced 665 research problems in differential geometry. Through human verification, we find that many of these mathematical problems are unknown to experts and possess unique research value.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) can generate genuinely new and valuable research problems in differential geometry, extending prior work on mathematical creativity benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Previous work by the authors defined criteria and benchmarks for assessing mathematical creativity in LLMs, but left open the deeper question of whether LLMs can originate truly novel and valuable research directions rather than just solving or slightly varying known problems. This paper aims to address that question, which is important for understanding if LLMs can contribute to frontier mathematical research.
AI.method: The authors design an LLM-based agent specialized for problem generation in differential geometry. This agent is prompted and guided to produce research-level problems that are intended to be unknown, rather than exercises or minor variants of known results. They then systematically generate a collection of 665 such problems. Human experts in differential geometry review the problems to check novelty (unknown to the experts and literature) and to assess whether they have genuine research potential and nontrivial mathematical value.
AI.result: The agent successfully generates 665 research problems in differential geometry. Expert evaluation reveals that a substantial portion of these problems are not recognized as known in the existing literature by the experts and are judged to have distinctive research interest and nontrivial mathematical challenges, rather than being trivial, ill-posed, or redundant with standard questions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, when structured as agents with appropriate prompting, can produce a significant number of research problems in a specialized mathematical field that are considered novel and valuable by human experts. This suggests that LLMs have emerging potential not only to assist in solving problems but also to contribute creatively to the formulation of new lines of mathematical inquiry, at least in the domain of differential geometry explored in this work.

title: A Dataset and Resources for Identifying Patient Health Literacy Information from Clinical Notes
summary: Health literacy is a critical determinant of patient outcomes, yet current screening tools are not always feasible and differ considerably in the number of items, question format, and dimensions of health literacy they capture, making documentation in structured electronic health records difficult to achieve. Automated detection from unstructured clinical notes offers a promising alternative, as these notes often contain richer, more contextual health literacy information, but progress has been limited by the lack of annotated resources. We introduce HEALIX, the first publicly available annotated health literacy dataset derived from real clinical notes, curated through a combination of social worker note sampling, keyword-based filtering, and LLM-based active learning. HEALIX contains 589 notes across 9 note types, annotated with three health literacy labels: low, normal, and high. To demonstrate its utility, we benchmarked zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies across four open source large language models (LLMs).

AI.tldr: Introduces HEALIX, the first publicly available, clinically derived, annotated health literacy dataset and benchmarks LLMs on automated health literacy detection.
AI.motivation: Existing health literacy screening tools are heterogeneous, time-consuming, and poorly integrated into structured EHRs. Unstructured clinical notes contain rich information about patients’ health literacy, but lack of annotated datasets has limited automated detection methods.
AI.method: Curate a dataset of real clinical notes using social worker note sampling, keyword-based filtering, and LLM-based active learning; manually annotate 589 notes from 9 note types with three health literacy levels (low, normal, high); evaluate zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies on four open-source LLMs for health literacy classification.
AI.result: Produced HEALIX, a labeled corpus of 589 clinical notes spanning 9 note types with three health literacy labels. Established baseline performance by benchmarking multiple open-source LLMs under zero-shot and few-shot prompting conditions.
AI.conclusion: HEALIX provides the first public, clinically grounded resource for automated health literacy detection from clinical notes, enabling systematic benchmarking and future model development to better capture and document health literacy in EHRs.

title: ProRL Agent: Rollout-as-a-Service for RL Training of Multi-Turn LLM Agents
summary: Multi-turn LLM agents are increasingly important for solving complex, interactive tasks, and reinforcement learning (RL) is a key ingredient for improving their long-horizon behavior. However, RL training requires generating large numbers of sandboxed rollout trajectories, and existing infrastructures often couple rollout orchestration with the training loop, making systems hard to migrate and maintain. Under the rollout-as-a-service philosophy, we present ProRL Agent , a scalable infrastructure that serves the full agentic rollout lifecycle through an API service. ProRL Agent also provides standardized and extensible sandbox environments that support diverse agentic tasks in rootless HPC settings. We validate ProRL Agent through RL training on software engineering, math, STEM, and coding tasks. ProRL Agent is open-sourced and integrated as part of NVIDIA NeMo Gym.

AI.tldr: ProRL Agent is an API-based, scalable rollout-as-a-service infrastructure for training multi-turn LLM agents with reinforcement learning across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Training multi-turn LLM agents with RL for complex, long-horizon tasks requires generating many sandboxed rollout trajectories. Existing systems tightly couple rollout orchestration with the RL training loop, making them hard to migrate, scale, and maintain, especially in HPC or heterogeneous environments. A modular, service-based rollout system can decouple these concerns and standardize environments.
AI.method: They design ProRL Agent as an API-driven service that manages the full lifecycle of agentic rollouts: task definition, sandboxed execution, data collection, and interaction with RL training loops. The system offers standardized, extensible sandbox environments suitable for various agentic tasks (software engineering, math, STEM, coding) and is deployable in rootless HPC settings. It is integrated into NVIDIA NeMo Gym to plug into existing RL workflows.
AI.result: Using ProRL Agent, they successfully conduct RL training for multi-turn LLM agents on software engineering, math, STEM, and coding benchmarks, demonstrating that the infrastructure can support diverse and complex agentic tasks at scale. The experiments validate that the service can generate the required trajectories and integrate with RL training loops in practice.
AI.conclusion: ProRL Agent provides a scalable, decoupled rollout-as-a-service infrastructure for RL training of multi-turn LLM agents, with standardized sandbox environments and compatibility with rootless HPC setups. Its open-source integration with NVIDIA NeMo Gym suggests it can serve as a general foundation for building and evaluating RL-based agentic systems across multiple domains.

title: DaPT: A Dual-Path Framework for Multilingual Multi-hop Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems have made significant progress in solving complex multi-hop question answering (QA) tasks in the English scenario. However, RAG systems inevitably face the application scenario of retrieving across multilingual corpora and queries, leaving several open challenges. The first one involves the absence of benchmarks that assess RAG systems' capabilities under the multilingual multi-hop (MM-hop) QA setting. The second centers on the overreliance on LLMs' strong semantic understanding in English, which diminishes effectiveness in multilingual scenarios. To address these challenges, we first construct multilingual multi-hop QA benchmarks by translating English-only benchmarks into five languages, and then we propose DaPT, a novel multilingual RAG framework. DaPT generates sub-question graphs in parallel for both the source-language query and its English translation counterpart, then merges them before employing a bilingual retrieval-and-answer strategy to sequentially solve sub-questions. Our experimental results demonstrate that advanced RAG systems suffer from a significant performance imbalance in multilingual scenarios. Furthermore, our proposed method consistently yields more accurate and concise answers compared to the baselines, significantly enhancing RAG performance on this task. For instance, on the most challenging MuSiQue benchmark, DaPT achieves a relative improvement of 18.3\% in average EM score over the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper targets multilingual multi-hop question answering for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and proposes a new benchmark and framework (DaPT) that improves performance across languages.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG systems and benchmarks mainly focus on English, yet real-world applications involve queries and corpora in multiple languages. There is no proper benchmark for multilingual multi-hop QA, and current methods rely heavily on English-centric semantic understanding, leading to degraded performance in multilingual settings. The authors want to systematically evaluate and improve RAG for multilingual multi-hop reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Construct multilingual multi-hop QA benchmarks by translating existing English-only multi-hop QA datasets into five additional languages. 2) Propose DaPT, a multilingual RAG framework that: (a) takes a source-language query and its English translation; (b) generates sub-question graphs in parallel for both language versions; (c) merges these graphs; and (d) performs a bilingual retrieval-and-answer process that sequentially solves the sub-questions using evidence from both languages.
AI.result: Experiments show that state-of-the-art RAG systems exhibit large performance disparities across languages in multilingual multi-hop QA. DaPT consistently outperforms baseline RAG approaches in terms of accuracy and answer conciseness. On the challenging MuSiQue benchmark, DaPT achieves an 18.3% relative gain in average Exact Match (EM) over the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: Multilingual multi-hop QA is challenging for existing RAG systems, which are overly optimized for English. By providing multilingual benchmarks and introducing DaPT, the paper demonstrates that exploiting parallel sub-question reasoning and bilingual retrieval significantly improves multilingual RAG performance, yielding more accurate and concise answers across languages.

title: RewardFlow: Topology-Aware Reward Propagation on State Graphs for Agentic RL with Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) holds significant promise for enhancing the agentic reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with external environments. However, the inherent sparsity of terminal rewards hinders fine-grained, state-level optimization. Although process reward modeling offers a promising alternative, training dedicated reward models often entails substantial computational costs and scaling difficulties. To address these challenges, we introduce RewardFlow, a lightweight method for estimating state-level rewards tailored to agentic reasoning tasks. RewardFlow leverages the intrinsic topological structure of states within reasoning trajectories by constructing state graphs. This enables an analysis of state-wise contributions to success, followed by topology-aware graph propagation to quantify contributions and yield objective, state-level rewards. When integrated as dense rewards for RL optimization, RewardFlow substantially outperforms prior RL baselines across four agentic reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance, robustness, and training efficiency. The implementation of RewardFlow is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/RewardFlow.

AI.tldr: RewardFlow is a lightweight method that derives dense, state-level rewards from the topology of reasoning trajectories, enabling more effective RL training for agentic LLMs without expensive reward models.
AI.motivation: In RL for agentic LLM reasoning, relying only on sparse terminal rewards makes it hard to optimize intermediate reasoning steps. Process reward models can give denser feedback but require separate models that are costly to train and scale. The authors seek a scalable way to obtain fine-grained, objective rewards at the state level for reasoning trajectories, without training heavy reward models.
AI.method: The paper proposes RewardFlow, which constructs a graph over intermediate reasoning states visited by an LLM agent in its trajectories. Using the topological structure of this state graph, it analyzes how much each state contributes to downstream success. Then it performs a topology-aware propagation over the graph to assign contribution scores to each state. These scores serve as state-level rewards that are used as dense signals in RL optimization of the agentic LLM.
AI.result: Across four benchmarks for agentic reasoning, using RewardFlow-derived dense rewards in RL leads to substantially better performance than prior RL baselines that rely on standard sparse rewards or other approaches. The method also shows improved robustness and greater training efficiency compared with these baselines.
AI.conclusion: RewardFlow demonstrates that exploiting the topology of reasoning trajectories to infer state-level contributions is an effective and lightweight alternative to training process reward models. It provides dense, objective rewards that significantly enhance RL-based training of agentic LLMs, yielding higher performance and more efficient learning on reasoning tasks.

title: UGID: Unified Graph Isomorphism for Debiasing Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit pronounced social biases. Output-level or data-optimization--based debiasing methods cannot fully resolve these biases, and many prior works have shown that biases are embedded in internal representations. We propose \underline{U}nified \underline{G}raph \underline{I}somorphism for \underline{D}ebiasing large language models (\textit{\textbf{UGID}}), an internal-representation--level debiasing framework for large language models that models the Transformer as a structured computational graph, where attention mechanisms define the routing edges of the graph and hidden states define the graph nodes. Specifically, debiasing is formulated as enforcing invariance of the graph structure across counterfactual inputs, with differences allowed only on sensitive attributes. \textit{\textbf{UGID}} jointly constrains attention routing and hidden representations in bias-sensitive regions, effectively preventing bias migration across architectural components. To achieve effective behavioral alignment without degrading general capabilities, we introduce a log-space constraint on sensitive logits and a selective anchor-based objective to preserve definitional semantics. Extensive experiments on large language models demonstrate that \textit{\textbf{UGID}} effectively reduces bias under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, significantly reduces internal structural discrepancies, and preserves model safety and utility.

AI.tldr: Proposes UGID, a representation-level debiasing framework that treats a Transformer as a computational graph and enforces structural invariance across counterfactual inputs to reduce social bias in LLMs while preserving utility.
AI.motivation: LLMs show strong social biases that persist even after output-level or data-level debiasing, because these biases are embedded in internal representations and can migrate across components. Existing methods cannot fully align internal model behavior with desired unbiased outputs without harming general performance, motivating a principled internal-representation–level debiasing approach.
AI.method: Model the Transformer as a structured computational graph where attention patterns are edges and hidden states are nodes. Define counterfactual inputs that differ only in sensitive attributes (e.g., demographic terms). Formulate debiasing as enforcing graph isomorphism (structural invariance) across these counterfactual inputs, allowing changes only in the explicit sensitive attributes. Jointly constrain attention routing and hidden states in bias-sensitive regions to prevent bias migration. Additionally, impose a log-space constraint on sensitive logits to control output behavior and use a selective anchor-based objective to preserve definitional semantics of sensitive terms and maintain general capabilities.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple LLMs and benchmarks show that UGID reduces measured social bias both on in-distribution and out-of-distribution tests, decreases internal structural discrepancies between counterfactual inputs, and maintains or minimally affects utility and safety performance compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Internal, graph-structured debiasing via UGID can effectively reduce social biases in LLMs by aligning internal representations across counterfactual inputs, while preserving definitional semantics and overall capability. This suggests that representation-level, structure-aware constraints are a promising direction for robust and scalable debiasing of large language models.

title: Conflict-Based Search for Multi Agent Path Finding with Asynchronous Actions
summary: Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) seeks collision-free paths for multiple agents from their respective start locations to their respective goal locations while minimizing path costs. Most existing MAPF algorithms rely on a common assumption of synchronized actions, where the actions of all agents start at the same time and always take a time unit, which may limit the use of MAPF planners in practice. To get rid of this assumption, Continuous-time Conflict-Based Search (CCBS) is a popular approach that can find optimal solutions for MAPF with asynchronous actions (MAPF-AA). However, CCBS has recently been identified to be incomplete due to an uncountably infinite state space created by continuous wait durations. This paper proposes a new method, Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA), which bypasses this theoretical issue and can solve MAPF-AA with completeness and solution optimality guarantees. Based on CBS-AA, we also develop conflict resolution techniques to improve the scalability of CBS-AA further. Our test results show that our method can reduce the number of branches by up to 90%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CBS-AA, a complete and optimal algorithm for multi-agent path finding with asynchronous actions, overcoming the incompleteness of prior continuous-time methods and greatly reducing search branching.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent path finding (MAPF) methods usually assume synchronized, unit-time actions for all agents. This is unrealistic for many real-world systems where agents move asynchronously and actions have variable durations. Continuous-time CBS (CCBS) tried to address this but was shown incomplete due to an uncountably infinite continuous-time state space created by arbitrary wait durations. There is thus a need for a principled algorithm that supports asynchronous actions while still guaranteeing completeness and optimality.
AI.method: The authors propose Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA), a variant of CBS tailored to MAPF with asynchronous actions (MAPF-AA). Instead of representing time as a fully continuous domain with arbitrary wait times (as in CCBS), CBS-AA designs a state representation and constraint model that avoid the uncountably infinite branching. It retains the high-level CBS idea of resolving conflicts by branching on constraints but adapts conflict detection, timing, and constraint generation to the asynchronous setting. They further introduce conflict resolution techniques—likely improved constraint generation, pruning rules, and branching heuristics—to reduce the number of search branches and improve scalability.
AI.result: Experiments on MAPF-AA benchmarks show that CBS-AA is both complete and optimal and that the added conflict resolution techniques significantly reduce search complexity. In particular, they report up to a 90% reduction in the number of branches explored compared to a baseline, implying substantial performance and scalability gains.
AI.conclusion: CBS-AA successfully removes the unrealistic synchronized-action assumption for MAPF while preserving completeness and optimality guarantees, overcoming the theoretical incompleteness of prior continuous-time CBS approaches. The proposed conflict resolution enhancements make the method substantially more scalable in practice, making optimal MAPF with asynchronous actions more tractable for real-world applications.

title: Optimal Splitting of Language Models from Mixtures to Specialized Domains
summary: Language models achieve impressive performance on a variety of knowledge, language, and reasoning tasks due to the scale and diversity of pretraining data available. The standard training recipe is a two-stage paradigm: pretraining first on the full corpus of data followed by specialization on a subset of high quality, specialized data from the full corpus. In the multi-domain setting, this involves continued pretraining of multiple models on each specialized domain, referred to as split model training. We propose a method for pretraining multiple models independently over a general pretraining corpus, and determining the optimal compute allocation between pretraining and continued pretraining using scaling laws. Our approach accurately predicts the loss of a model of size N with D pretraining and D' specialization tokens, and extrapolates to larger model sizes and number of tokens. Applied to language model training, our approach improves performance consistently across common sense knowledge and reasoning benchmarks across different model sizes and compute budgets.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes a scaling-law-based method to optimally allocate compute between general pretraining and domain specialization for multiple language models, improving downstream knowledge and reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Standard two-stage training of language models—large-scale general pretraining followed by domain specialization—requires training separate models per domain and uses compute suboptimally. In multi-domain scenarios, this split model training is expensive, and there is no principled way to decide how much compute to allocate to general pretraining versus continued domain-specific pretraining. The authors want a predictive, quantitative framework to make these trade-offs and avoid wasteful retraining while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: They pretrain multiple models independently on a general corpus and then continue pretraining (specialize) them on domain-specific subsets. They fit and use scaling laws to jointly model the effects of model size N, number of general pretraining tokens D, and number of specialization tokens D' on loss. With this empirical scaling model, they search over possible allocations of compute between the general and specialization stages to predict which allocation minimizes loss for a given computation budget, and they extrapolate these predictions to larger models and token counts.
AI.result: The learned scaling relationships accurately predict model loss as a function of N, D, and D' and generalize to unseen, larger model sizes and token budgets. Using these predictions to guide compute allocation yields models that are consistently better on common sense knowledge and reasoning benchmarks compared to baselines that use standard split model training or naïve allocation strategies, across a range of model sizes and total compute budgets.
AI.conclusion: A scaling-law-driven framework can effectively guide how to allocate compute between general pretraining and domain specialization when training multiple language models across domains. By modeling loss as a function of model size and both general and specialized tokens, practitioners can plan training runs that achieve better downstream performance under fixed compute constraints, making multi-domain language model training more efficient and scalable.

title: Bridging Network Fragmentation: A Semantic-Augmented DRL Framework for UAV-aided VANETs
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
summary: Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs) are the digital cornerstone of autonomous driving, yet they suffer from severe network fragmentation in urban environments due to physical obstructions. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), with their high mobility, have emerged as a vital solution to bridge these connectivity gaps. However, traditional Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based UAV deployment strategies lack semantic understanding of road topology, often resulting in blind exploration and sample inefficiency. By contrast, Large Language Models (LLMs) possess powerful reasoning capabilities capable of identifying topological importance, though applying them to control tasks remains challenging. To address this, we propose the Semantic-Augmented DRL (SA-DRL) framework. Firstly, we propose a fragmentation quantification method based on Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG). Subsequently, we design a four-stage pipeline to transform a general-purpose LLM into a domain-specific topology expert. Finally, we propose the Semantic-Augmented PPO (SA-PPO) algorithm, which employs a Logit Fusion mechanism to inject the LLM's semantic reasoning directly into the policy as a prior, effectively guiding the agent toward critical intersections. Extensive high-fidelity simulations demonstrate that SA-PPO achieves state-of-the-art performance with remarkable efficiency, reaching baseline performance levels using only 26.6% of the training episodes. Ultimately, SA-PPO improves two key connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5% over competing methods, while reducing energy consumption to just 28.2% of the baseline.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Semantic-Augmented DRL (SA-DRL) framework that uses an LLM as a topology expert to guide UAV placement in VANETs, improving connectivity and energy efficiency with far fewer training episodes.
AI.motivation: VANETs in urban areas suffer from network fragmentation due to buildings and other obstacles, degrading connectivity essential for autonomous driving. UAVs can act as relays, but existing DRL-based placement policies are sample-inefficient because they do not understand road topology and explore blindly. LLMs can reason about complex structures like road networks, but it is non-trivial to inject their semantic knowledge into control policies. There is a need for a method that fuses LLM-based topological reasoning with DRL to more efficiently deploy UAVs for connectivity repair.
AI.method: First, the paper introduces a fragmentation quantification method based on Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG) to formally capture connectivity issues. Then, it presents a four-stage pipeline that converts a generic LLM into a domain-specific topology expert capable of assessing topological importance in a road network. Finally, it proposes Semantic-Augmented PPO (SA-PPO), which integrates the LLM’s semantic guidance into a DRL policy via a Logit Fusion mechanism, injecting the LLM’s output as a prior over actions to steer the agent toward topologically critical intersections when deploying UAVs.
AI.result: In high-fidelity simulations, SA-PPO achieves the same performance as baseline methods using only 26.6% of their training episodes, demonstrating significantly improved sample efficiency. Compared with state-of-the-art competing methods, SA-PPO improves two main connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5%, respectively, while cutting energy consumption down to 28.2% of the baseline, indicating both better connectivity and much lower operational cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SA-DRL framework, instantiated as SA-PPO with Logit Fusion, effectively integrates LLM-based semantic reasoning about road topology into DRL-based UAV deployment. This fusion yields more sample-efficient learning, better VANET connectivity, and substantially reduced energy consumption, showing that LLM-guided semantic priors can meaningfully enhance control policies in complex networked systems like urban vehicular networks.

title: VEPO: Variable Entropy Policy Optimization for Low-Resource Language Foundation Models
comment: 23 pages. Includes figures and tables. Conference submission
summary: Large language models frequently exhibit suboptimal performance on low resource languages, primarily due to inefficient subword segmentation and systemic training data imbalances. In this paper, we propose Variable Entropy Policy Optimization (VEPO), which leverages Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards to incorporate deterministic structural constraints into the policy alignment process. This framework ensures prescribed sequence length, robust format consistency, and rigorous linguistic well formedness, all enforced during training. Central to our approach is a variable entropy mechanism that enables the model to dynamically calibrate the equilibrium between literal fidelity and semantic naturalness by modulating the exploration exploitation manifold. By integrating entropy tempered advantage estimation with asymmetric clipping, VEPO sustains robust exploration while mitigating policy collapse. Empirical evaluations across 90 FLORES-200, COMET-22, chrF directions demonstrate that VEPO yields substantial improvements in both tokenization efficiency and translation quality, bridging the performance gap for underrepresented languages.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VEPO, a reinforcement learning-based method to improve low-resource language performance in large language models by enforcing structural constraints and dynamic entropy control during policy optimization.
AI.motivation: Large language models perform poorly on low-resource languages due to inefficient subword segmentation and imbalanced training data. There is a need for a method that can explicitly control structure, length, and linguistic well-formedness during training to improve tokenization efficiency and translation quality for these languages.
AI.method: The authors propose Variable Entropy Policy Optimization (VEPO), a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards framework. VEPO integrates deterministic structural constraints into the policy alignment process, enforcing sequence length, format consistency, and linguistic well-formedness. It uses a variable entropy mechanism to balance literal fidelity and semantic naturalness through exploration-exploitation control and combines entropy-tempered advantage estimation with asymmetric clipping to maintain exploration and prevent policy collapse.
AI.result: Experiments run on 90 translation directions using FLORES-200, COMET-22, and chrF evaluation show that VEPO significantly improves tokenization efficiency and translation quality, especially for underrepresented languages.
AI.conclusion: VEPO effectively bridges the performance gap for low-resource languages by aligning large language model policies under structural constraints and dynamic entropy control, yielding more efficient tokenization and better translations.

title: Geography According to ChatGPT -- How Generative AI Represents and Reasons about Geography
summary: Understanding how AI will represent and reason about geography should be a key concern for all of us, as the broader public increasingly interacts with spaces and places through these systems. Similarly, in line with the nature of foundation models, our own research often relies on pre-trained models. Hence, understanding what world AI systems construct is as important as evaluating their accuracy, including factual recall.
