AI.result: Across five benchmark suites and three GPU types (T4, V100, A800), cuGenOpt significantly outperforms general-purpose MIP solvers in speed, matches or approaches specialized solvers in solution quality up to instance size n=150, and achieves a 4.73% optimality gap on the TSP instance with 442 nodes in 30 seconds. It solves twelve problem types (with five encoding variants) to optimality. Framework-level optimizations are shown to reduce the gap on pcb442 from 36% to 4.73% and to increase throughput on VRPTW by 75–81%.
AI.conclusion: cuGenOpt provides a high-performance, GPU-based metaheuristic framework that is both general and user-friendly, narrowing the traditional trade-offs among generality, performance, and usability. Its architecture, adaptive operators, and extensible interfaces allow it to tackle diverse combinatorial optimization problems efficiently, while Python and LLM-based tooling lower the barrier for domain experts to model and solve new problems.

title: OS-Themis: A Scalable Critic Framework for Generalist GUI Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has the potential to improve the robustness of GUI agents in stochastic environments, yet training is highly sensitive to the quality of the reward function. Existing reward approaches struggle to achieve both scalability and performance. To address this, we propose OS-Themis, a scalable and accurate multi-agent critic framework. Unlike a single judge, OS-Themis decomposes trajectories into verifiable milestones to isolate critical evidence for decision making and employs a review mechanism to strictly audit the evidence chain before making the final verdict. To facilitate evaluation, we further introduce OmniGUIRewardBench (OGRBench), a holistic cross-platform benchmark for GUI outcome rewards, where all evaluated models achieve their best performance under OS-Themis. Extensive experiments on AndroidWorld show that OS-Themis yields a 10.3% improvement when used to support online RL training, and a 6.9% gain when used for trajectory validation and filtering in the self-training loop, highlighting its potential to drive agent evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper presents OS-Themis, a scalable multi-agent critic framework that provides more accurate reward signals for training GUI agents with reinforcement learning, along with a new benchmark, OmniGUIRewardBench, to evaluate GUI outcome rewards.
AI.motivation: Training GUI agents with reinforcement learning in stochastic, real-world environments is challenging because performance depends heavily on the quality of the reward function. Existing reward mechanisms either do not scale well or provide weak, noisy signals, limiting the robustness and reliability of GUI agents across platforms.
AI.method: The authors design OS-Themis, a multi-agent critic framework that replaces a single judge with a set of coordinated evaluators. OS-Themis decomposes an agent’s trajectory into intermediate, verifiable milestones and isolates the critical evidence relevant to each. It then uses a review/audit mechanism over this evidence chain before producing a final reward verdict. They also construct OmniGUIRewardBench (OGRBench), a cross-platform benchmark to systematically evaluate GUI outcome reward models under different judging strategies.
AI.result: On the AndroidWorld environment, OS-Themis improves online RL training performance of GUI agents by 10.3% compared with prior reward setups, and yields a 6.9% gain when used to validate and filter trajectories in a self-training loop. Across models evaluated on OGRBench, OS-Themis consistently leads to the best observed performance.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing trajectories into milestone-level evidence and evaluating them via a multi-agent critic yields more accurate and scalable reward signals for GUI RL tasks. OS-Themis not only enhances training and self-training performance but, together with OGRBench, provides a strong foundation for future work on robust, reward-driven evolution of GUI agents across platforms.

title: Using Optimal Transport as Alignment Objective for fine-tuning Multilingual Contextualized Embeddings
summary: Recent studies have proposed different methods to improve multilingual word representations in contextualized settings including techniques that align between source and target embedding spaces. For contextualized embeddings, alignment becomes more complex as we additionally take context into consideration. In this work, we propose using Optimal Transport (OT) as an alignment objective during fine-tuning to further improve multilingual contextualized representations for downstream cross-lingual transfer. This approach does not require word-alignment pairs prior to fine-tuning that may lead to sub-optimal matching and instead learns the word alignments within context in an unsupervised manner. It also allows different types of mappings due to soft matching between source and target sentences. We benchmark our proposed method on two tasks (XNLI and XQuAD) and achieve improvements over baselines as well as competitive results compared to similar recent works.

AI.tldr: The paper improves multilingual contextual word representations by using Optimal Transport as an unsupervised alignment objective during fine-tuning, leading to better cross-lingual transfer on XNLI and XQuAD.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for multilingual contextual embeddings often depend on pre-computed word alignments or rigid alignment strategies, which become problematic and sub-optimal once context is introduced. There is a need for a more flexible, context-aware and unsupervised way to align source and target language representations that can directly enhance performance on cross-lingual downstream tasks.
AI.method: During fine-tuning of a multilingual contextual model, the authors introduce an Optimal Transport (OT) based alignment loss between representations of parallel source–target sentences. OT computes a soft matching (transport plan) between contextualized word embeddings in the two sentences, without relying on pre-given word alignment pairs. This OT loss is combined with the standard task loss, encouraging the model to learn better aligned multilingual embeddings in context while remaining fully unsupervised regarding word-level alignments.
AI.result: On two standard cross-lingual benchmarks, XNLI (for natural language inference) and XQuAD (for question answering), the proposed OT-based fine-tuning yields consistent improvements over baseline multilingual models that do not use OT alignment, and performs competitively with other recent approaches that attempt to improve cross-lingual alignment in contextual representations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Optimal Transport as an alignment objective during fine-tuning is an effective unsupervised way to improve multilingual contextualized word representations for cross-lingual transfer. By learning soft, context-dependent word alignments without external alignment supervision, the method enhances performance on downstream tasks and offers a flexible alternative to traditional alignment techniques.
